Lancelot became a hermit and died not long after. They were buried far apart, but in the stories of Britain, their names are forever linked. Guinevere was not a villain. She was a woman caught between duty and love, and her choices, however flawed, were the choices of a heart that could not deny itself. She was, as Malory wrote, a true lover, and therefore she had a good end.
moral: Passion and duty often conflict.

title: Robin Hood
summary: In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood.
full_text:
In the days when King Richard the Lionheart was away fighting in the Crusades and his brother Prince John ruled England with greed and cruelty, a young man from Locksley in Nottinghamshire was forced into the greenwood. His name was Robin Hood, and he was the finest archer England had ever seen.

The story of his outlawry varies. Some say he killed one of the king's deer to feed his starving family and was declared an outlaw. Others say he slew a band of the king's foresters who mocked his archery and then tried to arrest him for poaching. In the oldest ballad, Robin Hood's Progress to Nottingham, he was but fifteen years old when a wager with fifteen foresters turned to blood, and he fled to the forest with only his bow and his wits.

In Sherwood Forest, Robin found others like himself: men who had been wronged by the unjust laws of Prince John and the corrupt Sheriff of Nottingham. There was Little John, the giant who became Robin's second-in-command after a legendary quarterstaff duel on a narrow bridge. There was Much the Miller's Son, Will Scarlet the fiery one, and Friar Tuck, the jolly monk who could fight as well as he could pray. They lived in the greenwood, sleeping beneath the Major Oak, feasting on venison stolen from the king's deer, and wearing clothes of Lincoln green that blended with the leaves.

Robin's code was simple: he robbed from the rich, especially from corrupt churchmen and nobles who had grown fat on the suffering of the poor, and he gave to those in need. Widows were protected. Poor farmers were fed. Young men unjustly sentenced were rescued from the gallows. He was a thorn in the side of the Sheriff of Nottingham, who tried time and again to capture him. The sheriff once disguised himself and joined the outlaws, but Robin saw through the ruse and escaped. He sent the sheriff home naked on a horse.

Robin's fame spread across the land. He could split a willow wand at a hundred paces. He could shoot an arrow so swiftly that it would pin a man's sleeve to a tree before the man could flinch. He was merciful to those who showed mercy and merciless to those who did not. "I love not the rich who grind the faces of the poor," he would say. "But I love the poor man who works and is honest."

When King Richard returned from the Crusades, he heard of the bold outlaw in Sherwood Forest. Disguised as a monk, the king sought out Robin and shared his meal. Impressed by Robin's honor and generosity, Richard pardoned him and his men, restoring their lands and rights. But in some versions, Robin's end was more tragic. Feeling himself growing weak, he went to Kirklees Priory to be bled by his cousin, the prioress, who betrayed him and let him bleed to death. In his final moments, he raised his horn and blew a last blast through the greenwood. Little John came running, but it was too late. Robin Hood, the prince of outlaws, had shot his last arrow.

His grave lies in Kirklees, beneath a mossy stone. But his spirit lives wherever the poor are oppressed and the rich grow too powerful. He is the eternal rebel, the archer who never misses, the man who stole from the rich and gave to the poor.
moral: Justice may require breaking unjust laws.

title: Maid Marian
summary: Maid Marian was the heart of Sherwood Forest.. While Robin Hood was its soul and Little John its strength, Marian was the pulse that kept the Merry Men alive.
full_text:
Maid Marian was the heart of Sherwood Forest. While Robin Hood was its soul and Little John its strength, Marian was the pulse that kept the Merry Men alive. She was a noblewoman by birth but an outlaw by choice, and she was the only woman who could match Robin Hood with both a bow and a sharp word.

Before she came to Sherwood, Marian was Lady Marian Fitzwalter, the daughter of the Earl of Huntingdon. She was betrothed to Robin of Locksley before he became an outlaw. When Robin was declared an outlaw for poaching the king's deer, their engagement was broken by Marian's father, who feared the king's wrath.

But Marian had no intention of abandoning Robin. She slipped away from her father's castle dressed as a page boy and made her way to Sherwood. When she found Robin's camp, she challenged him to an archery contest. Robin, not recognizing her, accepted. She outshot him three arrows to two before revealing herself. Robin laughed with joy and welcomed her to the band.

In Sherwood, Marian was more than Robin's love. She was the band's strategist and diplomat. She negotiated with the local peasants for supplies, gathered intelligence from the village markets, and kept the Merry Men informed of the Sheriff's movements. When Robin was captured and imprisoned in Nottingham Castle, it was Marian who disguised herself as a washerwoman and smuggled a file and rope to him.

When King Richard returned from the Crusades and pardoned Robin, Marian was at his side at the royal court. But the court was too small for her wild heart. She and Robin returned to Sherwood, where they lived out their days in the greenwood, free and untamed.

Maid Marian is remembered as the first feminist of English folklore — a woman who chose love over comfort, freedom over safety, and the forest over the castle. She proved that a woman could be both a lady and a warrior, both beautiful and deadly.
moral: True love cannot be caged.
source: English Folklore (Arthur of the Swan tradition)

title: Little John
summary: When Robin Hood was about twenty years old and still gathering the men who would become his Merry Men, he grew restless after fourteen days without sport.
full_text:
When Robin Hood was about twenty years old and still gathering the men who would become his Merry Men, he grew restless after fourteen days without sport. He left his companions in the forest and set out alone, striding through the trees until he came to a narrow bridge that crossed a swift-flowing brook. As he stepped onto the bridge, a stranger appeared on the other end.

The stranger was a giant. He stood at least seven feet tall, with limbs like oak branches and shoulders that filled the path. His name was John Little, and he was a man of good mettle, a Yorkshireman through and through, with a strong jaw and eyes that did not flinch. He had no intention of giving way.

"Stand back," Robin called, stringing his bow. "I am Robin Hood, and I will not move for any man."

The stranger laughed, a deep sound like rolling thunder. "And I am John Little, and I yield to no one. It is a coward's trick to stand there with a longbow and threaten me when I have nothing but a staff."

Robin, who prided himself on his courage above all else, tossed his bow aside. "A fair point," he said. He walked to a thicket of trees, cut himself a stout staff of ground-oak, and returned to the bridge, hefting the wood in his hands. "Now we are equal. Whoever falls into the brook loses the fight."

They began a battle that would become legend. Their staffs whistled through the air and cracked against each other like thunder splitting a tree. Robin struck first, a blow that made the giant's bones ring. John answered in kind, and the impact shuddered up Robin's arms. They fought as fiercely as men threshing wheat, neither willing to yield, their feet planted on the narrow bridge. But the stranger was stronger and more patient. He caught Robin on the crown with a crack that drew blood, and then, with a final mighty blow, he sent the outlaw tumbling headfirst into the brook with a great splash.

Robin surfaced, sputtering and gasping, and found the stranger laughing on the bridge above him. "Where are you now, bold Robin?"

Instead of anger, Robin laughed too. He waded to the bank, dripping wet, and blew three notes on his horn. Fifty bowmen burst from the trees, bows drawn. "What is your will?" asked Will Stutely. The stranger braced himself. But Robin held up his hand. "This man has beaten me fairly in combat. He is the bravest soul I have met. John Little, will you join my band and learn to shoot a longbow?"

John Little agreed. Will Stutely, always one for ceremony, announced that a new infant needed christening. They fetched two fat does for the feast and gathered around. "This infant was called John Little," Stutely declared. "But his name shall be changed. We will transpose the words, so wherever he goes, his name shall be Little John." They roared with laughter at the joke, for he was anything but little.

And so the giant was called Little John. They dressed him in Lincoln green and gave him a longbow of yew. From that day forward, he was Robin's right hand, loyal unto death. In the ballads he is the one who rescues Robin from Nottingham prison, who disguises himself as Reynold Greenleaf to trick the sheriff, and who stands by his leader until the very end at Kirklees, where he helped carry Robin to the window so the outlaw could shoot one last arrow to mark his grave. In every story, Little John stands tall, not just in stature but in loyalty, the quiet giant beside the legendary archer.
moral: Strength serves wisdom; loyalty outlasts all.

title: Friar Tuck
summary: Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard.
full_text:
Of all Robin Hood's Merry Men, none was more surprising than the holy man who could fight like a soldier and laugh like a drunkard. His name was Friar Tuck, and he was a curtal friar, a wandering monk who had left the walls of Fountains Abbey when the life of cloisters proved too narrow for his immense spirit.

Tuck was a jovial mountain of a man, barrel-chested and broad-bellied, with a round face half-hidden by a bristling brown beard and a tonsured crown shining atop his head. His eyes were quick and merry, always one glance away from a wink. He wore a simple brown habit cinched at the waist with a rope, but beneath it he carried a broadsword and a buckler, and he knew how to use both. He was the only man in all of Sherwood Forest who could match Robin's wit as easily as his steel.

Their first meeting was a battle that became legend. Robin had heard rumors of a friar living near Fountains Abbey who could outfight any man in Yorkshire. Swearing an oath never to eat or drink until he found this holy terror, Robin set out with his bow and his pride. He found Tuck walking by a wild river, a stout cudgel in one hand and a sword at his hip, the very picture of a warrior monk. Robin, full of mischief, asked the friar to carry him across the water. Tuck, who knew mischief when he saw it, hoisted Robin onto his back without a word and waded into the swift current. But when they reached the far bank, Tuck drew his sword and demanded that Robin carry him back. The back-and-forth continued until, in the middle of the stream, Tuck flung Robin headlong into the water.

They fought with swords for six hours, neither gaining the advantage, their blades ringing through the forest. Robin blew his horn, and fifty bowmen appeared from the trees. Tuck put his fingers to his lips and whistled, and fifty ferocious bandogs bounded out of the forest, teeth bared. The dogs were trained to attack men, and they would have torn Robin's band apart. "Hold!" called Robin, laughing despite himself. "Stay your dogs, good friar, and let us be friends."

Tuck agreed, and from that day forward he was the Merry Men's chaplain. He heard their confessions, blessed their meals, and fought beside them in every skirmish. He was a man of profound contradictions: a holy man who drank ale from dawn till dusk, a peacemaker who wielded a blade as readily as a cross, a jester who could soothe the most troubled heart with a well-timed jest. He could say mass in the morning and crack a skull in the afternoon, and see no conflict between the two.

He was the one who married Robin and Maid Marian in the greenwood, beneath a canopy of oak leaves, with birds as witnesses and the wind as music. He baptized the children born in the forest and performed last rites for those who fell to the sheriff's men. He was, in many ways, the soul of Sherwood, proof that holiness and joy could be the same thing, that a man of God could also be a man of the people.

In the final days, when the Merry Men scattered, Tuck stayed on in the forest, living as a hermit. Some say he returned to his abbey and lived out his days in peace, a repentant sinner telling no one of his past. Others say he simply walked into the trees one morning and was never seen again. But wherever the story of Robin Hood is told, Friar Tuck is there, juggling, laughing, and showing the world that faith and joy belong together.
moral: Faith finds many paths.

title: Will Scarlet
summary: Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession.
full_text:
Will Scarlet was the most hot-tempered of Robin Hood's Merry Men, a man who wore his anger like a cloak and held a grudge like a treasured possession. He was tall and lean, with sharp features and a shock of red hair that gave him his name, and an equally red temper to match.

Before he joined the Merry Men, Will had been a huntsman in the service of a wealthy nobleman. One day, he caught the nobleman's steward beating an old poacher for stealing a single rabbit to feed his starving children. Will intervened, knocking the steward unconscious. For this act of mercy, Will was branded an outlaw and a price was placed on his head.

Will fled to Sherwood Forest, where Robin Hood found him, hungry and defiant, arguing with a group of Robin's men over a deer. Robin was amused by the young man's spirit and invited him to join the band. Will accepted, but only after challenging Robin to a quarterstaff duel, which Will lost — barely.

In Sherwood, Will was both a blessing and a challenge. He was fiercely loyal to the cause of the poor but possessed a sharp tongue that often got him into trouble. He argued with Little John constantly, each man's pride rubbing against the other's like flint on steel. But when a fight broke out, no one fought harder or more fiercely than Will Scarlet.

His finest moment came when the Sheriff of Nottingham ambushed Robin and a small group of the Merry Men. Outnumbered five to one, Will refused to retreat. He stood shoulder to shoulder with Robin, fighting with a fury that made the sheriff's men hesitate. By the time reinforcements arrived, Will had single-handedly held the line.

Later in life, after the Merry Men scattered, Will Scarlet took service with King Richard the Lionheart and fought in the Crusades. He never returned to Sherwood. But his legend lived on — the red-haired hero whose temper was matched only by his courage.
moral: Mercy can heal the angriest heart.

title: The Frog Prince
summary: In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden.
full_text:
In the days when wishes still worked and magic was as common as morning dew, there lived a king whose youngest daughter was so beautiful that the sun itself stopped to stare when she stepped into the garden. She was kind-hearted but willful, used to getting whatever she desired.

Near the king's castle lay a dark, ancient forest, and in that forest was a well so deep that no one had ever seen the bottom. On hot summer days, the princess would sit by this well and play with her favorite toy: a golden ball that sparkled like captured sunlight.

One afternoon, she tossed the ball higher than usual, and it slipped through her fingers. It bounced once, twice, and rolled into the well. The princess peered into the darkness and heard a faint splash as the ball disappeared into the black water. She burst into tears.

A voice rose from the water. "Why do you weep, princess?" It was a frog. His skin was green and bumpy, his eyes bulged, and his voice was a croak. The princess was startled. "My golden ball has fallen into the well," she said.

"I will retrieve it for you," said the frog, "if you promise to let me be your companion. Let me eat from your plate and sleep on your pillow." The princess agreed without thinking, desperate for her ball. The frog dove, retrieved the golden ball, and tossed it onto the grass. The princess grabbed it and ran home without looking back.

That night, a knocking came at the castle door. The king opened it to find the frog on the step. "Your daughter made a promise," the frog said. The king listened to the story and told his daughter, "A promise is a promise." Reluctantly, she let the frog eat from her plate.

But when the frog demanded to sleep on her pillow, the princess lost her temper. She grabbed the frog and threw him against the wall. Instead of a dying croak, there was a flash of light. The frog transformed into a handsome prince. He had been cursed by a wicked witch, and only a princess who kept her promise could break the spell.

They were married the next day. The princess learned that true beauty is not about appearance but about honor. The frog taught her that every creature deserves dignity, and a promise, once given, must never be broken.
moral: Do not judge by appearances.

title: Rapunzel
summary: Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden.. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there.
full_text:
Once upon a time, a married couple lived next to an old witch's garden. The wife became pregnant and craved the rapunzel plants growing there. Her husband, unable to resist her pleas, stole some plants from the garden at night.

When the witch discovered the theft, she came to their home in great anger. You have stolen my plants,' she cried, and now you must give me your child!' The couple had no choice but to surrender their newborn daughter.

The witch took the baby and named her Rapunzel after the plants she had craved. Rapunzel grew into the most beautiful girl with hair as golden as the sun. The witch kept her locked in a tall tower hidden deep in the forest, with no door and no stairs—only a window at the very top.

When Rapunzel was twelve years old, a young prince was hunting in the forest and heard beautiful singing. He followed the voice and discovered the tower. He called up to her, and Rapunzel let down her long golden hair so he could climb up. They met every day, and soon they fell deeply in love.

One day, the witch discovered their secret meetings. In her rage, she cut off all of Rapunzels beautiful hair and banished her to the desert wasteland. Then she waited for the prince with the stolen hair.

When the prince came to visit, he called up to the window and the witch let down the hair. The prince climbed up and found only the wicked witch, who laughed at him. 'You will never see your beloved Rapunzel again! she screeched.

The prince was so heartbroken that he jumped from the tower. He did not die, but thorns on the ground poked his eyes and blinded him. Wandering in despair, he wandered for years through the wilderness, eating berries and roots to survive.

One day, by some miracle, he heard a familiar voice—a woman's voice. It was Rapunzel calling out to her twins, a boy and a girl she had given birth to in the desert. The prince ran toward the voice, and when he reached her, he wept with joy. His tears fell upon her face and into his eyes, and suddenly he could see again—he had not truly been blind.

The prince took Rapunzel and the twins to his kingdom, where they lived happily ever after. The couple who had raised him took care of the children as if they were their own grandchildren.

And so the power of true love overcame all obstacles, as it always does.
moral: Love finds its way.

title: Beauty and the Beast
summary: A beautiful young woman agrees to live with a beast to save her father. Through her kindness and growing love, the beast transforms into a handsome prince.
full_text:
In a grand city by the sea, a wealthy merchant lost his fortune when storms sank his ships and creditors seized his estate. Forced to move his six children to a small cottage in the country, he watched his three daughters adapt in very different ways. The eldest two complained bitterly about peeling potatoes and milking goats, but the youngest, whom everyone called Belle, embraced their new life with grace. She rose before dawn to sweep the floors, tended the kitchen garden, and read by candlelight when her chores were done.
One day, word came that one of the merchant's ships had been found. He prepared to journey to the port, hoping to salvage something of his former wealth. Before leaving, he asked each daughter what gift she wished. The eldest demanded pearls and diamonds. The second wanted silk gowns. But Belle, seeing that winter had stripped the garden bare, asked only for a single rose.
The merchant reached the port to find his cargo had been seized for debts. Defeated, he began the long ride home. A blizzard drove him deep into a dark forest where he lost all sense of direction. Half-frozen, he stumbled upon a magnificent castle. Its gates stood open, and though no servant tended the halls, a fire blazed in the hearth and a feast awaited on a long oak table. He ate, warmed himself, and slept.
In the morning, he saw a garden of roses heavy with bloom despite the frost. Remembering Belle's wish, he plucked one. Instantly, a roar like thunder shook the castle walls. A monstrous Beast stood before him — covered in coarse fur, with tusks like a boar and eyes like burning embers. "Ungrateful wretch!" the Beast bellowed. "I saved your life, and you steal from me! For this, you must die."
The merchant fell to his knees and told the Beast about Belle. The Beast listened, then said, "You shall not die. But you must send me your youngest daughter in your place, willingly. You have three months." He sent the merchant home with gold and the rose.
When Belle heard the tale, she did not hesitate. "Father, I will go. You shall not die for a rose I asked for." She rode to the castle, her heart pounding. The Beast met her at the gates and led her to a room of impossible luxury — tapestries of gold thread, a bed of silk, windows overlooking gardens that changed with the seasons.
Each evening, the Beast visited her. He was clumsy and rough, but gentle. "Will you marry me, Belle?" he would ask. And each time she answered, "No, Beast." Yet as weeks passed, she began to see the kindness in his eyes, the loneliness in his voice. He brought her books from his library, showed her magic mirrors that revealed distant lands, and never pressed her beyond what she offered freely.
Through the mirror, Belle saw her father ill. She begged the Beast to let her visit. "Go," the Beast said, his voice breaking. "But promise to return in a week, or I shall die of grief."
Belle tended her father, who recovered. But her jealous sisters, seeing her fine gowns, conspired to keep her past the promised week. On the eighth night, Belle dreamed of the Beast lying cold in his rose garden. She cried out and wished herself back at the castle.
She found him exactly as in her dream — still and lifeless among withered roses. She threw herself upon him, sobbing. "I love you, Beast! I cannot live without you!"
At her words, light flooded the garden. The Beast rose, transformed into a prince with honey-colored eyes. A wicked fairy had cursed him, and only true love could break the spell — freely given and freely received. Belle and the prince married in the castle chapel, and the kingdom rejoiced.
moral: True beauty is found within - love transforms even the most beastly exterior.
source: Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve (1740), popularized by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont (1756)

title: Cinderella
summary: A mistreated girl goes to the ball with magic help. Centers on Fairy,  from Europe. nature-spirit associated with forest and meadow..
full_text:
A rich man's wife fell sick. On her deathbed, she called her daughter: Be good and pious, and God will protect you. I will watch over you from heaven.' She died. The girl visited her mothers grave daily, weeping.

When winter came, the father remarried—a cruel woman with two daughters. They were beautiful but had black hearts. 'Why should that stupid goose sit in the parlor? they mocked. They gave Cinderella old gray clothes and wooden shoes. 'Look at the proud princess! they laughed.

Cinderella worked from dawn to dusk—carrying water, making fires, cooking, washing. She slept by the hearth in ashes, covered in dust—so they called her Cinderella.

One day, the father went to the fair. The stepsisters asked for beautiful dresses and jewels. Cinderella, shy, asked for the first branch that hits his hat on the way home. He brought it—she planted it on her mother's grave. Her tears watered it—and it grew into a beautiful tree.

When she shook the tree, a white bird dropped whatever she wished for.

The king announced a three-day ball for the prince to choose a bride. The stepsisters were invited. Cinderella asked to go. You? In your dirty clothes? No!' But she went to the hazel tree: Shake, shake, little tree—silver and gold throw down to me!' A magnificent dress and golden slippers appeared.

At the ball, no one recognized her. The prince danced with no one else. At midnight, she fled—losing a slipper.

The prince announced: Whoever fits this glass slipper shall marry the prince.' The stepsisters tried—their toes were too fat, their heels too large. Then Cinderella appeared: Let me try.' The slipper fit perfectly.

She married the prince. Goodness triumphed over evil.

The lesson: Kindness and piety are rewarded.
moral: Good wins over evil.

title: Sleeping Beauty
summary: The Erl-King is the darkest fairy tale that the Brothers Grimm collected, a poem of terror that has haunted German children for centuries.
full_text:
The Erl-King is the darkest fairy tale that the Brothers Grimm collected, a poem of terror that has haunted German children for centuries. It is the story of a father riding through the forest at midnight, his sick son clutched to his chest.

The father rides with desperate speed. His son shivers with fever, his skin burning against his father's chest. The night is dark, the wind howls through the bare branches. And then the boy hears a whisper.

"Sweet child, come with me," the voice says. "I will play with you. I have flowers of gold and my mother has robes of silk."

The father does not hear the voice. He rides faster, his horse's hooves thundering on the frozen ground. But the boy sees the Erl-King now — a tall, thin figure with a crown of twisted twigs and eyes like dying coals. The Erl-King reaches out with pale fingers that look like birch bark.

"I have daughters who dance in the moonlight," the Erl-King says. "They will dance with you and sing you to sleep."

The father tries to comfort his son. "It is only the wind in the leaves," he says. "It is only the gray mist rising from the river."

But the Erl-King grows impatient. His voice turns from sweet to harsh. "If you will not come willingly, I will take you by force!"

The boy screams. The father digs his heels into his horse and rides with all his might. He reaches the courtyard of his home, gasping for breath. But when he looks down at his son, the boy is dead in his arms.

Goethe's poem "Der Erlkönig" made this story famous, and Schubert's music gave it wings. The Erl-King represents death itself — the final journey that every parent fears, the enemy that no love can outrun. The story is a reminder that some evils cannot be outridden, and that the most terrifying monsters are the ones only children can see.
moral: True love breaks any spell.

title: The Ugly Duckling
summary: It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful.. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch.
full_text:
It was summertime, and the countryside was beautiful. A mother duck was sitting on her nest, waiting for her eggs to hatch. At last, the eggs began to crack—one by one, fluffy yellow ducklings tumbled out. But one egg remained, larger than all the others.

Finally, the great big egg cracked, and out came a large, gray duckling. He was homely and awkward—nothing like his pretty yellow siblings. The mother duck looked at him with surprise, wondering if he could really be hers.

The next day, the mother duck took all her children to the farmyard. There was a pond, and the ducklings swam beautifully. But the gray duckling was left behind, and the farmyard was full of mean ducks who pecked at him. 'He is so ugly! they quacked. Even his own brothers and sisters were unkind to him.

The ugly duckling was so sad that he ran away. He wandered through the fields and meadows, lonely and cold. He came to a pond where wild ducks lived. 'You are very ugly, said the wild ducks, 'but as long as you dont bother our families, you may stay.' A hunters dog came close, but when he saw the ugly duckling, he thought he was not worth catching and ran away.

The ugly duckling wandered on through bushes and tall grass. An old woman found him and put him in her oven to warm him, but she shooed him out in a hurry. He wandered through a garden where roses bloomed, and a swallow asked him why he was so ugly. But the swallow was shot by a hunter, and the ugly duckling was left alone again.

Winter came, and the pond froze. The ugly duckling swam round and round to keep a patch of water open, but eventually he fell exhausted into the ice. A kind farmer found him, picked him up with his bare hands, and brought him home to his wife and children. The children wanted to play with him roughly, but the kind farmer saved him.

At last, spring arrived. The ugly duckling saw three beautiful white birds flying toward him—swans! They were so graceful, so beautiful. The other birds on the pond quacked merrily, and the children called out, 'Look! There are new birds—see how beautiful! The ugly duckling bowed his head, hoping they would not notice his ugliness.

But what did he see reflected in the water? Not an ugly gray duckling—he saw a beautiful white swan! The other swans came to him and stroked his feathers with their beaks. Children ran to call everyone: 'Come look! Three new swans—there is a new one, the most beautiful! They threw bread and cake into the water.

The ugly duckling was so happy he had been through so much and had never given up hope. Now he was beautiful, and the swans welcomed him. He felt such joy—he had never known such happiness.

And so he lived happily, floating on the beautiful pond, surrounded by the love and admiration he had always dreamed of.
moral: What seems ugly may become beautiful.

title: The Little Mermaid
summary: A young mermaid princess sacrifices her voice for legs to win a prince's love. When he marries another, she faces a choice: kill him or become sea foam. She chooses sacrifice.
full_text:
Far out in the ocean, where the water is as blue as the petals of the loveliest cornflower and as clear as the purest glass, in the deepest depths where no anchor can reach, lay the kingdom of the Sea King. His palace rose from the ocean floor — walls of coral, windows of amber, a roof of mussel shells that opened and closed with the tide, each shell holding a pearl that would be the pride of any queen's crown.
Here lived the Sea King's six daughters, mermaid princesses, but the youngest was the most beautiful of all. Her skin was as soft as a rose petal, her eyes as blue as the deepest sea, and like all mermaids, she had no feet — her body ended in a gleaming fish tail. She was a quiet child, thoughtful and dreamy. She loved nothing more than to hear stories of the world above the waves, where flowers had fragrance, birds sang, and humans had immortal souls.
"Grandmother," she asked, "do humans live forever?"
"No, dear child," the old queen said. "They live far shorter than our three hundred years, but when they die, their souls rise to heaven. We, on the other hand, dissolve into sea foam and are no more."
The little mermaid gazed upward. "I would give all my three hundred years for one day as a human and a share in that eternal kingdom."
On her fifteenth birthday, she swam to the surface for the first time. A great ship lay at anchor, lanterns blazing. On deck, a young prince was celebrating. He was the most beautiful human she had ever seen — dark eyes, fair skin, a smile that made her heart tremble. She watched him all evening.
Then a storm came. Lightning split the sky, waves as tall as mountains broke over the ship. The prince was thrown into the churning sea. The little mermaid swam through the wreckage, her arms and tail burning with effort, and caught him just as he lost consciousness. She held his head above the water and swam through the raging storm until she reached a sandy beach near a white temple.
She laid him on the sand and kissed his forehead. When a young woman from the temple approached, the little mermaid slipped back into the water, hiding among the rocks. She watched as the woman revived the prince. When he opened his eyes, he smiled at her — believing she had saved him.
Heartbroken yet determined, the little mermaid swam to the cave of the Sea Witch. The witch lived in a house built of drowned sailors' bones, with a garden of poisonous flowers. "I know what you want," the witch cackled. "And I will help you — for a price. I will brew a potion that will give you legs. Every step you take will feel like walking on knives. And you will never return to the sea. If the prince marries another, you will dissolve into foam at dawn. But most precious of all — I must have your voice."
The little mermaid agreed. Her tongue was cut out, and she drank the potion. As the sun rose, she swam to the palace steps, where the change took her. Her tail split into two legs — and the pain was as the witch had promised, like a thousand knives.
The prince found her on the shore, naked and unable to speak. He took her into the palace, dressed her in silks, and grew fond of her. She danced for him, though every step felt like treading on sharp blades. She slept on a cushion at his door. But he did not love her — not as he loved the woman from the temple, whom he believed had saved his life.
The king commanded the prince to marry a princess from a neighboring kingdom. The prince went reluctantly, and when he saw the princess, he gasped — it was the woman from the temple. "Now I have found the one who saved me!" he cried. The wedding was celebrated.
On the wedding night, the little mermaid stood on the deck of the ship, holding the knife her sisters had traded their hair for. If she plunged it into the prince's heart, she would become a mermaid again. But she looked at his sleeping face, at his bride beside him, and she could not. She flung the knife into the sea, where it turned to blood-red foam.
At dawn, she threw herself into the water, expecting to dissolve. But instead, she rose into the air among the daughters of the air — ethereal beings who earn immortal souls through good deeds. "You have suffered and endured," they told her. "By doing good for three hundred years, you may obtain an immortal soul." And the little mermaid rose toward the sun, kissed the prince's bride unseen, and began her long journey toward eternal life.
moral: True love is about sacrifice, not possession - the little mermaid's selflessness earns her an immortal soul.

title: The Princess and the Pea
summary: Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry.
full_text:
Once upon a time, in a kingdom of rolling green hills and turreted castles, there lived a prince who searched the world for a true princess to marry. He traveled to kingdoms near and far, meeting dozens of royal maidens, but something was always wrong with each one. Some were too proud, others too dull. Some lacked refinement, others were too calculating. After years of fruitless searching, the prince returned home heartbroken. "There are princesses enough in the world," he told his father the king, "but how can I tell which one is real?"
One evening, a terrible storm descended upon the kingdom. Thunder rolled like drums of the gods, lightning split the darkness, and rain fell in sheets so thick that the guards could barely see the castle walls. The wind howled through the turrets, and ancient oaks bent nearly to the ground. As the storm raged, a knocking echoed through the great hall. The old king himself answered, for he could not bear to leave any traveler stranded in such weather.
There stood a young woman, drenched and shivering. Water streamed from her hair and clothing, pooling at her bare feet. Her dress was plastered to her body, her face was pale with cold, and yet there was something in her bearing — a quiet dignity even in this bedraggled state. "I am a princess," she said through chattering teeth. "I lost my way in the storm. I beg shelter for the night."
The queen, wise and practical, was not easily convinced. She smiled warmly but said nothing of her doubts. Instead, she went to the guest chamber and devised a test. She stripped the bed of all its linens, placed a single dried pea on the bare mattress, then piled twenty mattresses on top of the pea, and then twenty eider-down feather beds on top of the mattresses. On this mountain of bedding, the princess was to sleep.
In the morning, the queen asked how she had rested. "Oh, very badly!" the princess exclaimed. "I scarcely closed my eyes all night. Heaven only knows what could be in that bed, but I was lying on something so hard that I am black and blue all over!"
Now they knew she was a true princess, for only a real princess could possess such delicate sensitivity as to feel a single pea through twenty mattresses and twenty feather beds. The prince married her at once, overjoyed that he had finally found his real princess. As for the pea, it was placed in the royal museum, where it may still be seen to this day — if no one has stolen it.
moral: Real is recognized.

title: Thumbelina
summary: There once lived a woman who wanted a tiny child more than anything in the world.
full_text:
There once lived a woman who wanted a tiny child more than anything in the world. She went to an old fairy woman, who gave her a barleycorn and told her to plant it in a flowerpot. The woman did as instructed, and immediately a beautiful flower sprouted — a tulip with petals of red and gold, wrapped tight as a blanket. The woman kissed the petals, and with a soft pop, the flower opened. At its center, on the green velvet stamens, sat a tiny girl, no larger than the woman's thumb. She was delicate as porcelain, with hair fine as spider silk and eyes bright as dew. The woman named her Thumbelina.
She made Thumbelina a bed from half a polished walnut shell, with a mattress of violet petals and a coverlet of rose petal. During the day, Thumbelina sailed across a plate of water on a tulip petal, using two white horsehairs as oars. Her singing was the sweetest ever heard — soft as a lullaby, clear as a bell.
One night, a great ugly toad hopped through the broken windowpane and saw Thumbelina sleeping. "What a pretty little wife for my son!" she croaked. She seized the walnut shell and carried it to the middle of a stream, where her son lived on a lily pad. The son was even uglier than his mother, covered in warts, but when he saw Thumbelina, all he could say was, "Croak, croak, croak!"
Thumbelina wept at the thought of marrying a toad and living in the mud. The fish in the stream took pity on her and nibbled the lily pad's stem until it broke free. Thumbelina drifted away downstream, leaving the toads far behind.
Her joy was short-lived. A large cockchafer beetle found her and carried her to a tree, where he introduced her to the other beetles. But they declared her hideous — she had only two legs, no feelers, and was shaped all wrong. The beetle, ashamed, abandoned her. Thumbelina wept alone. For the entire summer and autumn, she lived in the forest, eating nectar from flowers and drinking dew. She wove herself a hammock from grass blades and slept under a burdock leaf.
But winter came. The birds flew away, the flowers died, and the cold wind cut through her thin leaf clothing. Snow began to fall — each flake to her was as terrible as a shovelful of snow falling on a human. She wrapped herself in a withered leaf, trembling and hungry.
She found the door of a field mouse, who took her in. The mouse was kind but narrow-minded. "You may stay the winter," she said, "if you keep my house clean and tell me stories." Thumbelina agreed. One day, the mouse's neighbor, a wealthy mole, came to visit. He was blind and covered in dark velvet fur. He fell in love with Thumbelina at once, though he could not see her. He told her about his underground tunnels and showed her his vast stores of grain.
In one of the tunnels, Thumbelina found a swallow, frozen and seemingly dead. She nursed it back to health, warming it with stolen moments of care. As spring came, the swallow offered to take her away. "Come with me to warm countries where flowers bloom all year!"
But Thumbelina felt obligated to the field mouse. She stayed. The mole proposed marriage. Thumbelina was horrified at the thought of living underground forever, never seeing the sun. The field mouse forced her to prepare her dowry — spinning and weaving all summer.
On the wedding day, Thumbelina crept outside to say goodbye to the sun. "Farewell, bright sun!" she cried. At that moment, the swallow appeared. "Come with me now!" he urged. Thumbelina climbed onto his back, and they flew away over mountains and seas to a land of eternal warmth.
The swallow set her down in a bed of white marble among the most beautiful flowers in the world. In the center of the largest flower sat a tiny prince, no bigger than Thumbelina, with golden wings and a crown of diamonds on his head. He was the king of the flower angels. When he saw Thumbelina, he fell in love. He placed a crown on her head and gave her wings. They were married among the flowers, and her name was changed to Maia. And Thumbelina, who had been so small and so alone, finally found where she belonged.
moral: Small size does not limit a gentle heart.

title: The Red Shoes
summary: There was once a little girl named Karen, pretty and delicate, but so poor that she wore wooden shoes in winter that chafed her ankles raw.
full_text:
There was once a little girl named Karen, pretty and delicate, but so poor that she wore wooden shoes in winter that chafed her ankles raw. When her mother died, the only thing left was a pair of clumsy red shoes stitched from scraps of cloth. Karen wore those red shoes to her mother's funeral — not mourning shoes at all, but she had no others.
An old lady in a carriage saw the orphan and took pity on her. She brought Karen to her home, clothed her, fed her, and taught her to read and sew. The old lady was nearly blind and did not know the shoes had been red. When she saw them, she declared them hideous and had them burned. Karen was given proper clothing and grew up in comfort.
But she never forgot those red shoes. One day, she saw a pair in a shop window — red morocco leather, just like the shoes the princess had worn when the royal carriage passed through town. She begged the old lady to buy them for her confirmation, and the old lady, not seeing their color, agreed.
On confirmation day, Karen walked into the church in those red shoes. Everyone stared at her feet. She thought of nothing during the service — not the holy words, not the organ music — only the shoes. The old lady was told and was deeply shocked. "You must never wear them to church again," she said. "It is not proper."
But the next Sunday, Karen put on the red shoes anyway. An old soldier with a red beard stood outside the church and exclaimed, "What beautiful dancing shoes! They will stick fast when you dance!" Karen danced a few steps, and her feet began to move of their own accord. The shoes seemed to pull her into a wild dance she could not control. She danced through the church doors, down the path, and into the street. She could not stop.
The old lady fell ill, but Karen did not stay to nurse her. She put on the red shoes and went to a ball instead. The shoes danced her through the night, past midnight, into the dawn. She could not stop. She danced through fields and meadows, through brambles that tore her skin. The shoes danced her to the old lady's funeral, and past it, because she could not stop.
An angel appeared before her, white and terrible. "You shall dance," he said, "in your red shoes until you become pale and thin, until you are nothing but a skeleton. You shall dance from door to door, and wherever proud, vain children live, you shall knock and they shall fear you."
Karen begged for mercy, but the shoes danced her away. She danced through rain and sun, through day and night. Her feet bled. She grew thin and pale. At last, she crawled to the house of the executioner. "Cut off my feet!" she cried. "Cut off my feet and free me from this curse!"
The executioner did as she asked. The red shoes danced away with her severed feet, disappearing into the forest. But Karen had no feet now. She hobbled to the church on crutches and prayed. She prayed until her heart broke with remorse and her eyes ran with tears of repentance. "Have mercy on me, O God," she whispered.
The angel returned, but this time with a branch of roses. He touched Karen's head, and where he touched, peace flowed into her soul. She felt such light and love that her heart nearly burst. She was given a new pair of feet, not of flesh but of grace, and she lived out her days in quiet devotion, humble and grateful, never again looking at shoes with longing.
moral: Vanity leads to suffering; humility brings grace.

title: The Wild Swans
summary: Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa.
full_text:
Far away, in a land where swallows fly when winter comes, there lived a king who had eleven sons and one daughter named Elisa. The eleven princes went to school with stars on their chests and swords at their sides, writing with diamond pens on golden slates. Elisa sat on a little stool of polished glass, turning the pages of a picture book worth half a kingdom. They were the happiest children in the world.
But the king married a wicked queen who had no love for her stepchildren. On the very day of the wedding, she gave them sand in a teacup and told them to pretend it was cake. A week later, she sent Elisa to live with peasants in the countryside. Her hatred for the princes burned even hotter. "Go out into the world and fend for yourselves!" she shrieked, throwing enchanted dust over them. "Fly like great birds without voices!" The eleven princes were transformed into eleven magnificent wild swans. With a strange, mournful cry, they rose from the courtyard and flew into the deep forest.
Elisa grew up in the peasant cottage, dreaming of her brothers. At fifteen, she returned to the palace, but the queen, seeing her beauty, rubbed her face with walnut juice and tangled her hair with foul ointments until even her father did not recognize her. Heartbroken, Elisa slipped away into the forest. She wandered for days, eating berries, drinking from streams, sleeping on moss.
One evening, she came to a lake. At sunset, eleven white swans with golden crowns descended from the sky. As the sun sank behind the mountains, the swans shed their feathers and became her brothers. They wept and embraced, telling of their enchantment. "When the sun rises, we become swans," said the eldest. "Only at sunset do we regain human form. We live across the sea, in a land even more beautiful than this."
"We will carry you there tomorrow," they said.
At dawn, the brothers became swans again. They lifted Elisa on their backs and flew across the vast ocean. The journey was perilous — storms raged, and the sun burned. Elisa prayed for strength.
On the far shore, she had a dream. A fairy appeared, resembling the old woman who had given her berries in the forest. "Your brothers can be saved," the fairy said, "but you must have courage. You must weave eleven shirts from stinging nettles that grow in churchyards and on graves. While you weave, you must not speak a single word — not one — until the shirts are finished. If you speak, your brothers will die."
Elisa set to work. She gathered nettles that blistered her hands. She crushed them with her bare feet and wove the fibers into thread. Night after night she worked, her hands covered in blisters. Her brothers wept to see her suffering, but she could not speak to comfort them.
One day, a great king hunting in the forest found Elisa and fell in love with her. He took her to his palace and married her, though she never spoke. The archbishop grew suspicious of her silence. "She is a witch," he whispered. "She practices dark magic."
Elisa continued weaving in the palace, even as her hands grew raw. The nettles ran out, and she had to gather more from the churchyard at night. The archbishop followed her and saw her among the graves. "She is a witch!" he declared.
Elisa was condemned to be burned at the stake. On the morning of her execution, she was taken through the streets in a cart, still weaving the last shirt. The people jeered. The executioner seized her arm — but at that moment, eleven swans descended from the sky, circling overhead.
Elisa threw the nettle shirts over the swans. In an instant, they became her eleven brothers — all except the youngest, who still had a swan's wing instead of an arm, for she had not finished the last sleeve.
"Now I may speak!" Elisa cried. "I am innocent!" She told the king everything. The archbishop was banished, and the brothers were restored to their kingdom. Elisa and her brothers returned home, where they lived together in peace, their bond unbroken by the years of silence.
moral: Love endures any sacrifice.

title: The Fisherman and His Wife
summary: There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty.
full_text:
There was once a poor fisherman who lived with his wife, Ilsebill, in a miserable hovel by the sea — a place so small and filthy it was scarcely better than a pigsty. Every day the fisherman cast his line into the sparkling water, hoping for a decent meal.
One day, his line jerked downward with tremendous force. He pulled and pulled until he dragged up a magnificent flounder. But this was no ordinary fish. It looked at him with intelligent eyes and spoke: "I pray you, let me live. I am not a real flounder but an enchanted prince. What good would killing me do? Put me back, I beg you."
The startled but kindhearted fisherman said, "A fish that can talk I shall certainly let go." He released the flounder, who swam away leaving a trail of blood behind him.
When the fisherman returned home and told his wife, she was furious. "You caught a magical fish that grants wishes, and you asked for nothing? Look at this stinking hovel! Go back and demand a proper cottage!"
The fisherman did not want to, but his wife gave him no peace. He returned to the shore, where the sea had turned from clear to green and yellow. He called out, "Flounder, flounder in the sea, come, I pray thee, here to me. For my wife, good Ilsebill, wills not as I'd have her will."
The flounder appeared. "What does she want?" "A cottage," the fisherman whispered. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has it already."
When the fisherman returned, the hovel was replaced by a charming cottage with a flower garden, a yard full of ducks and chickens, and a well-stocked pantry. Ilsebill was delighted — for a week. Then she said, "This cottage is too small. Go back and demand a stone castle."
The fisherman went, though his heart was heavy. The sea was purple with rage. "Go home," said the flounder. "She has her castle."
Ilsebill now lived in a grand castle with a hundred servants, gold and silver furniture, and a park half a mile long. But she was not satisfied. "I want to be king," she declared. The fisherman protested, but she screamed until he went back. The sea was dark gray and churning. "She wants to be king," the fisherman said. "She is king already," the flounder replied.
Ilsebill sat on a throne of gold and diamonds. But the next morning, she woke the fisherman. "I want to be emperor — emperor of all the lands!" The fisherman begged her to be content. She threatened him. The sea was black and boiling. "She wants to be emperor," the fisherman told the flounder. "Go home. She is emperor."
Ilsebill sat on a throne of solid gold, guarded by soldiers from a hundred nations. Still, she was not satisfied. "Husband," she said, "go back to the flounder. Tell him I want to be pope." The fisherman's blood ran cold. "Wife, how can you be pope? There is only one pope in all of Christendom." "I will be that pope!" she screamed.
The sea raged in a tempest. The fisherman could barely stand. "She wants to be pope." "Go home. She is pope."
Ilsebill sat enthroned in St. Peter's, wearing the triple crown. Kings knelt before her. But that night, she could not sleep. "Husband! Go back! Tell the flounder I want to be like God — to command the sun and moon to rise at my will!"
The fisherman crawled to the shore through a hurricane. "She wants to be like God," he cried.
The flounder looked at him with ancient, weary eyes. "Go home. You will find her back in the pigsty."
And there she was, sitting in their original hovel, the stinking, filthy shack by the sea, where they lived for the rest of their days.
moral: Contentment is wisdom.

title: The Goose Girl
summary: There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before.. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom.
full_text:
There once lived an old queen whose husband had died years before. She had one beautiful daughter, who was promised in marriage to a prince in a distant kingdom. When the time came for the princess to depart, the queen prepared a rich dowry — jewels, gold, fine garments, and two horses: a common nag for the waiting-maid, and for the princess, a magnificent horse named Falada, who could speak. Before leaving, the queen pricked her finger, let three drops of blood fall onto a handkerchief, and tucked it into her daughter's bodice. "Keep this carefully," she said. "It will protect you on the road."
The princess tucked the handkerchief into her bosom and set out. As they rode through the countryside, the princess grew thirsty. "Pray, fetch me water from the stream in my golden cup," she said to her maid.
"If you are thirsty," the maid replied rudely, "dismount yourself and drink. I will be your maid no longer." The gentle princess obeyed without complaint. As she bent over the water, the handkerchief fell from her bodice and floated away. The maid saw this and rejoiced, for she now had power over her mistress.
The maid forced the princess to exchange clothes. She mounted Falada and threatened to kill the princess unless she swore never to tell anyone what had happened. The poor princess had no choice.
When they arrived at the prince's palace, the maid was welcomed as the bride, while the true princess was given the lowliest task: tending geese in the fields with a boy named Conrad.
The false bride feared Falada, who could speak and reveal the truth. She ordered the horse killed. The true princess begged the knacker to nail Falada's head above the dark gateway through which she passed each morning with the geese. As she passed the gate, she would say, "Falada, Falada, hanging there," and the horse's head would answer, "Alas, young queen, if your mother knew, her heart would break in two."
One day, Conrad noticed the goose girl talking to the horse's head. He also saw that her hair gleamed like spun gold when the wind lifted her bonnet. He told the king, who became curious. The next day, the king hid behind the gateway and heard the conversation between the girl and the horse's head.
The king called the goose girl before him. She threw herself at his feet, weeping, and told him everything — but she had sworn an oath of silence about the betrayal. So she told him only that she had been forced to take a terrible oath. The king, wise and kind, said, "If you cannot tell me, then tell this iron stove." He left her alone in a room with an old stove, and she poured out her heart to the iron grating, believing no one could hear. But the king had hidden a pipe that carried her voice to him in the next room. He heard everything.
The king ordered a great feast. He seated the false bride on one side and the true princess on the other. He told the story of a maid who had betrayed her mistress. "What punishment does such a woman deserve?" he asked. The false bride, not recognizing the story, answered, "She should be stripped naked and thrown into a barrel lined with sharp nails, dragged through the streets by wild horses."
"You have pronounced your own sentence," said the king. The false bride was dealt with as she had described. The true princess married the prince, and Falada's head was buried in the garden, where a tree grew in his memory — a tree that whispered the truth to anyone who would listen.
moral: Truth always revealed.

title: The Six Servants
summary: A prince travels with six extraordinary servants who each possess unique abilities (super hearing, super sight, super strength, etc.). Together they overcome impossible challenges to win a princess.
full_text:
Long ago, an aged queen who was a sorceress ruled over a kingdom with her daughter, the most beautiful maiden beneath the sun. But the old woman's heart was black. Whenever a suitor appeared, she set him three impossible tasks, and when he failed, his head was struck off. Many princes had died this way.
A certain king's son heard of the maiden's beauty and declared, "I will go and win her." His father forbade it, but the prince fell so ill with longing that for seven years he lay sick, and no physician could cure him. "Go, then," the father relented. The prince rose from his bed, instantly well, and joyfully set out.
As he rode across a heath, he saw what looked like a great heap of hay. Drawing closer, he realized it was the stomach of a man, enormous as a mountain. "If you need a servant," the fat giant rumbled, "take me." The prince asked his talents. "I can stretch myself to become even vaster, and I can eat and drink more than a thousand men."
Next, he met a man lying on the ground with his ear pressed to the earth. "I can hear the grass growing," the Listener said. "And I just heard someone being beheaded in the queen's castle far away."
Then came a man so tall his legs could stride over mountains. "I can see across the whole world," the Looker said. "At this moment I see a man in Cathay counting his coins."
They found a man with a cloth tied over his eyes. "Why do you cover them?" the prince asked. "Because my gaze shatters everything I see," said the Sharp-Eyed Man. "If I remove this cloth, I can reduce mountains to gravel."
Further on, a man sat shivering in the midday sun. "I feel cold," he said, "even though you burn." Yet when they passed a freezing glacier, steam rose from his body. The Hot-and-Cold Man could endure any temperature.
The sixth servant was a man whose neck stretched like a serpent's. He could see over the highest walls and reach across chasms.
The prince took all six into his service. They arrived at the sorceress's castle. "I wish to marry your daughter," the prince announced. "By all means," the queen smiled wickedly. "But first, fetch my ring from the bottom of the Red Sea."
The Listener heard where the ring lay, wedged between two rocks. The Tall Man strode to the shore. The Stout Man drank the entire sea in seven gulps, leaving the seabed dry. The Sharp-Eyed Man spotted the ring, and the Long-Necked Man reached down and plucked it up.
The queen was enraged but smiled. "Well done. Now, for the second task — eat three hundred oxen, hair, hide, bones, and all, and drink three hundred casks of wine to the last drop."
The Stout Man stepped forward. "Leave this to me." He devoured every ox, crunched every bone, drank every cask, and patted his stomach. "I could have managed more."
The queen's face darkened. "For the third task, you must guard my daughter through the night without sleep. If she is gone by morning, you die."
The prince sat with the princess, but she drugged his wine and slipped away. The Listener, however, heard her footsteps through twenty locked doors. The Long-Necked Man saw her hiding in a distant cave. The Tall Man brought her back before dawn. The prince had passed all three tasks.
The furious queen tried to burn the prince at the stake, but the Hot-and-Cold Man sat in the fire and shivered. When she sent her army to kill them, the Sharp-Eyed Man removed his blindfold and shattered the soldiers to rubble.
The prince married the princess, and the six servants, their work done, departed to seek their own fortunes. The prince never forgot that his greatest strength lay not in himself, but in the unique gifts of those he had chosen to stand beside him.
moral: Every person has unique talents - together, diverse abilities can overcome any obstacle.

title: The Donkey Skin
summary: A princess flees an unwanted marriage to her father by wearing a donkey skin disguise. She works as a servant until a prince falls in love with her true identity.
full_text:
In a magnificent kingdom, there lived a king who was the most powerful sovereign in all the land. His gardens overflowed with exotic flowers, his treasury with gold, and his stables held the finest horses. But the most marvelous creature in his possession was a donkey that produced gold coins instead of manure. Every morning, fresh gold glittered in the donkey's stall.
The king had a wife he loved dearly and a daughter who was the most beautiful princess in the world. But the queen fell gravely ill. On her deathbed, she made the king promise: "Only remarry if you find a woman more beautiful than I."
After the queen died, the king's advisors urged him to marry again. Suitors from across the land presented their daughters, but none matched the late queen's beauty. Then a terrible realization dawned: the only woman more beautiful was his own daughter.
The princess was horrified when her father declared his intention to marry her. She ran to her fairy godmother, the Lilac Fairy, who lived in a grotto deep in the forest. "Do not despair," the fairy said. "Tell your father you require a dress the color of the sky."
The king summoned the finest tailors, who produced a gown of celestial blue, embroidered with silver clouds and diamond stars. The princess demanded a dress the color of the moon — the king delivered one of silver light. She demanded a dress the color of the sun — he produced a gown woven from actual sunbeams, so radiant that those who looked upon it had to shield their eyes.
The princess had run out of excuses. "Ask for the skin of his gold-producing donkey," the fairy whispered. Surely this would stop him.
But the king, mad with love-obsession, killed the donkey and presented the skin to his daughter. The princess wept. "Now you must go," said the fairy. "Put on the donkey skin. It will make you ugly and unrecognizable. Travel to a distant kingdom and find work. No one will know you."
The princess covered herself in the shaggy, foul-smelling donkey skin. She traveled far, her face blackened with soot, until she reached a great city. She found work as a scullery maid on a farm, where she was called Donkey Skin. The other servants mocked her appearance and made her sleep in the attic.
One day, a prince from the neighboring kingdom came to the farm. He was bored and restless. He wandered into the scullery, and through a crack in the door, he saw Donkey Skin in her attic removing the filthy skin. Beneath it, she wore a dress of sunlight. Her beauty struck him like a physical blow.
The prince fell gravely ill with love. "I will only recover," he told his parents, "if Donkey Skin bakes me a cake with her own hands."
The princess, hearing this, baked him a cake. As she worked, a ring from her finger slipped into the batter — a gold ring with an emerald. The prince ate the cake, found the ring, and declared he would only marry the woman whose finger fit this ring.
Every woman in the kingdom tried. The ring fit no one — until Donkey Skin was brought before the prince. She slipped the ring onto her finger. Then she removed the donkey skin and stood before the court in her dress of moonlight.
The prince and princess were married. The princess's father, who had long since repented his madness, attended the wedding and was joyfully reunited with his daughter. The donkey skin was placed in a glass case in the palace, a reminder that true nobility can never truly be hidden.
moral: True nobility cannot be hidden - a princess in donkey skin is still a princess.

title: Bluebeard
summary: Once there lived a man of immense wealth who owned fine houses in town and country, with silver and gold plate, embroidered furniture, and gilded coaches.
full_text:
Once there lived a man of immense wealth who owned fine houses in town and country, with silver and gold plate, embroidered furniture, and gilded coaches. But he was so unfortunate as to have a blue beard, which made him so frightfully ugly that women ran from him. He was known as Bluebeard.
One of his neighbors had two beautiful daughters. Bluebeard desired to marry one, but neither wanted him. Dark rumors said he had married several wives before, and no one knew what had become of them. To win their affections, Bluebeard invited the young women, their mother, and friends to a week of lavish festivities at his country estate. There was hunting, feasting, and dancing without end. The youngest daughter began to think the master was not so terrible after all. When the week ended, she agreed to marry him.
A month after the wedding, Bluebeard announced he must travel. He gave his wife a ring of keys. "Here are the keys to my wardrobes, my strongboxes, my jewels. Open everything and enjoy it freely. But this small key opens the closet at the end of the long gallery. I forbid you to enter that room. If you open it, my anger will be terrible."
His wife promised to obey. As soon as Bluebeard departed, her curiosity burned like fire. She threw a grand party, but the thought of the forbidden room consumed her. Unable to resist, she slipped away, took the small key, and climbed the staircase. Her hand trembled as she unlocked the door.
The room was dark. As her eyes adjusted, she saw the most horrifying sight imaginable. The floor was covered with dried blood. Along the walls hung the bodies of Bluebeard's former wives — women he had married and murdered, their faces frozen in terror.
She dropped the key in shock. When she picked it up, she saw it was stained with blood — blood that would not wash off, no matter how hard she scrubbed. The key was enchanted. Bluebeard would know.
When Bluebeard returned, he saw the stained key. "You have disobeyed me," he said, his voice cold as a tomb. "You entered the forbidden closet. Now you shall join the others."
His wife fell at his feet, weeping, begging for a few moments to pray. "You shall have fifteen minutes," Bluebeard said. "Not a second more."
She ran to her sister Anne, who lived with them. "Sister! Go to the tower! Watch for our brothers! They promised to visit today!"
Anne climbed to the tower. "Do you see anyone coming?" the wife cried. "I see nothing," Anne replied, "but the sun and the dust."
Bluebeard, his sword in hand, roared from below. "Come down, or I will come up!"
"Just a moment more!" the wife pleaded.
"Anne, sister Anne, do you see anyone?"
"I see two riders in the distance!" Anne cried. "They ride fast!"
Bluebeard stormed up the stairs, his sword raised. "Your time is up!"
"Anne! Sister Anne!"
"I see them! Our brothers!" Anne screamed.
The two brothers — a dragoon and a musketeer — burst through the gate. Bluebeard tried to flee but was cornered and run through with a sword. He fell dead.
Bluebeard's wife inherited his entire fortune. She used it wisely, marrying a good man and ensuring her sister Anne married the man she loved. The story of Bluebeard was told for generations, a warning about curiosity — and also about the importance of trusting one's instincts when something feels wrong.
moral: Curiosity kills.

title: Puss in Boots
summary: A clever cat in boots uses trickery and deceit to win his poor master wealth, a castle, and a princess. The cat's cunning elevates his master from poverty to royalty.
full_text:
There was once a poor miller who died and left his three sons nothing but the mill, the donkey, and the cat. The eldest took the mill, the second took the donkey, and the youngest was left with only the cat. "What am I to do with a cat?" the young man lamented. "My brothers can earn a living, but I will starve once I have eaten this cat and made a muff of his skin."
The cat, who had been listening, spoke in a clear, serious voice. "Do not trouble yourself, my good master. Give me a bag and have a pair of boots made for me so that I may scamper through the brambles. You shall see you are not so poorly off as you think."
The astonished young man did as asked. The cat pulled on the boots with great dignity, slung the bag over his shoulder, and set off. He filled the bag with bran and lay down in a rabbit warren, pretending to be dead. A foolish rabbit hopped in, and Puss drew the strings tight. He marched to the king's palace. "A gift from my noble master, the Marquis of Carabas!" he announced, presenting the rabbit. The king was delighted.
For months, Puss continued to bring gifts of game, always claiming they were from the Marquis of Carabas. The king grew to think very highly of this mysterious nobleman.
One day, Puss learned the king and his daughter were to ride along the riverbank. He instructed his master, "Go bathe in the river at the spot I show you. Leave the rest to me." As the king's carriage approached, Puss began to shout, "Help! The Marquis of Carabas is drowning!" The king recognized the cat and ordered the "Marquis" rescued. His servants brought fine clothes for the young man, who looked handsome in them. The princess fell in love instantly.
Puss ran ahead of the carriage. He came to fields where reapers worked. "The king will ask who owns these fields," he told the reapers. "If you say they belong to the Marquis of Carabas, you will be rewarded." He ran to meadows where mowers worked, then to farms, giving the same instructions. When the king asked who owned each property, the terrified workers all answered, "The Marquis of Carabas, Your Majesty."
Puss reached a magnificent castle belonging to an ogre — the wealthiest being in the land, who could change shape at will. The ogre was the true owner of all the fields and forests through which they had passed. Puss knocked at the castle gates. "I have heard you can transform into any animal," Puss said to the ogre. "A lion, for instance. Could you become a mouse? Surely even the greatest shape-shifter could not manage something so small."
"Could I not?" the ogre laughed, and transformed into a tiny mouse. Puss pounced and ate him in one bite.
When the king arrived at the castle, Puss threw open the gates. "Welcome to the castle of the Marquis of Carabas!" The king, overwhelmed by the young man's wealth and impressed by his charm, offered him his daughter's hand. The miller's son married the princess and became a great lord. And Puss in Boots lived out his days as a noble cat, catching mice only for sport, having elevated his master — and himself — far beyond their humble beginnings.
moral: Cleverness and presentation matter more than wealth - a cat in boots can make his master a king.

title: Little Red Riding Hood
summary: In Jamaica, when the sun goes down and the shadows grow long, grandmothers gather the children on their porches and tell them about Rolling Calf.
full_text:
In Jamaica, when the sun goes down and the shadows grow long, grandmothers gather the children on their porches and tell them about Rolling Calf. This is the beast that walks the roads at midnight, and its appearance means trouble for anyone who sees it.

Rolling Calf is a massive bull that walks on its hind legs like a man. Its eyes blaze like two coals from a fire, and chains rattle around its neck as it moves. It has no tail, and its hooves are cloven like the devil's. But the most terrifying thing about Rolling Calf is the sound it makes — not a moo or a bellow, but the sound of a man crying in pain.

Where does Rolling Calf come from? The old folks say it is the spirit of a cruel slave master or a wicked overseer who mistreated the people under his charge. When he died, he was cursed to walk the earth as a beast, forever tormented by the chains he once used on others. His crying is the sound of his own guilt, which he cannot escape.

Rolling Calf haunts the roads of rural Jamaica, especially in the parish of St. Elizabeth. He appears to anyone who travels alone at night. If you see him, do not run — he will chase you. Do not show fear — he will feed on it. Instead, stop and face him. Spit on the ground three times and say, "I am a child of God, and you have no power here."

There is only one sure way to defeat Rolling Calf. You must throw a handful of rice at his feet. The beast is compelled to count every grain before he can continue his pursuit. By the time he finishes counting, the sun will be rising, and he must retreat to the shadows until the next night.

Rolling Calf is a symbol of Jamaica's painful history — a reminder that the sins of slavery do not disappear with death. The beast walks the roads still, his chains rattling, his crying echoing through the hills, waiting for those who travel alone in the darkness.
moral: Never talk to strangers.

title: Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs
summary: A beautiful princess escapes her jealous stepmother-queen and finds refuge with seven dwarfs. The queen tricks her with a poisoned apple, but a prince's kiss awakens her.
full_text:
In the middle of winter, when snowflakes fell like feathers from heaven, a queen sat sewing at a window framed in black ebony. She pricked her finger, and three drops of blood fell onto the snow. "How I wish for a daughter," she sighed, "with skin as white as snow, lips as red as blood, and hair as black as ebony." Soon after, she gave birth to a baby girl who was exactly that — skin white as snow, lips red as blood, hair black as ebony. They named her Snow White. But the queen died in childbirth.
A year later, the king married again. His new wife was beautiful but vain and cruel. She possessed a magic mirror, and each morning she asked, "Mirror, mirror, on the wall, who is the fairest of them all?" The mirror always answered, "You, O Queen, are the fairest of all." This satisfied her, for she could not bear anyone being more beautiful than herself.
Snow White grew more beautiful each year. When she was seven, the mirror told the queen, "O Queen, you are fair, it is true, but Snow White is a thousand times fairer than you." The queen flew into a rage. From that moment, she hated Snow White with a jealousy that consumed her.
She called a huntsman. "Take Snow White into the forest and kill her. Bring me her heart as proof." The huntsman led Snow White into the woods, but when he drew his knife, the girl's innocent tears melted his heart. "Run away, poor child," he said. "Run deep into the forest and never return." He killed a wild boar and brought its heart to the queen, who believed it was Snow White's.
Alone in the vast forest, Snow White ran through brambles that tore her dress and past wild animals that somehow did not harm her. As night fell, she stumbled upon a tiny cottage, neat and orderly. She pushed open the door. Inside was a table set with seven plates, seven cups, seven knives, and seven spoons. On the walls hung seven little beds, each with a white coverlet. She was so hungry and tired that she ate a few bites from each plate and fell asleep across several of the beds.
When the seven dwarfs returned from their work mining gold in the mountains, they found a beautiful girl sleeping in their home. They were astonished by her loveliness and moved by her story. "You may stay with us," they said, "if you keep house — cook, wash, sew, and make the beds."
Snow White agreed, and the dwarfs warned her: "Beware the queen. She will try to find you. Let no one into the cottage while we are away."
The queen, consulting her mirror, learned that Snow White still lived. She disguised herself as an old peddler woman and traveled to the cottage. "Pretty laces for sale!" she called. Snow White, seeing only a harmless old woman, bought a pair of laces. The queen laced them so tightly that Snow White collapsed. The dwarfs returned just in time to cut the laces and revive her.
The queen tried again, this time with a poisoned comb. Snow White fell as if dead when the comb touched her hair, but the dwarfs removed it and saved her.
The third time, the queen prepared a poisoned apple — half red, half white. The white half was harmless; the red half was death. She disguised herself as a farmer's wife and approached the cottage. The dwarfs had warned Snow White to let no one in, but the queen held up the apple. "Taste this beautiful apple," she said, and bit the white half herself. Snow White, seeing the woman eat it safely, took a bite of the red half — and fell dead.
This time, the dwarfs could not revive her. She looked so beautiful, with rosy cheeks and peaceful expression, that they could not bear to bury her. Instead, they made a coffin of glass and gold and placed her on a hillside, taking turns watching over her.
Years passed. A prince traveling through the forest saw Snow White in her glass coffin and fell in love with her. "Let me have her," he begged the dwarfs. "I cannot live without her." The dwarfs, moved by his grief, agreed. As the prince's servants carried the coffin, they stumbled, and the piece of poisoned apple lodged in Snow White's throat dislodged. She woke.
The prince married Snow White that very day. The queen, invited to the wedding, was forced to put on red-hot iron shoes and dance until she dropped dead — a punishment for her cruelty. Snow White and the prince lived happily ever after, and the seven dwarfs were frequent guests at the royal palace, their friendship cherished forever. egg into the dark water. The salmon Boots had saved dove deep and brought the egg back in its mouth.

"Squeeze it," said the wolf. Boots squeezed, and far away, the giant screamed in agony. "Squeeze again," said the wolf, and when Boots did, the giant's voice came thin and desperate: "Spare me!
