But when she saw Kastytis, her anger melted. He was the most beautiful man she had ever seen. Instead of punishing him, she fell in love.

Jūratė brought Kastytis to her amber palace. They lived together in happiness, the goddess and the fisherman, their love bridging the divide between the divine and the human. Jūratė thought the gods would not notice.

But the thunder god Perkūnas noticed. He was the ruler of the sky, the punisher of those who broke the cosmic laws. A goddess could not love a mortal — it was the most ancient of taboos. Perkūnas hurled a lightning bolt from the sky. It struck the amber palace, shattering it into a million pieces. The walls crumbled, the domes collapsed, and Jūratė's beautiful home was destroyed.

Jūratė was chained to the ruins of her palace at the bottom of the sea, forever mourning her lost love. Kastytis was drowned. But the pieces of amber from Jūratė's palace continue to wash ashore on the beaches of Lithuania and Latvia, carried by the waves, scattered by the storms.

Every piece of amber found on the Baltic coast is a fragment of Jūratė's shattered palace. The small insects and leaves trapped inside the amber are the decorations of her home. The roar of the storm at sea is Jūratė's grief, still crying for Kastytis after thousands of years. When you hold a piece of Baltic amber in your hand, you are touching the ruins of a love story that has never ended.
moral: Some loves are too powerful for this world; they shatter everything around them.

title: The Shtriga: The Vampire Witch of Albanian Folklore
summary: An ancient witch feeds on the life force of infants, escaping through keyholes in the form of a flying insect.
full_text:
In the mountains of Albania, where the Ottoman-era stone houses cluster on the hillsides and the old traditions of hospitality and blood feuds survive into the modern age, there is a creature that mothers fear more than any enemy. She is the Shtriga, a vampire witch who feeds on the life force of infants and children.

The Shtriga appears as an ordinary woman. She might be a neighbor, a relative, a stranger passing through the village. There is nothing distinctive about her appearance. She could be young or old, beautiful or plain. That is what makes her so dangerous.

At night, while the family sleeps, the Shtriga enters the home. She can pass through locked doors, sealed windows, and barred gates. She approaches the cradle of the youngest child. She bends over the sleeping infant and begins to draw out its life force through its mouth. The infant does not wake. It makes a small sound, a whimper, and then grows still.

The child who survives a Shtriga's feeding grows pale, weak, and sickly. Over time, if the Shtriga returns night after night, the child wastes away and dies. The family does not understand what is happening. They think it is disease. They think it is bad luck. But the old grandmother knows. She has seen this before.

The only person who can save a child from the Shtriga is the Shtriga herself. If she is caught and forced to spit into the child's mouth, the life force returns. But catching a Shtriga is nearly impossible. She is vulnerable only while feeding, and if disturbed, she transforms into a flying insect — a moth or a fly — and escapes through the smallest crack.

The legend of the Shtriga dates back to ancient Illyrian times, predating the arrival of Christianity in the Balkans. The word shtriga itself comes from the Latin striga, meaning witch or evil spirit. The same root gives us the Romanian strigoi and the Italian strega.

Protection against the Shtriga includes consecrated iron, garlic hung above the cradle, and a cross made of ash wood over the door. But the most powerful protection is knowledge: if you know that the Shtriga exists, you will recognize her feeding signs in time.

The Shtriga embodies the deepest fear of every parent: the helplessness of watching a child fade without understanding why. In a region where infant mortality was once devastatingly high, the Shtriga gave a name to the inexplicable tragedy of a child's death. She is the face of grief, the explanation for the unexplainable, the enemy that every mother would fight if only she could see her.
moral: Fear of losing a child is the oldest and most powerful fear of all.

title: The Zana: Benevolent Fairies of the Albanian Mountains
summary: Beautiful mountain fairies protect lost travelers and bless newborns with gifts of beauty and talent.
full_text:
In the Accursed Mountains of northern Albania, where the peaks are so steep that the sun reaches the valley floor for only a few hours each day, there exists a kind of spirit that is the opposite of the Shtriga. She is the Zana, a fairy of the mountains, and she is one of the most beloved figures in Albanian folklore.

The Zana are female spirits of extraordinary beauty. They live in the forests and high pastures of the Albanian Alps, far from human settlements. Their skin is pale as limestone, their hair is long and dark, and their eyes flash like lightning in a summer storm. They dress in white, woven from the mist of the mountain valleys.

Unlike the witches and demons of other traditions, the Zana are benevolent. They are the protectors of lost children and injured travelers. A hiker who loses the path in the Accursed Mountains might suddenly feel a warm breeze, see a flicker of light, and find the trail again. This is the Zana, guiding them home.

The Zana are particularly associated with childbirth. When a baby is born in the mountains of Albania, the family will leave offerings of honey, bread, and wine at the edge of the forest. The Zana come at night and bless the newborn: one grants beauty, one grants talent in dance, one grants luck, one grants wisdom. A child blessed by all the Zana will grow up to be extraordinary.

But the Zana demand respect. A man who boasts of his strength or beauty, a woman who is cruel to others, a traveler who disrespects the mountain — these will find the Zana's mood has changed. The Zana can curse as easily as they can bless. A disrespectful person may find their good looks fade, their talents vanish, or their luck turn.

The Zana are also warriors. When the Accursed Mountains are threatened — by invaders, by evil spirits, by the dark witch Muma Pădurii — the Zana pick up their weapons and fight. They are the defenders of the Albanian highlands, the guardians of a way of life that has persisted for thousands of years.

The Zana represent the best of Albanian values: hospitality, protection of the weak, respect for nature, and the courage to fight for what is right. In a land that has been invaded by Romans, Byzantines, Ottomans, and communists, the Zana are the eternal defenders of Albanian identity. As long as the mountains stand, the Zana will be there, watching, protecting, and blessing the newborn children of Albania.
moral: Kindness and respect attract blessings; arrogance drives them away.

title: Guri Kunna: The Sea Witch of Hitra
summary: A dangerous sea witch lures fishermen to their doom along the Hitra and Fosen coast.
full_text:
Along the wild coast of Trøndelag, where the Norwegian Sea pounds against the skerries of Hitra and Fosen, fishermen have told the same story for generations. It is the story of Guri Kunna, the sea witch who waits among the rocks.

Guri Kunna was not always a creature of the sea. Some say she was a young woman from a fishing village on Hitra, beautiful beyond measure, who fell in love with a sailor. She waited for him on the cliffs, watching the horizon, day after day, but he never returned. Her heart turned to salt, her longing turned to rage, and she cursed the sea that had taken her love. The sea answered her curse by making her part of it forever.

Others say she was always a supernatural being — a sjøheks (sea witch) born of the cold ocean and the jagged rocks, as old as the first fishing boat that sailed the Trøndelag coast.

The fishermen know her by her song. On calm summer evenings, when the sea is glass and the sun hangs low over the horizon, a voice rises from the rocks. It is the most beautiful sound a man can hear — sweet and clear, like a mother's lullaby mixed with a lover's whisper. The song speaks of warm hearths and soft beds, of safety and comfort, of everything a tired fisherman longs for after a long day at sea.

And the fishermen steer toward the song.

Guri Kunna sits on a skerry just beneath the waterline, her golden hair spread around her like a net, combing it with a comb that gleams like a star. She is beautiful beyond words — her skin pale as sea foam, her eyes the color of deep water, her lips red as coral. She smiles and beckons.

The fisherman who sees her forgets everything — his boat, his catch, his family waiting at home. He sees only her. He steers closer, closer, until the hull grinds against the hidden rock. The boat shudders. Water pours through the cracked planks. And Guri Kunna's song turns to laughter as the sea swallows another victim.

But not all fishermen fall prey to Guri Kunna. Those who respect the sea, who never boast of their catch, who leave a small offering of food or a coin at certain rocks before setting out — these are spared. Some say she guides them to the best fishing grounds instead.

The oldest fishermen on Hitra still remember the tradition. Before the first fishing trip of the season, you row to a certain skerry shaped like a woman's face, and you leave a piece of flatbread or a silver coin. You do not speak Guri Kunna's name at sea. You do not speak it at all unless you mean to call her. And if you hear singing on the water at twilight, you row in the opposite direction, no matter how beautiful the song sounds.

Guri Kunna is the sea itself — beautiful, dangerous, and utterly without mercy for those who do not show her respect. She has claimed more fishermen than any storm, and she will claim many more, as long as the waves break against the rocks of Hitra and the wind carries her song across the water.
moral: The sea is beautiful but deadly; respect is the only protection.

title: The Ghostly Monks of Trondheimsfjorden
summary: Three monasteries in the Trondheimsfjord left behind more than ruins — their ghostly monks still walk the shores.
full_text:
The Trondheimsfjord is one of the great fjords of Norway, a deep blue waterway that reaches from the Norwegian Sea into the heart of Trøndelag. Along its shores and on its islands, three medieval monasteries once stood — Nidarholm on Munkholmen, Tautra on Frosta, and the great Nidaros Cathedral in Trondheim itself. All three were destroyed or taken during the Reformation of 1537, when Denmark-Norway broke with the Catholic Church. But the monks did not leave. Their spirits remain, and they are still seen to this day.

The first ghost is the Munken i Nidarosdomen — the Monk of Nidaros Cathedral. The cathedral was the most important church in medieval Norway, the coronation church of kings, the seat of the Archbishop of Nidaros, and the resting place of Saint Olav. When the Reformation came, the cathedral was stripped of its Catholic treasures, its altars were torn down, and its monks were driven out. But one monk refused to leave. He was killed at the high altar, and now his ghost walks the cathedral at night.

The Nidaros-Munken appears as a tall figure in a black Benedictine habit, his face hidden in the shadow of his hood. He moves silently through the octagonal choir, sometimes kneeling at the high altar in prayer. On some nights, he is headless, carrying his own head in his hands. He is most often seen during the Christmas season and on the anniversary of the Reformation. Those who have seen him say he leaves a cold that no fire can warm, and that the smell of incense follows him.

The second ghost is the Tautramunken — the Monk of Tautra. Tautra is a small island in the Trondheimsfjord, connected to Frosta by a causeway. Its Cistercian monastery was founded in 1207 and was one of the most peaceful and beautiful religious houses in Norway. White-robed Cistercian monks lived there in silence and prayer for over three centuries until the Reformation closed the monastery in 1532.

Today, only ruins remain. But on misty evenings, local people see a white figure walking among the stones. He is a Cistercian monk in his white habit, kneeling where the church once stood, praying silently. Sometimes his voice is heard chanting Latin hymns in the fog. The Tautramunken is a gentle ghost — he harms no one and seems unaware of the living. He is simply continuing the prayers he began eight hundred years ago.

The third ghost is the Munkholmsånden — the Spirit of Munkholmen. Munkholmen (originally Nidarholm) is a small island in the fjord just off Trondheim. Its Benedictine monastery was founded in the 1100s and was destroyed by cannon fire from Danish warships in 1537, on the direct orders of General Johan Rantzau. The island later became a fortress, a prison, and an execution ground. Its history is written in blood.

The Munkholmsånden is darker than the other two. He is a tall, gaunt figure in a torn black habit, sometimes carrying a lantern, sometimes just a shadow in the mist. He walks the shores of the island at night, looking out across the fjord. Some say he is the ghost of the last abbot, killed when the monastery was sacked. Others say he is the spirit of a prisoner executed on the island. All agree that his appearance is an omen of death.

Each of these three ghosts is bound to the Trondheimsfjord by the event that destroyed their monastery. The fjord connects them — three ruined monasteries, three restless spirits, three prayers that never ended. On winter nights, when the wind is right, some say you can hear all three chanting together across the dark water, a ghostly chorus that rises from the ruins and fades into the mist.
moral: Violent endings leave restless spirits; memory and tradition outlast institutions.

title: Sir Olof and the Elves
summary: A young knight riding to his wedding encounters the elf queen in the forest. She offers him her love, but he refuses — and her curse leads to tragedy on his wedding day.
full_text:
Sir Olof rode out before the dawn, his horse's hooves silent on the mossy path through the deep forest of Tiveden. He was dressed in his finest clothes, for today he was to marry his beloved, the fairest maiden in all of Sweden. The sun was just beginning to filter through the ancient oaks, and the mist rose from the forest floor like smoke from hidden fires.

As he rounded a bend in the path, Sir Olof reined in his horse. Before him, in a clearing where the morning light fell like gold through the trees, a group of elven maidens were dancing. They moved in a circle, their feet barely touching the dew-covered grass, their gowns shimmering with colors that seemed to shift between green and silver. In the center of the circle stood the Elf Queen herself, more beautiful than any woman Sir Olof had ever seen.

"Ride not so fast, Sir Olof," the Elf Queen called out, her voice like wind through harp strings. "Dance with me instead. Come, join our circle, and you shall know joys beyond mortal dreams."

Sir Olof felt the pull of her words like a current drawing him into deep water. But he remembered his bride waiting at the church. "I cannot," he said. "I ride to my wedding today. My bride awaits me."

The Elf Queen's eyes turned cold. "You refuse me? You choose a mortal woman over the Queen of Elves?"

"I must," Sir Olof said, and urged his horse forward.

But as he passed the elven circle, the Elf Queen reached out and laid her hand upon his heart. He felt a chill spread through his chest, cold as the deepest winter stream. "Then go to your wedding, Sir Olof, and dance with your bride. But know that the dance you have refused me will be your last."

Sir Olof rode on, but the chill in his heart did not fade. By the time he reached the church, his face was pale as birch bark, his hands trembled, and a strange fever burned in his eyes.

His bride saw his face and cried out. "Olof! What has happened?"

But Sir Olof could not answer. The Elf Queen's curse was upon him. As the priest began the ceremony, Sir Olof fell from his horse, dead before he touched the ground.

His bride looked down at his body, and something broke inside her. Without a word, she walked to the church door, collapsed, and joined him in death.

They were buried together in the churchyard of Tiveden, side by side. But on moonlit nights, when the mist rises from the forest floor, the people of the village say you can still see Sir Olof riding through the trees — and if you listen closely, you can hear the Elf Queen's laughter on the wind. The medieval ballad of Sir Olof and the Elves (Swedish: 'Herr Olof och Älvorna') is one of Scandinavia's most famous supernatural ballads, collected in Sweden in the 16th century and recorded in Denmark by Anders Sørensen Vedel in 1591. It belongs to the genre of medieval Scandinavian ballads known as 'kämpavisor' (heroic ballads) in Sweden, and its haunting melody and tragic story have been passed down through countless oral versions across Scandinavia.
moral: The supernatural world demands respect; refusing it can carry a cost greater than acceptance.
source: Medieval Scandinavian ballad tradition, collected in Sweden during the 16th-17th centuries.

title: The Tomten's Christmas Vigil
summary: In the deep of a winter night, the Tomten — the ancient guardian spirit of the farm — makes his silent rounds while the household sleeps, watching over all creatures and reflecting on the mysteries of life.
full_text:
The snow lay deep over the land, drifted high against the walls of the farmhouse and smoothing the contours of the frozen fields into a single white expanse. The winter moon hung cold and bright over the forest, casting long blue shadows across the yard. Inside the farmhouse, the family slept — the farmer and his wife, their children snug under heavy quilts, the fire banked low in the hearth.

But someone was awake.

From the hayloft above the stable, a small figure emerged. He was no taller than a child of seven, with a long white beard that reached his chest and a face as wrinkled and wise as the bark of an ancient oak. He wore gray wool clothes, worn smooth by centuries of use, and a red pointed cap that drooped slightly to one side. The Tomten moved silently across the loft, his bare feet making no sound on the wooden boards.

The Tomten was old — older than the oldest person in the village remembered, older than the farm itself, older perhaps than the land. He had been here since the first tree was felled, since the first stone was laid. Some said he was the spirit of the first person who had cleared this land, bound to the farm forever. The Tomten himself had long forgotten. He only knew his duty.

He descended the ladder into the stable, where the horses stood in their stalls, breath steaming in the cold air. The Tomten moved among them, checking each one, his weathered hands stroking their flanks. He whispered to them in a language older than Swedish, and the horses nickered softly in reply. The cow lowed a greeting. The sheep stirred in their pen. The Tomten was their caretaker, their protector, the unseen guardian who ensured the animals thrived and the farm prospered.

In the chicken coop, the hens slept in a tight cluster. The Tomten counted them, noted the new cracks in the wall that would need mending, and made a mental note to remind the farmer — though how he would do this was his own secret.

He made his way back through the snow to the farmhouse. He did not feel the cold. He had not felt warmth or cold in centuries. He slipped through the door, invisible as the draft, and stood in the darkened kitchen. The remnants of the family's Christmas porridge sat in a bowl on the table — a gift left for him, as tradition demanded. The Tomten smiled, a rare expression that creased his weathered face. He dipped his finger in the porridge and tasted the butter melting at its center. Good. They remembered.

He climbed the stairs and looked in on the sleeping children. Their faces were peaceful in the moonlight, their breath soft and regular. The Tomten stood for a long moment, watching them. They would grow, he knew. They would leave this farm, have children of their own, and some of them would return. He had watched this pattern repeat for so many generations that he had lost count.

As the first gray light of dawn touched the eastern horizon, the Tomten returned to his hayloft. The rooster would crow soon. The farmer would wake. The daily rhythm of the farm would begin again. The Tomten settled into his corner, pulled his cap over his eyes, and listened to the sounds of the world waking around him. Another night had passed. The farm was safe. His duty was done.

This story is adapted from Viktor Rydberg's beloved 1881 poem 'Tomten,' one of Sweden's most famous literary works. Rydberg's poem transformed the traditional farm tomte from a sometimes capricious household spirit into a contemplative, almost melancholic figure — alone in the universe, keeper of ancient traditions. The poem was illustrated by Jenny Nyström, whose images of the white-bearded tomte in his red cap became the definitive visual representation of the Swedish jultomte (Christmas gnome) and influenced the modern image of Santa Claus worldwide.
moral: The truest guardians are unseen; they serve not for reward but because the duty itself is the reward.
source: Viktor Rydberg, Tomten (1881). Adapted from Swedish folklore traditions of the tomte.

title: The Lame Dog
summary: A royal couple longing for a child trust a mysterious old woman who gives them a son with a terrible prohibition — if he ever sees the face of the one who loves him most, he will vanish like morning mist.
full_text:
There was once a king and queen who had everything in the world except the one thing they wanted most: a child. Year after year, the queen prayed and the king offered rewards, but no child was born. The kingdom grew quiet. The laughter of children was never heard in the palace halls.

One winter evening, as snow fell softly on the palace roof, an old woman appeared at the gate. She was bent and wrinkled, leaning on a staff, and at her side limped a small dog with one leg held up from the ground. The guards would have turned her away, but the queen saw her from the window and ordered her brought inside.

"I have traveled far," the old woman said, "and my dog is weary. May we rest by your fire?"

The queen welcomed her. As they sat together, the old woman looked at the queen with eyes that seemed to see through to her very soul. "You are sad, my queen. I can see it in your face. Tell me what troubles you."

The queen, who had told her sorrow to no one outside her closest ladies, found herself speaking freely. She told the old woman about her longing for a child, the years of disappointment, the growing fear that the kingdom would have no heir.

The old woman listened without interruption. When the queen finished, she was silent for a long moment. Then she said: "I can give you what you desire. But there is a price."

"Anything," the queen said. "Anything at all."

"The price is this," the old woman said. "Your child will be the most beautiful child ever born. He will be strong and clever and loved by all who meet him. But if he ever sees the face of the one who loves him more than life itself, he will vanish like morning mist."

The queen did not understand. "Who loves him more than life itself?" she asked.

"You will know when the time comes," the old woman said. She rose, called her lame dog, and walked out into the snow. The queen never saw her again.

Nine months later, the queen gave birth to a son, exactly as the old woman had promised. He was the most beautiful child ever born, with eyes like summer sky and hair like spun gold. The king and queen named him Erik, and the kingdom rejoiced.

Erik grew tall and strong, clever and kind. Everyone who met him loved him. But the queen remembered the old woman's words and lived in dread of the day they would come true.

On Erik's twentieth birthday, a great celebration was held. Princes and princesses came from every kingdom in the North. But among the guests was a young woman of such extraordinary beauty that everyone fell silent when she entered the hall. Her name was Signe, and she and Erik fell in love at first sight.

They were married within the month, and their happiness was complete. But the queen watched with growing fear. She saw the way Erik looked at Signe, the way Signe looked at Erik. She knew, with a mother's certainty, who the old woman had meant.

One night, the queen could bear it no longer. She went to Erik's chamber and told him everything — the old woman, the lame dog, the prophecy. "You must never look upon Signe's face," she said. "If you do, you will vanish."

Erik laughed. "Mother, that was twenty years ago. An old woman's tale. Surely you don't believe it still?"

"I believe it," the queen said. "I have believed it every day of your life."

Erik saw the fear in his mother's eyes and agreed to be careful. But that night, as he lay beside his sleeping wife, he could not stop thinking about the prophecy. He turned to look at her, just for a moment, to see her face in the moonlight.

The moment his eyes fell upon her, he felt himself dissolving, like morning mist before the sun. He tried to cry out, but his voice was gone. He tried to reach for her, but his hands were nothing. And then he was gone entirely, leaving only the impression of his body on the sheets.

Signe woke to an empty bed. She found only a single silver coin where Erik had lain. And she understood, with a grief so deep it had no bottom, that the old woman's prophecy had claimed her husband.

The story of the Lame Dog is one of Sweden's most beloved fairy tales, collected in the 19th century by folklore scholars. It belongs to the international cycle of tales about supernatural prohibitions and their consequences, with parallels to the Greek myth of Cupid and Psyche and the Norwegian tale of East of the Sun and West of the Moon.
moral: Some truths are heavier than ignorance; knowing the danger does not always protect us from it.
source: Swedish fairy tale tradition. Collected in the 19th century. Related to the ATU 425 cycle.

title: Knös
summary: A boy born from an egg grows to giant size and becomes the strongest man at the king's court, defeating a fearsome sea troll to rescue three princesses and win the youngest's hand. This classic Swedish hero-tale draws on the Thor-like motif of the giant-slaying goblet found in the _Hymiskvida_ of the Poetic Edda.
full_text:
KNOeS
        II
moral: True strength is matched by courage and a gentle heart.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.I: Knös. Source: _Tecknigar og Toner ur skanska allmogenslif_, Lund, 1889, p.14 (from Gudmundstorp, Froste Harad). The hero-giant parallels Thor of Norse myth.

title: Lasse, My Thrall!
summary: A ruined nobleman discovers a magic scrap of paper that summons a tireless servant named Lasse, who builds him a castle and wins him a princess — but losing the paper costs him everything until he learns wisdom. This popular Swedish tale is a distinctive Nordic variant of the Aladdin cycle, set down in the dialect of Nerike.
full_text:
LASSE, MY THRALL!
       III
moral: Wisdom comes from adversity; treat those who serve you with respect.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.II: Lasse, My Thrall! Source: Djurklau, _Sagor och Aefventyr pa Svenska Landsmal_, Stockholm, 1883 (Nerike dialect). A Swedish variant of the Aladdin folktale cycle with a uniquely Scandinavian magical-servant motif.

title: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund
summary: A giant named Finn builds Lund Cathedral in exchange for Saint Lawrence's eyes, but the saint overhears Finn's name in a lullaby and Finn turns to stone in the crypt. This world-famous master-builder legend is connected to several European cathedrals; Finn's petrified form can still be seen grasping a pillar in the crypt of Lund Cathedral.
full_text:
FINN, THE GIANT, AND THE MINSTER OF LUND
        IV
moral: Knowledge is power; a name held in secret can overcome even the strongest foe.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.III: Finn, the Giant, and the Minster of Lund. Retold by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund from variants in his collection. Connected to the world-famous giant master-builder legend, also associated with Nidaros Cathedral in Trondheim.

title: The Skalunda Giant
summary: A giant who fled Skalunda mountain because of church bells meets shipwrecked sailors from his homeland, tests their blood with a red-hot iron bar, and sends a magical girdle home that uproots an oak tree.
full_text:
THE SKALUNDA GIANT
         V
moral: Even the mightiest feel nostalgia for their homeland and test the mettle of those who remain.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.IV: The Skalunda Giant. Source: Hofberg, _Svenska Folksagner_, Stockholm, 1882, p.98. Related to the Norwegian mountain giant of Mesingeberg (Asbjørnsen).

title: Yuletide Specters
summary: A woman riding to Christmas mass encounters witch-trolls at a bridge and finds the church filled with headless specters and a headless priest, barely escaping with scraps of her mantle scattered on every grave. This ghostly Christmas legend is common throughout Scandinavia.
full_text:
YULETIDE SPECTERS
        VI
moral: Faith and the sign of the cross protect even in the most haunted of places.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.V: Yuletide Specters. From an mss. communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. The tale of the weird service of the dead on Christmas night is common throughout Scandinavia.

title: Silverwhite and Lillwacker
summary: Two faithful brothers journey to rescue their sister from a sea troll who killed their father, overcoming enchanted forests, talking animals, and magical trials through unbreakable brotherly loyalty. The tale draws on an ancient Indo-Germanic cycle of faithful brothers.
full_text:
SILVERWHITE AND LILLWACKER
       VII
moral: Brotherly loyalty and courage can overcome any enchantment or obstacle.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.VI: Silverwhite and Lillwacker. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, _Svenska Folkasagor och Aefventyr_, Stockholm, 1848, p.58 (from Värmland). From an ancient Indo-Germanic source cycle of faithful brothers.

title: Stompe Pilt
summary: A giant named Stompe Pilt terrorizes a parish until a brave little shepherd boy challenges him with a sling and a bag of tricks, proving that size is no match for wit. The tale is an entertaining parody of David and Goliath.
full_text:
STOMPE PILT
      VIII
moral: Brains and courage can overcome even the greatest of physical strength.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.VII: Stompe Pilt. Source: Hofberg, p.10. An entertaining Scandinavian parody of the David and Goliath story.

title: The Girl and the Snake
summary: A girl lost in the forest meets a talking snake who offers to guide her home in exchange for a promise — a promise that leads to an enchanted romance beneath the earth. This tale from Södermanland shows distinctive Scandinavian features.
full_text:
THE GIRL AND THE SNAKE
        IX
moral: Keep your promises, for even the lowliest creature may hold a royal secret.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.VIII: The Girl and the Snake. From Södermanland. Source: mss. collection of the metallurgist Gustav Erikson, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Shows distinctive Scandinavian features.

title: Faithful and Unfaithful
summary: Two brothers — one faithful, one unfaithful — set out into the world; the faithful brother wins a princess through kindness while the unfaithful one schemes and falls to ruin. This tale is a distant offshoot of the Tristan legend cycle.
full_text:
FAITHFUL AND UNFAITHFUL
         X
moral: Faithfulness and kindness are always rewarded; treachery brings its own punishment.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.IX: Faithful and Unfaithful. From the Hyltén-Cavallius mss. collection. A distant offshoot of the Tristan legend cycle involving a golden-haired maiden and water spirits.

title: Starkad and Bale
summary: The legendary hero Starkad — raised by Odin himself and the greatest warrior of the North — teams up with the sly trickster Bale to exact revenge through feats of impossible strength and clever deception against a treacherous king.
full_text:
STARKAD AND BALE
        XI
moral: Even the greatest hero needs a clever companion to overcome treachery.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.X: Starkad and Bale. Source: Hofberg, p.181 (from Medelpad, after ancient traditional sources). Features Starkad, the most famous hero of Northern legend, who was raised by Odin himself.

title: The Werewolf
summary: A prince cursed to become a werewolf roams the wilderness until a princess's self-sacrificing love and courage break the spell. Rooted in the ancient Teutonic belief in werewolves, this tale from Uppland emphasizes redemption through love.
full_text:
THE WEREWOLF
       XII
moral: Self-sacrificing love has the power to break even the darkest curse.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XI: The Werewolf. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.312 (from Uppland). Based on the Teutonic belief in humans who could transform into wolves; redemption through a maiden's self-sacrificing love.

title: First Born, First Wed
summary: A king's son and a queen's daughter, promised to each other at birth, must overcome separation, enchantment, and a wicked stepmother before they can fulfill their destiny. This is a purely Swedish treatment of the redemption motif.
full_text:
FIRST BORN, FIRST WED
      XIII
moral: True love promised at birth will find its way despite all obstacles.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XII: First Born, First Wed. From the mss. collection of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A purely Swedish treatment of the redemption-motive.

title: The Mount of the Golden Queen
summary: A poor cattle-tending lad discovers a hidden mountain hall where a golden queen holds court, and his kindness toward forest creatures earns him treasure and a royal bride. This distinctly visionary tale from Södermanland sends its hero on a world-wide quest.
full_text:
THE MOUNT OF THE GOLDEN QUEEN
        XV
moral: Kindness to all creatures, no matter how small, opens doors to hidden wonders.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XIV: The Mount of the Golden Queen. From Södermanland. Source: mss. collection of the metallurgist Gustav Erikson, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A distinctly visionary fairy-tale.

title: Old Hopgiant
summary: A poor peasant shares his meager food with a strange old giant who rewards him with a magic mill that grinds anything — but the greedy neighbor learns that kindness cannot be stolen. Old Hopgiant is a genuine folk-tale figure who aids the poor against oppression.
full_text:
OLD HOPGIANT
       XVI
moral: Generosity to the strange and needy brings rewards that greed can never steal.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XV: Old Hopgiant. Source: Bondeson, _Svenska Folksagor_, Stockholm, 1882, p.41 (from Dalsland). A genuine folk-tale figure — a giant helper who aids the poor against their oppressors.

title: The Princess and the Glass Mountain
summary: A princess sits atop a treacherous glass mountain, and the man who can ride to the top wins her hand — a poor boy with a magic horse succeeds where knights fail. The princess is a relative of Brunhilde of Germanic heroic legend, and the tale is very popular across the North.
full_text:
THE PRINCESS AND THE GLASS MOUNTAIN
      XVII
moral: Humility and a pure heart can achieve what pride and arrogance cannot.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVI: The Princess and the Glass Mountain. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.390 (somewhat abridged). Very popular throughout the North; the princess is a relative of Brunhilde of heroic legend.

title: Queen Crane
summary: A poor shepherd boy named Proud is befriended by a crane who is actually an enchanted queen, and through a series of magical transformations, he wins her hand and a kingdom. This very popular Northern tale features a seemingly witless hero with an unusual transformation motive.
full_text:
QUEEN CRANE
     XVIII
moral: Loyalty and a pure heart see through enchanted disguises to find true royalty.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVII: Queen Crane. From the collection of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A very popular Northern fairy-tale with a witless hero and an unusual transformation motive.

title: Tales of the Trolls
summary: Three short tales of troll encounters: a peasant meets a troll at a spring, a girl is lured into a mountain by a handsome troll, and a farmer outwits a troll who steals his harvest. These tales reflect the Scandinavian belief in _bergtagning_ — being taken into the mountain by trolls.
full_text:
TALES OF THE TROLLS
       XIX
moral: Trolls may be powerful, but human wit and faith can overcome them every time.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVIII: Tales of the Trolls. Communicated from mss. belonging to Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Three folk-tales reflecting primitive faith and superstition; the first is also current in Norway. The later tales tell of women _bergtagen_ (taken into the mountain).

title: The Three Dogs
summary: A king falls under the spell of an evil witch-queen, and only his faithful queen and three magical dogs — with powers of strength, speed, and music — can break the enchantment. From Västergötland, this tale emphasizes the magic of music against trolls.
full_text:
THE THREE DOGS
       XXI
moral: The magic of loyalty and music can break even the strongest evil spell.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XX: The Three Dogs. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.195 (from Västergötland). Fairy tales hold a high opinion of music's power — the magic flute breaks the evil troll's spell, as in 'Faithful and Unfaithful'.

title: The Poor Devil
summary: When a peasant prays for his cow's protection, God and the Devil argue over who truly controls fate — leading to a wager that reveals the Devil's limitations and God's grace. This tale from Småland is at once humorous and philosophical.
full_text:
THE POOR DEVIL
      XXII
moral: Even the Devil has limits; divine protection is stronger than evil schemes.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXI: The Poor Devil. Source: Bondeson, p.212 (from Småland). A humorous and philosophical tale showing the Devil attempting to rival God.

title: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be
summary: An etiological tale explaining why Småland is rocky and barren while Skåne is flat and fertile — because the Devil tried to create land but was foiled at every turn by God. This humorous origin legend was also used by Selma Lagerlöf in her writings.
full_text:
HOW SMALAND AND SCHONEN CAME TO BE
     XXIII
moral: God's creation is perfect; the Devil's imitations always fall short.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXII: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. An etiological legend — the Danes and Swedes would say the Devil created these regions, a motif also used by Selma Lagerlöf.

title: The Evil One and Kitta Grau
summary: The Devil meets Kitta Grau, a sharp-tongued old woman, and she out-argues, out-cheats, and out-schemes him at every turn — proving that a clever woman can best even the Prince of Darkness. This tale from Halland shows that a wicked woman outdoes the Devil himself.
full_text:
THE EVIL ONE AND KITTA GRAU
      XXIV
moral: A sharp tongue and quick wit can defeat even the Devil himself.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXIII: The Evil One and Kitta Grau. Source: Bondeson, p.206 (from Halland). Shows that it is child's play for a clever woman to accomplish what the Devil himself cannot.

title: The Lady of Pintorp
summary: A cruel noblewoman makes a pact with the Devil, and after a life of wickedness, her ghost returns to haunt Pintorp Castle in a spectral carriage on stormy nights. The lady may have been a historical figure — the wife of the royal counselor Erik Gyllenstjerna.
full_text:
THE LADY OF PINTORP
       XXV
moral: Evil deeds in life bind the soul beyond death; no wealth can buy redemption from a wicked pact.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXIV: The Lady of Pintorp. Source: Hofberg, p.157. The devil appears in grim Satanic majesty. The evil woman may be a historical figure, the wife of royal counselor Erik Gyllenstjerna.

title: The Specter in Fjelkinge
summary: An innocent man is wrongly executed, and his ghost appears to the true murderer night after night until justice is done and his soul can finally rest. The tale is founded on the ancient belief that innocent blood calls for atonement.
full_text:
THE SPECTER IN FJELKINGE
      XXVI
moral: Innocent blood cries out for justice, and no guilty party can escape its call.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXV: The Specter in Fjelkinge. Source: Hofberg, p.21. Founded on the ancient belief that innocent blood calls for atonement, and the murdered cannot rest until the deed is brought to light.

title: The Rooster, the Hand-Mill and the Swarm of Hornets
summary: A poor peasant trades a pig for three seemingly worthless gifts — a rooster, a hand-mill, and a hornets' nest — but each proves invaluable when he outwits a greedy innkeeper. The gold-laying rooster is a widely known international folktale motif.
full_text:
THE ROOSTER, THE HAND-MILL AND THE SWARM OF HORNETS
     XXVII
moral: The humblest gifts can become the greatest weapons in the hands of the clever.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVI: The Rooster, the Hand-Mill and the Swarm of Hornets. Mss. record by Stephens, from Värmland, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A poor peasant receives three splendid gifts. The gold-laying rooster is a widely known motif.

title: Torre Jeppe
summary: A mummified corpse named Torre Jeppe sits in a church night after night until a brave sexton discovers that the ghost cannot rest because of an ancient wrong that must be righted. A ghost-story founded on the belief that wrongdoing torments the guilty even after death.
full_text:
TORRE JEPPE
    XXVIII
moral: Even the dead cannot rest until old wrongs are made right.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVII: Torre Jeppe. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund after the mss. version of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens. A ghost-story founded on the belief that a wrong torments the doer even after death.

title: The Man Who Died on Holy Innocents' Day
summary: A wicked man named Kalle Kula dies on Holy Innocents' Day and finds himself caught between Heaven and Hell, bargaining for his soul with a blend of humor and desperation. Part fairy-tale, part legend, this tale has a Danish variant and displays the innocently malicious humor of Gottfried Keller.
full_text:
"No sooner had he spoken the words than he was lying in the most
    magnificent room he had ever seen"

    "Then Silverwhite drew his sword with a great sweep, and rushed
    upon the sea-troll"

    "The pike rose to the surface with the golden keys in his mouth"

    "So heartfelt was her happiness that she forgot everything else
    in the world"

    "A shrine adorned with gold and precious stones appeared"

    "The lion turned into a handsome young prince"

    "The rich man had to go along hanging to him like a hawser"

    "He saw a girl sitting in the mountain hall, weaving a web of
    gold"




THE SWEDISH FAIRY BOOK




Swedish Fairy Book




I

KNOeS


Once upon a time there was a poor widow, who found an egg under a pile
of brush as she was gathering kindlings in the forest. She took it and
placed it under a goose, and when the goose had hatched it, a little
boy slipped out of the shell. The widow had him baptized Knoes, and
such a lad was a rarity; for when no more than five years old he was
grown, and taller than the tallest man. And he ate in proportion, for
he would swallow a whole batch of bread at a single sitting, and at
last the poor widow had to go to the commissioners for the relief of
the poor in order to get food for him. But the town authorities said
she must apprentice the boy at a trade, for he was big enough and
strong enough to earn his own keep.

So Knoes was apprenticed to a smith for three years. For his pay he
asked a suit of clothes and a sword each year: a sword of five
hundredweights the first year, one of ten hundredweights the second
year, and one of fifteen hundredweights the third year. But after he
had been in the smithy only a few days, the smith was glad to give him
all three suits and all three swords at once; for he smashed all his
iron and steel to bits.

Knoes received his suits and swords, went to a knight's estate, and
hired himself out as a serving-man. Once he was told to go to the
forest to gather firewood with the rest of the men, but sat at the
table eating long after the others had driven off and when he had at
last satisfied his hunger and was ready to start, he saw the two young
oxen he was to drive waiting for him. But he let them stand and went
into the forest, seized the two largest trees growing there, tore them
out by the roots, took one tree under each arm, and carried them back
to the estate. And he got there long before the rest, for they had to
chop down the trees, saw them up and load them on the carts.

On the following day Knoes had to thresh. First he hunted up the
largest stone he could find, and rolled it around on the grain, so
that all the corn was loosened from the ears. Then he had to separate
the grain from the chaff. So he made a hole in each side of the roof
of the barn, and stood outside the barn and blew, and the chaff and
straw flew out into the yard, and the corn remained lying in a heap on
the floor. His master happened to come along, laid a ladder against
the
moral: Death does not erase a life of wickedness, but humor and desperation may soften even the hardest judgment.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVIII: The Man Who Died on Holy Innocents' Day. Communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Has a Danish variant. Its innocently malicious humor is worthy of Gottfried Keller.

title: The Changeling
summary: A mother suspects her child has been replaced by a troll changeling and uses clever tricks — including brewing beer in an eggshell — to force the troll mother to return her real baby.
full_text:
In a quiet farming valley in Värmland, a young mother named Kerstin sat by the cradle of her newborn son, little Nils. He had been a healthy, happy baby — until one night when the house was filled with an unnatural wind, the candles flickered blue, and the cattle lowed in terror. The next morning, Nils was different. His eyes, once bright and blue, had turned a muddy gray. His skin had taken on a yellowish pallor. He no longer smiled or cooed. Instead, he stared at the ceiling with a knowing, ancient gaze, and when he cried — which was often — it was a sound like the scraping of stones. He had an enormous appetite but never grew. He would break the furniture, pull the cat's tail until it yowled, and laugh when anyone stumbled.

Kerstin's mother, an old wise woman named Sigrid, came to visit. She looked at the child for a long time, then took Kerstin aside. "That is not your son," she said. "That is a bortbyting — a changeling. The trolls have taken your little Nils into the mountain and left one of their own in his place."

Kerstin wept. "How can I get him back?" she asked.

"You must make the changeling show its true nature," said Sigrid. "Trolls are old, no matter how young they look. They cannot hide their age when faced with something impossible. Listen carefully."

Sigrid instructed Kerstin to brew beer in an eggshell. Not a pot of beer — a single eggshell. She was to set it over the fire and pretend to brew. Then she was to hide and watch.

Kerstin did as she was told. She took an empty eggshell, balanced it over the flames, and pretended to brew beer. Then she crept behind the woodpile and watched.

The changeling sat up in its cradle, its gray eyes narrowing. It watched her for a long time, then climbed out of the cradle, toddled to the fire, and peered at the eggshell. Its face twisted into an expression of ancient contempt.

"I am as old as the forests of Värmland," it said in a voice that was not a child's voice, but the voice of something old and bitter. "I have seen the mountains rise and the seas retreat. I have seen the first king crowned and the last pagan temple burned. And in all my years, I have never — never — seen anyone brew beer in an eggshell."

Kerstin leaped from her hiding place and grabbed a birch rod. "You have admitted it!" she cried. "You are a troll, not my son! If you do not return my little Nils, I will beat you until you howl!"

The changeling shrieked — a sound like a hundred bats taking flight — and vanished in a puff of sulfurous smoke. At that same moment, the front door opened, and in walked a woman tall as a pine tree, with moss in her hair and eyes like dark pools. She carried a bundle wrapped in birch bark. She set it down on the bed. "Take your brat back," she said. "He has been nothing but trouble. Cries all night. Won't eat troll food. Keeps asking for his mother."

She snatched up the changeling and was gone before Kerstin could thank her. Kerstin rushed to the bundle. Inside was little Nils, healthy and whole, his blue eyes blinking in the firelight. He smiled when he saw his mother and reached out his tiny arms.

From that day on, Kerstin always placed a piece of steel over the cradle at night and kept a bowl of porridge with butter on the doorstep for any passing troll who might be hungry. And little Nils grew up strong and clever, though his mother never told him the whole story.

Sometimes, when the wind howls around the cottage and the fire casts long shadows, the old women of Värmland still warn young mothers: "Never leave your child alone at night, and never trust a silent baby. For the bortbyting is patient, and the trolls are always watching."
moral: A mother's love and quick thinking can overcome even the trickery of trolls.

title: The Boy Who Ate with the Giant
summary: A clever little boy uses a series of tricks — a bag of porridge, a handful of bird feathers, and a stone — to convince a giant that he is stronger and hungrier than the giant himself.
full_text:
In the province of Småland, where the forests stretch dark and deep between the rocky fields, there once lived a poor farmer and his wife. They had many children, and the youngest was a boy so small that they called him Lill-Petter. But what Lill-Petter lacked in size, he made up for in cleverness.

One autumn, when the crops had failed and the family had nothing to eat, Lill-Petter said to his father, "Let me go into the forest. Perhaps I can find us a deer or a hare."

His father laughed. "You? You are so small a hare would mistake you for a tuft of grass! Stay home and help your mother."

But Lill-Petter did not stay home. He took a small bag, filled it with porridge, slung it over his shoulder, and walked into the dark forest.

He walked until the sun was high, then until it began to sink. And at last, he came to a clearing where a giant sat on a boulder the size of a cottage. The giant was eating a whole roasted boar, bones and all. He crunched the ribs like twigs and swallowed the hooves whole.

The giant noticed Lill-Petter and laughed — a sound like boulders rolling down a mountain. "Well, well! A little morsel! Have you come to be my dinner?"

"Not at all," said Lill-Petter, bowing politely. "I have come to challenge you."

"Challenge me?" The giant's laughter shook the leaves from the trees. "To what?"

"To an eating contest," said Lill-Petter. "Whoever can eat more shall be the winner."

The giant laughed even harder. "Little boy, I could eat a hundred of you and still be hungry! But I accept your challenge. What are the stakes?"

"If I win," said Lill-Petter, "you must give me your treasure — all the gold and silver you have gathered."

"And if I win?" asked the giant.

"Then you may eat me," said Lill-Petter with a smile.

They sat down across from each other. The giant produced a cauldron of porridge the size of a washtub. "We will take turns," said the giant. "I will go first."

He plunged his face into the cauldron and ate and ate until the porridge was half gone. Steam rose from his ears. His belly swelled.

"Your turn," he said, wiping his mouth with his sleeve.

Lill-Petter stepped forward. He had secretly cut a hole in the bottom of his bag, through which the porridge leaked onto the ground. He held the bag to his mouth and pretended to eat, while the porridge drained away. "Delicious!" he said. "But this is child's play. I am barely warmed up!"

The giant stared in disbelief as Lill-Petter "ate" the entire remaining half of the porridge.

"Now," said Lill-Petter, "let us have a squeezing contest!" He picked up a round stone from the ground and squeezed it until a few drops of water from a nearby puddle ran down his wrist. "See? I squeezed water from this stone! Let me see you do better."

The giant grabbed a massive boulder and squeezed with all his might. But of course, no water came from the dry rock. "Impossible!" roared the giant. "You cheated!"

"I did not," said Lill-Petter. "You are simply too weak. But I will give you one more chance. Let us have a throwing contest."

Lill-Petter reached into his pocket and pulled out a handful of bird feathers he had gathered. He threw them into the air, and they scattered across the sky. "There! I threw these feathers so high that they touched the clouds!"

The giant picked up a stone and hurled it with all his strength. It flew over the trees and disappeared. "I threw that one higher!" he insisted.

"Ah, but did it touch the clouds?" asked Lill-Petter. "No. Mine did. I win."

The giant hung his enormous head. He had never been beaten by anyone, let alone a tiny boy. "You are right," he said. "You are stronger and cleverer than I. My treasure is yours."

He led Lill-Petter into his cave, where piles of gold and silver coins glittered in the torchlight. Lill-Petter filled his bag as much as he could carry.

"Wait," said the giant. "I have one more thing to give you." He reached into a corner and pulled out a small wooden box. "This is a magic flute. When you play it, anyone who hears must dance. It may serve you well."

Lill-Petter thanked the giant and returned home. His family was overjoyed to see him, and when he emptied his bag of gold onto the table, they could scarcely believe their eyes. They never went hungry again.

And ever after, when anyone in Småland tells the story of Lill-Petter, they call him "Pojken som åt i kapp med jätten" — the boy who ate in a race with the giant — and they remember that cleverness is stronger than strength.

As for the giant, some say he learned humility that day. Others say he moved deeper into the forest, where he still tells the tale of the tiny boy who out-ate, out-squeezed, and out-threw him.
moral: Cleverness and wit can overcome even the greatest strength.

title: The Giant Vrål of Bohuslän
summary: The giant Vrål of Bohuslän forces a farmer to give up his daughter in marriage, and she serves him a bowl of porridge laced with 24 horseshoe nails — which he eats without noticing until the last one.
full_text:
On the rocky coast of Bohuslän, where the granite cliffs meet the gray North Sea, there once lived a giant named Vrål. He was not the tallest of giants, nor the strongest, but he was the greediest and the most stubborn. His home was a cave in the mountainside, filled with bones and broken tools and the treasures he had taken from passing ships.

Vrål had an eye for the human folk who lived in the valleys below. He watched them plant their fields and fish the waters, and he grew envious of their warm houses and laughing children. Most of all, he envied the farmer Björn, who had a daughter named Karin, whose hair was the color of the sun on the water and whose laugh was like the bells of the Gothenburg cathedral.

One day, Vrål came down from his mountain and stood before Björn's farm. His shadow covered the entire yard. The chickens scattered. The dog hid under the porch.

"Farmer!" boomed Vrål. "I have come for your daughter. She will be my bride. If you refuse, I will crush your house and eat your cattle and scatter your bones across the fjord."

Björn was a practical man. He looked at the giant, looked at his own small frame, and nodded. "She is yours," he said. "But you must treat her well."

Karin was not consulted. But Karin was no ordinary girl. She had a mind as sharp as a fishing hook and a temper that could sour milk. When her father told her what had happened, she smiled a thin, dangerous smile.

"Very well," she said. "I will marry the giant. But first, I will cook him a wedding feast to remember."

She went into the kitchen and lit the fire. She took the largest pot in the house and filled it with creamy porridge — the best oats, the richest milk, the sweetest butter. And while the porridge simmered, she took twenty-four horseshoe nails from the blacksmith's shed and hammered them into the porridge one by one, stirring them deep into the golden mass so that not a single nail showed.

Then she carried the pot to the giant's cave, walking slowly so as not to spill a drop.

"Husband!" she called sweetly. "I have brought you a wedding feast. Eat, and be strong."

Vrål's eyes lit up. He loved porridge more than anything in the world. He took the pot and plunged his face into it, eating with enormous, grunting gulps. He swallowed the first horseshoe nail without noticing. Then the second. Then the third through the twenty-third, each one sliding down his gullet like a pebble down a well.

But when he reached the bottom of the pot, his spoon hit the twenty-fourth nail. He picked it up, examined it, and frowned.

"Wife!" he roared. "There is a nail in the porridge! I could have been killed!"

Karin put her hands on her hips. "And so you would have been," she said, "had you not been too stupid to notice the first twenty-three. You have eaten twenty-three horseshoe nails, Vrål. They are sitting in your belly right now. If you ever harm me or my family, I will make sure you eat a hundred more. And this time, I will sharpen them first."

Vrål stared at her. He felt his stomach, where the nails were indeed rattling around. He had never been so frightened in his life. This tiny woman had fed him twenty-three nails, and he had not noticed a single one.

"You are more dangerous than any giant," he whispered. "Please, do not cook for me again."

From that day on, Vrål treated Karin with the utmost respect. He brought her gifts of silver and gold. He helped her father with the plowing. And whenever anyone asked him why he was so well-behaved, he would pat his stomach and say nothing.

As for Karin, she became known as the wisest woman in Bohuslän, and young girls would come from miles around to learn how to handle difficult husbands. Her secret was simple: "If a man is going to eat twenty-three nails without noticing, he deserves whatever he gets."
moral: A clever woman can outwit even the mightiest of giants.

title: When Mother Troll Took in the King's Washing
summary: Troll Mother and her son Drulle live in a forest cottage where she does laundry for travelers. When a king builds a nearby castle for his queen and newborn princess, the lady-in-waiting hires Troll Mother to wash the royal clothes cheaply so she can pocket the difference. Troll Mother becomes enchanted by the beautiful baby clothes and begins stealing them, using a troll rhyme her son recites during counting to conceal the theft. An orphan girl named Inga, who mends clothes at the castle, is blamed for the missing garments and sent away. She wanders to the trolls' cottage and is taken in, where Drulle becomes infatuated with her. Inga meets the queen's young page in the forest, who believes in her innocence and asks for her hand, but she refuses out of shame. When Troll Mother reveals the stolen baby clothes and her tail becomes loose, Inga realizes she is living with trolls and flees. She marries the page, while Drulle's misery causes him to forget the counting rhyme, leading to exposure of the theft. The trolls abandon their cottage, and years later return the remaining baby clothes. Inga and the page live happily, becoming the royal forester and his wife.
moral: Honesty and kindness prevail over greed and deception.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by Holger Lundbergh, Delacorte Press/S. Lawrence, 1973. Originally published in Swedish as 'När trollmor skötte kungens storbyk' (1914) by Elsa Beskow.

title: The Magician's Cape
summary: An evil magician lives in a mountain palace filled with frightened young girls he has stolen from their families. He forces them to dance and sing for him, and when one displeases him, he casts her into the forest to be devoured by bears and wolves. The magician possesses a black flying cape: when a maiden steps onto it, the cape turns into wings and he whisks her away. One day he discovers Alvida, a smith's beautiful daughter, combing her golden hair at a window. He tries to ensnare her with his cape, but she is clever and cautious. When a ram (summoned by the magician's whistle) charges at her, Alvida stumbles and tears the cape with the ram's horns. Feeling remorseful, she mends the tear using two thorns as a needle and a strand of her golden hair as thread. The magician then tricks her into stepping onto the cape, and she is carried away. As she cries for help, her golden hair catches on a tree branch and refuses to break. The magician is forced to release her. Enraged, he returns to his castle, but finds he cannot sleep—the golden thread sewn into his cape glows so brightly it fills every room with light. No matter how he tries to hide the cape, the radiance follows him. Desperate, he returns to Alvida's village and offers gifts, but she refuses to remove the thread, knowing trolls and magicians cannot enter Christian homes. Every good deed the magician performs dims the light slightly, and he is forced into a life of reluctant kindness. The golden thread represents the power of good deeds over evil ones.
moral: A good deed shines brighter than any magic, and even evil can be tempered by kindness.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by Holger Lundbergh, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollkarlens kappa' (1912) by Anna Wahlenberg, in the annual 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Barrel Bung
summary: Famine has struck the parish of Bolinge. All the cows have gone dry except for those belonging to the rich, elderly Lady Skinflint and her ugly daughter, who somehow have hundreds of cheeses and tubs of butter from only four cows. When Jerker, the eldest son of the poor farmer Jonson, discovers that his family's cow Star has stopped giving milk, he decides to investigate. He stays awake and catches the family tomte (house spirit), who tells him the answer lies in Lady Skinflint's storeroom. Jerker trades his Sunday clothes for food and offers himself as a farmhand at the manor. He breaks the lock on the storeroom and hides to observe. That night, he watches Lady Skinflint take out a large barrel bung (stopper), wedge it into a hole in the wall, and milk the udders of the villagers' cows that magically appear through the bung. Enraged, Jerker recites a rhyme that causes a cow's leg to kick out from the bung instead, beating the old woman and her daughter until they beg for mercy. Jerker forces them to hand over all the gold they earned from stealing milk and takes the magic bung. He returns the gold to the villagers, who share it equally, rewarding Jerker with a portion that allows him to buy a farm and marry his sweetheart. He destroys the bung in a fire so it can never harm anyone again.
moral: Greed and theft will be punished, and cleverness and courage prevail over injustice.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Tunntappen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg, later in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Seven Wishes
summary: Olle Niklasson is a thirteen-year-old boy who is exceptionally ugly, lazy, and stupid. His hair resembles a dried juniper bush, his cheeks like swollen fly agaric mushrooms, and he is so lazy he barely moves when he falls. Even the magpies laugh at him. One day in the forest, Olle sees a viper staring down a frog that is slowly crawling toward the snake's open mouth. Too stupid and lazy to intervene with a stick, he nonetheless shouts at the frog for being foolish. This breaks the snake's spell, and the frog escapes. The frog is revealed to be a fairy queen who had been punished for mocking a frog in danger — she was transformed into a frog for twenty-four hours. Grateful for her rescue, she grants Olle seven wishes. Olle uses his wishes foolishly — his first wish makes his bundle of sticks fly wildly through the village, destroying crops and frightening animals. He wastes wish after wish on trivial, selfish things. But after using up six wishes with nothing to show for it, he finally wishes, almost accidentally, that he could learn to read. The fairy queen grants this wish, but tells him that true wisdom comes only through his own hard work. Olle discovers that reading opens his mind. He becomes diligent, studious, and kind, eventually growing into a good and handsome man whom everyone loves. The story teaches that laziness and ignorance are the true sources of ugliness, while knowledge and hard work bring beauty and happiness.
moral: True happiness comes not from magical wishes but from self-improvement through reading and hard work.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'De sju önskningarna' (1907) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The King's Choice
summary: King Gustav must leave his country on a long journey and needs to choose one of his six councilors to govern in his absence — but each has a flaw: one is too bossy, another too young, one drinks too much, one is obsessed with money, another with his appearance, and the last is simply uninspiring. Riding through the forest, the king encounters a fairy who rises from the river mist. She transforms him into a woodcutter and places him in a cottage by the river. The fairy arranges for the six councilors' boat to be caught in a storm, forcing them to seek shelter at the woodcutter's cottage. Disguised as a poor woodcutter, the king invites them to dinner and observes their true natures when they believe no one of consequence is watching. Through this test, he discovers which councilor possesses genuine inner strength, humility, and wisdom. The story explores themes of leadership, the difference between appearance and reality, and the value of looking beneath the surface to find true merit.
moral: True leadership is revealed not by titles or appearances, but by character tested in humble circumstances.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Kungens val' by Anna Wahlenberg, included in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Four Big Trolls and Little Peter Pastureman
summary: When the old Troll King dies, four enormous trolls — each as large as a mountain — vie for the throne. Their vicious rivalry threatens all magical folk in the kingdom, as their power struggles cause chaos throughout the land. Little Peter, a brave and lighthearted shepherd boy (called Vill-Vallareman in Swedish) who works as a pastureman for the four trolls, finds himself caught in the middle of their conflict. Despite his small size, Peter possesses quick wits and a courageous heart. While the four giant trolls argue, scheme, and battle one another, Peter uses his intelligence to outmaneuver them. He blows on a birchbark horn, which plays a key role in the resolution of the trolls' dispute. Through his bravery and clever thinking, Peter prevents what would have been a tragic outcome for all the magical folk. The story, richly illustrated by John Bauer with iconic images of the trolls and the small shepherd boy, demonstrates that size and strength matter less than intelligence and courage. It is a charming blend of adventure and folklore that teaches the triumph of good over evil through wit rather than force.
moral: Intelligence and courage can overcome even the greatest physical strength.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om de fyra stortrollen och lille Vill-Vallareman' (1909) by Cyrus Granér, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Troll Ride
summary: Peder Lars, a farmer's son, is riding to town to buy a new jacket before courting the beautiful and wealthy Lisa that evening. He is confident she will accept his proposal. But on the way through the forest, he encounters a hideous troll-woman lying injured in a ditch. She begs him to collect resin from seven pine trees to heal her wounded leg, offering gold coins in return. Peder Lars is repulsed by her appearance and initially refuses, but the haunting cries of the troll-woman's mother calling for her drive him to collect the resin. He tosses it at her in irritation and rides on. The delay causes him to be late: while en route, his brother meets him with terrible news — the rich miller Jonas has also come to propose to Lisa. With only minutes to spare and miles to go, Peder Lars' horse suddenly gains supernatural speed. Unseen, the grateful troll-woman rides behind him, her magic urging the horse forward at breakneck speed through the dark forest. Though his jacket is torn and his hair is wild, Peder Lars arrives at Lisa's house at the very last moment. Lisa, seeing his determined and disheveled appearance, finds him handsome and rushes into his arms. Peder Lars wins her hand, and from that day forward, he is never late again — because the troll-woman's magic ensures he always arrives on time.
moral: Even a reluctant act of kindness can be rewarded in unexpected ways.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollritten' (1910) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Trolls and the Youngest Tomte
summary: In the world of Swedish tomtar (house gnomes), the youngest and most curious tomte listens with wonder as the older tomtar tell stories of their adventurous visits inside the trolls' mountain. The older tomtar warn of the dangers, but the youngest tomte's curiosity gets the better of him. He decides to venture into the trolls' mountain himself, despite the warnings. Inside the mountain, he discovers an open treasure chest guarded by two terrible trolls. The young tomte must use all his wits and courage to navigate the perilous troll realm and escape with his life. He narrowly avoids being captured and uses clever tricks to outsmart the trolls, who are large and powerful but not very intelligent. The story, beautifully illustrated by John Bauer (whose artwork for this tale is among his most celebrated), captures the tension between youthful curiosity and the wisdom of experience. The youngest tomte's adventure teaches respect for the dangers of the world while also celebrating the bravery required to explore the unknown. He eventually escapes with barely his life intact, learning a valuable lesson about the importance of heeding the warnings of elders.
moral: Curiosity can lead to dangerous adventures, and it is wise to listen to the counsel of those with more experience.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollen och tomtepojken' (1909) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Ring
summary: A young prince rides out in the moonlight and finds a tiny ring by the shore — a delicate band with blue stones arranged like a forget-me-not flower. He picks it up and puts it in his pocket. The ring belongs to a captive princess who, as a child, was taken from her mountain castle when a foreign conqueror killed her father and imprisoned her. Now, the foreign prince wants to marry her, but he is a coarse, giant-like brute. Desperate to escape, the princess had thrown the ring into the river as a plea for help, following a spell her mother taught her. The ring leads the prince to her. Meanwhile, a mountain troll has also been pursuing the princess, intending to eat her (keeping her fattened up for years). When the prince discovers the princess imprisoned in the troll's mountain domain, he must face both the mountain troll and the foreign prince to free her. He and the princess escape on his horse, with the mountain troll bellowing after them like a pack of wolves. They ride through forests and across rivers until they reach the prince's castle. The story is notable for its romantic opening (described as having 'a perfect fairy tale first line') and its layered conflicts — the innocent ring, the captive princess, the hungry troll, and the prince's steadfast pursuit of love and justice. John Bauer's illustration of the knight riding with the princess is one of his most famous works.
moral: Love and courage can overcome even the most terrible obstacles.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Ringen' (1914) by Helena Nyblom, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll' (Vol. 8).

title: The Old Troll of Big Mountain
summary: A poor cottager and his wife own little more than a small cottage, two goats, and their five-year-old son Olle. While they work as day laborers, they lock Olle in the cottage with bread and milk, letting the goats graze in an enclosure. One evening, they return to find both goats gone — the evil old troll of Big Mountain (Storberget) has stolen them. The loss is devastating, and Olle now gets only water instead of goat milk. Worse, they fear the troll might return for Olle himself. Determined to recover what was taken, Olle sets out despite his young age. Armed with a board with a nail and a wooden sword, he ventures into the forest to confront the troll. The old troll of Big Mountain is a fearsome creature — described in Swedish folklore as a pipe-smoking, flute-playing giant who steals livestock and children. Olle's courage in facing this terrifying being demonstrates that even the smallest and weakest can stand against oppression. The story, originally published in the magazine Tummeliten in 1900 with illustrations by Jenny Nyström, presents a classic tale of a child's bravery against a supernatural adversary, with the troll representing the harsh and unpredictable dangers of the natural world that rural Swedish families had to contend with.
moral: Even the smallest and youngest can find the courage to face great dangers.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Trollgubben i Storberget' (1900) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Länge, länge, sedan... Sagor'.

title: Leap the Elk and Little Princess Cottongrass
summary: Princess Cottongrass (Tuvstarr) has lived her entire life in the Dream Castle, a sheltered world of safety and beauty. One day, a magnificent elk named Long-Leap (Skutt) appears. Enchanted by his stately bearing and crown-like antlers, she begs him to carry her out into the world. The elk warns her: 'The world is big and cold, little child, and you are so small. The world is full of evil and wickedness, and it will hurt you.' But she insists, and he relents. On their journey through the deep Swedish forest, the princess encounters a series of supernatural dangers. First, fairies lure her, and in her distraction, she loses her golden crown. Then the Lady of the Forest (Skogsrå) tricks her into letting go of the elk's antlers to show her dress — and snatches it away, leaving her naked. Each time, the elk warns her to hold tight to his antlers, but her innocent curiosity and trust lead her to lose more of herself. Finally, they reach a dark tarn deep in the forest. Mesmerized by the dark, still water, Princess Cottongrass leans forward to see her reflection, and the golden heart her mother gave her at birth slips from her neck and sinks into the depths. Despite the elk's desperate warnings, she cannot tear herself away from the water. The elk, heartbroken, leaves her there. The story ends with a poignant transformation: the princess becomes a small white cottongrass flower, forever leaning over the edge of the pool, gazing into the water for her lost heart. The elk returns now and then, the only one who remembers she was once a princess. John Bauer's illustrations for this tale are among the most famous in Swedish art.
moral: Innocence untempered by caution can be lost to the dangers of the world, piece by piece.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om älgtjuren Skutt och lilla prinsessan Tuvstarr' (1913) by Helge Kjellin, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Magpie with Salt on Her Tail
summary: A poor boy named Olle (or Theo in some versions) is always wishing for things he cannot afford — a knife, a sled, a horse and carriage. An old man tells him an old legend: if you sprinkle salt on a magpie's tail and make a wish before the salt falls off, the wish will come true. Olle spends days trying to catch a magpie, but they are too clever. Finally, a magpie speaks to him, claiming to be an enchanted prince. The magpie agrees to let Olle sprinkle salt on her tail, but only if he first brings her increasingly extravagant gifts — first a fine knife, then a bicycle, then a sled, then a horse and carriage, then a castle, and finally chests of gold. Each time Olle acquires the item through his own hard work, the magpie demands something grander. Olle's frustration grows, but he persists. Finally, after years of labor, he has earned the castle, the gold, and everything the magpie demanded. When at last he sprinkles the salt on her tail and she asks him to make his wish, Olle realizes he already has everything he could ever want — all earned through his own efforts. The magpie flies away, having taught him the greatest lesson of all. The story is a Swedish folk tale variation on the theme that hard work, not magic, is the true path to achieving one's dreams.
moral: The true path to achieving your wishes is not through magic but through your own hard work and perseverance.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Skatan som fick salt på stjärten' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg.

title: Stalo and Kauras
summary: This tale originates from the Sami people of northern Sweden (Lapland) and features a Stalo — a giant, man-eating troll-like creature from Sami folklore. Stalo is described as a large, evil being who wears impenetrable black iron clothing, hunts and devours people and reindeer, and terrorizes the region. He is rich, powerful, and ruthless, but also clumsy and unintelligent. In this story, a Sami boy named Kauras confronts the Stalo.
