Using his wits rather than strength, Kauras devises a clever plan to defeat the giant. He tricks Stalo into a head-butting competition against a tree trunk, rendering the giant unconscious. The Stalo's stupidity and vanity are his undoing — he cannot believe that a small boy could outsmart him. John Bauer's illustration for this tale depicts the moment of confrontation between the small, clever boy and the massive, armored Stalo. The story belongs to a broader tradition of Sami Stalo tales (including 'How the Stalos were Tricked' and 'The Tale of Njunje Paggas'), which serve as teaching tools about courage, resourcefulness, and the importance of maintaining Sami identity in the face of external threats. The Stalo figure in Sami folklore is sometimes interpreted as a symbolic representation of encroaching outside forces that the Sami people have historically had to resist.
moral: Intelligence and cunning can defeat even the most physically powerful enemy.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally by P.A. Lindholm, based on Sami oral tradition. Collected in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Flower of Happiness on Sunnymount Crest
summary: Deep in the Swedish forest, on the sun-drenched peak of Sunnymount Crest (Solberga klint), grows a magical flower that grants happiness to whoever finds it. Two young girls set out on a perilous journey through the deep woods where trolls, fairies, and witches keep their secrets. Their only hope of true happiness lies in reaching this elusive flower at the top of the mountain. Along the way, they encounter various supernatural beings from Swedish folklore — some helpful, some hostile. They must navigate enchanted forests, outwit dangerous creatures, and persevere through exhaustion and fear. The flower at Sunnymount Crest represents not just magical happiness but the idea that true fulfillment comes from the journey itself and the courage to pursue one's dreams despite all obstacles. The story, by Alfred Smedberg, was first published in the 1907 edition of 'Bland Tomtar och Troll' with illustrations by John Bauer. It reflects Smedberg's characteristic themes of love for the Swedish countryside, moral clarity, and the triumph of goodness and perseverance over adversity.
moral: The pursuit of happiness requires courage and perseverance through many obstacles.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Lyckoblomman på Solberga klint' (1907) by Alfred Smedberg, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: Dag and Daga, and the Flying Troll of Sky Mountain
summary: Dag and Daga are orphaned siblings who live alone in a forest cottage after their parents die. Despite being young, they manage to survive through their own resourcefulness: Dag hunts with his bow, and Daga gathers herbs, berries, and mushrooms. They live happily until one day Dag goes hunting and does not return. Daga waits in vain, then discovers only his hat and quiver left behind. Despite the warnings of neighbors who tell her to abandon hope, Daga sets out alone to find her brother. She travels through vast forests and over high mountains, facing dangers and exhaustion. Unbeknownst to her, her loyal house tomte follows and protects her. She encounters a prince who falls in love with her and offers her fine clothes and a golden crown, but when she realizes his servants are not actually searching for Dag, she leaves the finery behind and continues alone. She reaches Sky Mountain, where the terrible Flying Troll dwells. Inside, she finds Dag encased in rock — the troll imprisoned him for failing to learn gold-forging. The troll offers a bargain: if Daga can produce a queen's crown of pure gold before he counts to three, he will free Dag. As the troll begins counting, Daga produces the golden crown the prince gave her and throws it onto the troll's clawed hand. Stunned, the troll must keep his word, and Dag is freed. The reunited siblings return home, and the prince, having punished his dishonest servants, finds Daga and marries her, valuing her loyalty and bravery above all. The story celebrates sibling devotion, courage, and the power of keeping one's word.
moral: Loyalty and courage in the face of danger will ultimately triumph, and faithful love is rewarded.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om Dag och Daga och flygtrollet på Skyberget' (1907) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: Linda-Gold and the Old King
summary: Long ago there lived an eccentric old king who had suffered great sorrow: his queen and children had died, and someone had torn his heart to pieces — someone 'with claws,' he said. Ever since, the king imagines that everyone has claws on their hands. No one is allowed to come within two arm's lengths of him. Servants must serve food at the farthest edge of the table, and anyone who steps closer spends a week in prison. Despite his oddities, he rules justly and is beloved by his people. But they worry because he refuses to remarry or name an heir. One day, a little girl named Linda-Gold arrives at the palace. Unlike everyone else, she is not afraid of the king. With the innocent audacity that only a child possesses, she approaches him and touches his hand. The king is so startled by her fearless touch that instead of ordering her imprisoned, he allows her to stay. Linda-Gold's pure-hearted love and her complete lack of fear gradually work their way into the king's wounded heart. She cuts her fingernails to show him she has no claws. Through her gentle presence, the king begins to heal from his trauma. His delusion fades, and he eventually adopts Linda-Gold as his daughter and heir, discovering that she truly has no claws — only the pure heart of a child. The story is a poignant exploration of grief, trauma, and the healing power of innocent love.
moral: Innocent love and fearless kindness can heal even the deepest emotional wounds.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Linda-Gull och den gamle kungen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg, in 'Länge, länge, sedan... Sagor'.

title: The Boy and the Trolls, or The Adventure
summary: An unnamed young lad packs his lunch, leaves the city, and heads into the woods in search of adventure. Meanwhile, in the kingdom, the young princess has gone missing, and her father the king has surrendered control of the realm to his second wife — a conniving woman suspected of orchestrating her stepdaughter's disappearance. On his journey, the boy encounters three troll brothers — Big Brother (with enormous ears), Medium Brother (with a huge chin), and Little Brother (with a prominent nose). Through his unfailingly polite manner, the boy treats them as he would any strangers, calling them 'uncle.' The trolls, who think the boy is hideous (to accentuate their own beauty), are heading home to their mother's mountain with treasures (silver, gold, precious stones) and delicacies (a garden snake, a frog, a toad) to court a captive princess. The boy follows them. Inside the mountain, the princess is being held captive by the troll mother until she chooses which son to marry. Benevolent elves bring fresh air and fruit, but cannot break the trolls' magic. The boy learns from the troll mother that the queen is actually her sister — a troll herself. The only thing that can defeat the trolls is a magic spell spoken by a fearless boy. The boy recites the words, rescues the princess, and they return to the city with the trolls' treasure. There, the evil queen is plotting to seal all windows and build a wall against the west wind. The boy exposes the queen as a troll, recites the magic words again, and the west wind blows the trolls away. The boy is rewarded, and the kingdom is saved.
moral: Courage, politeness, and a pure heart can overcome even the most fearsome adversaries.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Pojken och trollen eller Äventyret' (1915) by Walter Stenström, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Maiden in the Castle of Rosy Clouds
summary: A poor, hardworking young man (named Cedric in some versions) collapses from exhaustion and dreams of a beautiful fairy maiden who walks out of rosy clouds and invites him to find her. When he wakes, he is determined to win her. But no one knows where the Castle of Rosy Clouds is, and people mock him for his quest. Finally, an old woman tells him a poem revealing three magical items he must obtain: a gray stallion who can find his way through the clouds, a red mantle to protect him from fire, and a sword named Gull to split the iron serpent's skull. The young man has no idea how to acquire these items. He returns to ordinary life, but over many years, he encounters three situations where people are in danger. Each time he selflessly helps — rescuing someone from a snake, saving a town, and so on — and each heroic act rewards him with one of the three magical items. But the quest takes so long that by the time he finally reaches the Castle of Rosy Clouds, he is an old man. As he ascends through the clouds on the gray stallion, wielding the sword Gull and wearing the red mantle, a giant appears. He charges and the giant vanishes. At the castle, the fairy maiden welcomes him and, looking at his reflection in the moat, he sees that he has been made young and handsome again. They live happily together as rulers of the castle. The story celebrates perseverance, selflessness, and the idea that true love is worth a lifetime of effort.
moral: A lifetime of selfless deeds is the only path to winning true and lasting love.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Jungfrun i rosiga molnens borg' (1911) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

title: The Queen
summary: Queen Adelgunda possesses a remarkable gift: she can see into the hearts of men. This power makes her a wise and just ruler, but it also creates complications. When the noble Sigmund — whom the queen secretly loves — is accused of dishonorable intentions, the queen's gift suggests his guilt. To save Sigmund from the dishonor that her veiled judgment implies, Adelgunda must make a painful choice: reveal her own love for him, risking her reputation and position, or allow an innocent man to be condemned. The story follows the queen's internal struggle as she balances her public duty with her private heart. In a climactic moment, she chooses love and truth over safety and appearances, baring her soul to save the man she loves. The story is notable for its psychological depth and focus on a female protagonist who holds real power and must navigate complex emotional and political terrain. Anna Wahlenberg, who wrote over 200 fairy tales, was known for embedding social commentary and feminist themes in her stories. 'The Queen' exemplifies her ability to combine traditional fairy tale enchantment with nuanced human emotion, portraying a ruler whose supernatural gift becomes a burden that only love can truly overcome.
moral: True love requires the courage to be vulnerable and the willingness to sacrifice for another.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Drottningen' (1903) by Anna Wahlenberg. Also appears in 'The Diamond Bird and Other Tales' (1969, Doubleday).

title: The Bird Grip
summary: A king goes blind, and an old woman tells him that only the song of the magical bird Grip — kept in a cage by a king in another land — can restore his sight. His eldest son sets out to fetch the bird but stops at a merry inn and forgets his mission, drinking and gambling. The second son does the same. The youngest son, however, resists temptation and continues his journey. He stays at a sinister inn where the host murders guests who cannot pay. The prince pays the dead man's debt, arranges his burial, and escapes with the help of a captive maid. He then meets a talking fox who becomes his guide. The fox gives him magic grains to steal the bird Grip, but warns him not to stroke it. The prince succeeds in taking the bird but strokes it anyway, causing it to scream. Captured, he is put on trial and, following the fox's advice, answers 'Yes' when asked if he is a master thief. The king spares him on condition that he steal the world's most beautiful princess, then a golden horse. Each time the prince succeeds with the fox's help (though he nearly fails each time by breaking the fox's warnings). After rescuing the princess and horse, the prince finds his jealous older brothers about to be hanged for debt. He ransoms them, but they betray him, throwing him into a lion pit and claiming the treasures for themselves. The bird will not sing, the horse will not be ridden, and the princess weeps constantly. The prince escapes the lions (the fox was there protecting him), beheads the fox (which transforms into the dead man whose debt he paid), and disguises himself as a horse-shoer. He fits the horse with golden shoes and, by calling the bird by name, makes it sing, restoring the king's sight. The truth is revealed, the brothers are banished, and the youngest marries the princess.
moral: Loyalty, perseverance, and kindness to strangers are rewarded, while betrayal and jealousy are punished.
source: Andrew Lang, The Pink Fairy Book (1897). Swedish folk tale, originally collected from Swedish oral tradition.

title: King Lindorm
summary: A childless queen receives advice from an old woman: bathe in running water and eat two red onions. In her excitement, she eats one onion with the skin on, and peels the other. As a result, she gives birth first to a hideous lindorm (serpent), who is secretly thrown into the forest, and then to a beautiful prince. Years later, when the prince sets out to find a bride, the lindorm blocks the road and demands that a mate be found for him first. The king and queen try to appease the lindorm by forcing women to marry him, but he devours each bride. Finally, a shepherd's daughter is offered. She wanders into the forest weeping and meets a wise old woman who tells her exactly what to do: she must wear all her dresses, and for each dress she removes, demand that the lindorm shed one of his skins. On her wedding night, the girl follows this advice. The lindorm sheds one skin after another, until nine skins lie on the floor and he is revealed as a handsome prince beneath — the true eldest son, freed from the curse caused by the queen eating the onion with its skin. The story has a complex second part: King Lindorm goes to war, leaving his queen with his evil stepmother, who writes that the queen gave birth to 'whelps.' The faithful servant hides the queen and her twin sons. After years of wandering, she stays with a man who has made a pact with the Devil, which she rescues using three magical nuts from her mother's grave. Eventually, King Lindorm returns, discovers the truth, and the family is reunited.
moral: Courage and wisdom can break even the most powerful curses.
source: Andrew Lang, The Pink Fairy Book (1897). Swedish variant collected by Eva Wigström from Landskrona. ATU type 433B.

title: The Sea Nymph
summary: One night on the northwest shores of the island of Gotland, fishermen sleeping in a hut see a white, dew-besprinkled woman's hand reaching through the door. They recognize it as a sea nymph seeking to destroy them, and pretend to be asleep. The next day, a young, newly married man from Kinnar in Lummelund joins them. He boasts that had he been present, he would have taken the hand. That night, a plump white arm with a beautiful hand reaches in. True to his word, the young man seizes it. Instantly, he is drawn silently out through the door and disappears. Despite an extensive search, he is not found. Three years pass, and his wife, having mourned him as dead, is persuaded to remarry. On the evening of her wedding, a stranger enters the cottage — it is her first husband, returned. He explains that he was dragged down to the sea nymph's pearly halls, where he forgot his former life completely. Only that morning, the sea nymph told him 'There will be a dusting out in Kinnar this evening,' meaning his wife's wedding. His memory returned, and he begged to see her. The sea nymph allowed him to go but forbade him from entering the house. Unable to resist, he entered anyway. A great tempest immediately rose, tearing off half the roof. The man fell sick and died three days later. The legend, collected by Herman Hofberg in 'Swedish Fairy Tales' (1890), is a classic cautionary tale about the dangers of supernatural beings, the consequences of arrogance, and the irrevocable nature of bargains made with otherworldly powers.
moral: Arrogance and rash promises can lead to terrible consequences, and some bargains with the supernatural cannot be undone.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales, translated by W.H. Myers (1890). Collected from Gotland, Sweden.

title: Ljungby Horn and Pipe
summary: At Trolle-Ljungby Castle in Scania, southern Sweden, a large glacial boulder called Maglestenen stands in the fields. Legend says that trolls live beneath this stone, and every Christmas Eve, the stone rises on golden pillars while the trolls feast and make merry beneath it. In the mid-16th century, the widowed Lady Sissela Ulfstand, mistress of Ljungby, grew curious about these tales and promised her best horse to any servant who would ride to Maglestenen on Christmas night and investigate. A brave stable hand took the challenge. When he arrived, he found the stone indeed raised on golden pillars, with trolls reveling beneath. They offered him a drinking horn and a pipe (whistle), urging him to drink the troll king's health and blow the pipe. As he was about to drink, a captive girl (a human taken by the trolls) secretly warned him. He threw the drink over his shoulder — the drops burned his horse's flank — and galloped away with the horn and pipe. The trolls chased him, but the girl had advised him to ride across a plowed field (forming a cross shape, which trolls cannot cross). The trolls were forced to run along the furrows, slowing them down. He reached the castle just in time, the drawbridge was raised, and the trolls could not enter. Furious, they cursed the estate: the horse and rider would die, the castle would burn three times, and if the artifacts were ever removed, the owner would face great misfortune. The horn (a drinking vessel with gilded silver mounts) and the pipe (carved from ivory, playable from both ends) remain at Trolle-Ljungby to this day. The curse is said to have come true: the horse died, the rider died three days later, and the castle burned three times.
moral: Some treasures come at too great a cost, and ancient curses should not be taken lightly.
source: Swedish folk legend from Scania, recorded by Wilhelm Julius Coyet (1692) and by Carl Linnaeus in his 'Skånska resa' (1750). Artifacts preserved at Trolle-Ljungby Castle.

title: The Brownies (Tomtar)
summary: In Swedish folklore, tomtar (also called brownies or nisses) are small, elderly-looking men about the size of a three- or four-year-old child, dressed in gray clothes with red caps. They live on farms, usually in barns or pantries, and work tirelessly to maintain the household. They wake servants for threshing, help in forges by signaling when to turn the iron, and watch over the cattle. Where there is order and prosperity, the tomte thrives; where there is disrespect or uncleanliness, he leaves. One story tells of a farmer whose harvest never ran out. Hiding in the barn, he saw a crowd of tomtar each carrying a stalk of rye, including one tiny tomte no bigger than a thumb carrying a straw. When the farmer asked why the smallest one puffed so hard under such a light load, luck left his house forever. Another story tells of a tomte who was offered Christmas porridge with honey. When the porridge was too hot and the honey melted to the bottom, he thought there was no honey and killed a cow in anger. Finding the honey at the bottom of the bowl, he bitterly regretted his haste and stole an identical cow from a neighbor to replace the one he killed. Yet another tale describes a housewife whose meal bins never emptied. Peeking through the door, she saw a tomte sifting meal tirelessly. Feeling grateful, she made him new clothes and left them on the bin. When he tried to sift while wearing his fine new suit, the meal made him dusty. He threw down the sieve and declared, 'Junker Grand is dusting himself. He shall sift no more,' and all his blessings ceased. These stories teach that one should never call attention to the brownies' work or try to reward them openly.
moral: Respect the unseen helpers who maintain good fortune, and never question the gifts of luck.
source: Swedish folk tradition. Collected in Herman Hofberg, 'Swedish Fairy Tales' (1890) and various Swedish folklore collections.

title: Agneta and the Sea King
summary: Agneta, daughter of a lord, encounters the Sea King by the lake at sunset. Enchanted by his music, she forgets her former life and becomes his queen beneath the waters for seven years, bearing him seven sons. When church bells reach her underwater realm, she begs to attend church. Above the surface, memories of her father and former life overwhelm her. When the Sea King comes to reclaim her, she refuses, choosing her own freedom over returning to her children.
full_text: Agneta, the daughter of a lord of a castle, lingers by the lake at sunset and encounters the Sea King, a handsome but sorrowful being who has ruled the lake for hundreds of years. He sings and plays his harp, enchanting her so completely that she forgets her former life, her father, and her childhood. He pulls her into his underwater kingdom where she becomes his queen. For seven years she lives beneath the lake, bearing him seven sons, forgetting the world above. One Sunday, the sound of church bells penetrates the depths, stirring buried memories. She begs to attend church, promising to return. Above the surface, she sees her aged father weeping in the church and her memories return fully. When the Sea King appears at the church door to reclaim her, pleading that their youngest child cries for her, she refuses: 'None of you will make me return to your realm.' The Sea King departs sorrowfully, and Agneta is joyfully reunited with her father. Unlike traditional tales where the mother returns to her children, Agneta chooses her own freedom, making this story unusually striking for its feminist subversion of folkloric conventions.
moral: True freedom means choosing your own path, even when it requires painful sacrifice.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1910 (Helena Nyblom). English translation in Great Swedish Fairy Tales (Holger Lundbergh, 1973).

title: The Boy Who Went to the Wind's Cave
summary: Little Mats sets out on a perilous journey to the end of the world to find his kidnapped sister. To reach the Wind's Cave and the magical realm where she is held, he must make terrible sacrifices along the way, giving up one ear, one eye, and one hand, each loss enabling him to pass through a different barrier. When he finally reaches the wind's domain, he confronts the forces that took his sister and rescues her from captivity. As a reward, his losses are miraculously restored, and he wins a princess and half the kingdom.
full_text: Little Mats sets out on a perilous journey to the end of the world to find his kidnapped sister. To reach the Wind's Cave and the magical realm where she is held, he must make terrible sacrifices along the way — he gives up one ear, one eye, and one hand, each loss enabling him to pass through a different barrier or gain a necessary boon. His journey takes him through enchanted forests, across impossible mountains, and past guardians that test his courage and resolve. When he finally reaches the wind's domain, he confronts the forces that took his sister and rescues her from captivity. As a reward for his bravery and self-sacrifice, he not only retrieves his sister safely but also saves a princess from the same fate. The magical losses he suffered are miraculously restored to him. In classic fairy-tale fashion, he marries the princess and receives half the kingdom. The story follows the archetypal pattern of the youngest or humblest hero who succeeds through courage and sacrifice where others would fail.
moral: Great rewards require great sacrifices, and true courage is measured by what one is willing to give up.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1910 (Alfred Smedberg). English translation in Great Swedish Fairy Tales (Holger Lundbergh, 1973).

title: The Queen's Necklace
summary: The cruel King Hendrik possesses a necklace of one hundred perfect pearls. He forces his nephew to find him a wife, and the kind Blanzeflor is chosen. On their wedding day, the king fastens the pearls around her neck with a threat: the day she is found without them will be her last. Blanzeflor, trapped in a cold marriage, gives away the pearls one by one to help the needy. When the king discovers the necklace is gone, he sentences her to death, but the birds she befriended rescue her and tear the wicked king to pieces.
full_text: A cruel and greedy king named Hendrik possesses a necklace of one hundred perfect pearls, which he prizes above all his treasures. He sends his nephew, Nils, to find him a wife. Nils returns with the kind and beautiful Blanzeflor. On their wedding day, the king fastens the pearls around her neck with a terrible warning: 'The day I find you without them will be your last.' Blanzeflor, trapped in a cold marriage, finds her only happiness in feeding the birds. She begins giving away the pearls one by one to help the needy villagers — the poor, the sick, the hungry — each pearl buying food or warmth for someone desperate. When the king discovers the necklace is gone, he sentences her to death. On the morning of her execution, however, the birds she befriended come to her cell, rescue her, and tear the wicked king to pieces, freeing the villagers from his tyranny. Blanzeflor then marries her true love, Nils, and they live in peace.
moral: Kindness to the smallest creatures can bring the greatest deliverance.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1914 (Anna Wahlenberg). English translation in An Illustrated Treasury of Swedish Folk and Fairy Tales (Floris Books, 2019).

title: Brother Martin
summary: Set during Sweden's Christianization, Brother Martin, a monk from Sigtuna, travels into the remote Bergslagen region to missionize among pagan forest dwellers. The deep woods are still ruled by the old beliefs, and he finds himself unable to convert the stubborn, idolatrous people. As he pushes deeper into the wilderness, he is set upon by trolls, elves, and other supernatural beings that guard the ancient ways. Despite his unwavering devotion, Brother Martin is overwhelmed by the hostile forces and suffers martyrdom, receiving the martyr's crown in heaven.
full_text: Set during the time of Sweden's Christianization, this story follows Brother Martin, a monk who travels from Sigtuna — one of Sweden's oldest Christian settlements — into the remote Bergslagen region to missionize among the pagan forest dwellers. The deep woods of Bergslagen are still ruled by the old beliefs, and Brother Martin finds himself unable to convert the stubborn, idolatrous people who worship the ancient gods and spirits. As he pushes deeper into the wilderness, he becomes lost and increasingly isolated from the human world. The forest closes around him, and he is set upon by trolls, elves, and other supernatural beings that guard the ancient ways. They torment him both physically and spiritually, testing his faith. Despite his unwavering devotion, Brother Martin is ultimately overwhelmed by the hostile forces of the forest and suffers martyrdom. His reward comes not on earth but in heaven, where he receives the martyr's crown for his sacrifice. John Bauer's illustrations for this story — particularly 'Brother St. Martin and the Three Trolls' (1913, Nationalmuseum) — show the monk standing amid a press of grotesque trolls while a luminous figure shines with otherworldly light.
moral: Faith maintained in the face of overwhelming darkness is its own reward.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1913 (Emil Eliason). Illustrated by John Bauer.

title: The Troll Son Who Had Sun Eyes
summary: Humpe, the red-haired son of a troll mother, has large clear eyes with a gleam of sunlight in them, marking him as different from troll-kind. His unnatural affinity for daylight makes him an outcast. Filled with daylight's vigor, he leaves troll society and ventures into the forest. He encounters a birch maiden who, when he kisses her tree, transforms into a beautiful woman made of light. She accepts him despite his trollish appearance, and his love for sunlight transforms him, making him lose his troll-grey skin and become a handsome human forester.
full_text: Humpe is the red-haired son of a troll mother named Humpa, who is gentler than the other trolls. Unlike his kin, whose eyes are red and swollen, Humpe's eyes are large and clear with a gleam of sunlight in them, marking him as fundamentally different from troll-kind. His unnatural affinity for daylight makes him an outcast in the dark troll world — other trolls literally explode if exposed to too much sunlight, and they attempt to kill Humpe by keeping him awake all night so he will fatally encounter dawn. But Humpe proves immune; not only does sunlight fail to harm him, he can capture and reflect it through his eyes. Filled with daylight's vigor, he leaves troll society and ventures deeper into the forest. Initially self-conscious about his ugliness, he encounters personified nature in three stages: a forest spring where he sees his reflection and is encouraged to drink; a hazelnut tree that gently reprimands his presumption; and a birch maiden who, when he kisses her tree, transforms into a beautiful woman made of light. She accepts him despite his trollish appearance, telling him 'you have beautiful eyes, Humpe Trollson.' They fall in love, and she names him a forester. His love for sunlight transforms him: he loses his troll-grey skin, his hair becomes healthy brown, and he emerges as an admirably handsome human.
moral: True beauty lies not in appearance but in the light within one's eyes and heart.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1912 (Vilhälm Nordin). English summary in University of Texas thesis analysis (2014).

title: The Golden Keys
summary: Lena, a humble meadow-maid, encounters three fairies who present her with a golden key that leads to a magical dream garden. This gift comes with a strict condition: the secret must never be revealed to anyone. Lena accepts and experiences the joy of the dream garden, but her loyalty is soon tested when the young king falls into grave danger. The only way to save him is to reveal the secret, breaking her promise. She chooses sacrifice over self-preservation, saves the king, and is rewarded with his hand in marriage.
full_text: Lena, a humble meadow-maid (vallpiga), encounters three fairies who present her with a golden key that leads to a magical dream garden — a paradise of wonders beyond mortal imagination. However, this gift comes with a strict condition: the secret of the golden key must never be revealed to anyone. Lena accepts and experiences the joy of the dream garden, but her loyalty is soon tested. The young king of the realm falls into grave danger, and the only way to save him would be to reveal the secret of the golden key, breaking her promise to the fairies. Despite knowing the cost, Lena chooses sacrifice over self-preservation. She gives up the secret to rescue the king. Her selfless act, while breaking the fairy enchantment, reveals her true character. The king is saved, and Lena is rewarded not only with his gratitude but with his hand in marriage, becoming queen. The story emphasizes the value of self-sacrifice, suggesting that love and compassion for others are worth more than any magical gift or personal treasure.
moral: Selfless love for others is worth more than any magical treasure.

title: Innocence's Journey
summary: Princess Bella sets out on a solitary journey through the deep forest, a passage that strips away the trappings of civilization. A raven steals her crown for its nest. Undeterred, she continues deeper into the woods, shedding her garments as she becomes one with the wild. The story builds to a confrontation with a great dark eagle, and Bella reaches out her last garment to it in a gesture of complete trust. John Bauer's iconic illustration captures the tiny, pale princess facing the enormous bird in the twilight forest.
full_text: Princess Bella sets out on a solitary journey through the deep forest, a passage that strips away the trappings of civilization and leaves her in a state of pure innocence. Early in her wanderings, a raven swoops down and steals her crown, carrying it to its nest where the baby ravens play with it as a toy. Undeterred, Bella continues deeper into the woods, gradually shedding her garments of silk and finery as she becomes one with the wild. The story builds to a striking confrontation with a great dark eagle — a majestic and slightly menacing embodiment of the untamed forest. Bella, now nearly naked, reaches out her last remaining garment to the eagle in a gesture of complete trust and childlike innocence. John Bauer's iconic illustration for this tale captures this moment: the tiny, pale princess facing the enormous bird in the twilight forest. The story explores the relationship between innocence and the wild, suggesting that purity of heart can disarm even the most fearsome aspects of nature. Bella's journey becomes a spiritual pilgrimage back to a state of grace, where she is neither afraid nor corrupted by the darkness around her.
moral: Purity of heart can disarm even the wildest forces of nature.

title: The Boy Who Strapped the Troll to the Sled
summary: A boy wanders deep into the forest and comes to the trolls' cave. Because he is not receptive to their temptations, the trolls have no power to hold him and are forced to bring him home. The tale illustrates the folk belief that trolls can only hold power over those who yield to their enticements.
full_text: A young boy, adventurous and unafraid, wanders deep into the dark forest and stumbles upon the cave where a family of trolls dwell. The trolls, grotesque and hungry, try to tempt the boy with treasures, sweets, and promises of power, hoping to ensnare him in their dark world. But the boy remains pure of heart and unreceptive to their temptations. He neither fears them nor desires what they offer. Because he does not yield to their enticements, the trolls have no power to hold him. Bound by their own rules, the frustrated trolls are forced to bring him safely home. The tale illustrates the deep folk belief that trolls and other supernatural beings can only hold power over those who surrender to greed, fear, or temptation. An innocent heart is the best protection against the darkest forces.
moral: Those who resist temptation have nothing to fear from evil.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1907 (Harald Östenson). Illustrated by John Bauer.

title: He Who Could Ride in All Weathers
summary: A castle-dwelling daughter is brought home by her father to the castle in Tyrol. She does not give her hand to the richest or most handsome suitor, but instead chooses the one who defies all dangers and makes his way through all weathers. The tale carries a feminist message about a woman's right to choose her partner based on character rather than wealth or status.
full_text: A young woman of noble birth living in a castle in Tyrol is brought home by her father, who wishes to see her properly married. Suitors arrive from far and wide — the richest lords, the handsomest knights, the most powerful noblemen — each presenting their wealth, titles, and grand estates. But the daughter looks past all their finery. She watches how each man conducts himself, noticing who takes shelter from the storm and who braves it. She does not give her hand to the richest, most handsome, or most powerful suitor. Instead, she chooses the one who defies all dangers and makes his way through all weathers — a man of true character who proves his worth through action rather than boasting. The tale carries a subtle but clear feminist message about a woman's right to choose her own partner based on the content of his character rather than his wealth, title, or social status.
moral: Character is revealed not by fair weather but by the storms one is willing to face.

title: The Skin Satchel
summary: Hunger and need have fallen upon Niklas's village. He accidentally witnesses a troll take six grains from a leather satchel and in minutes grow an entire field of wheat. He resolves to obtain the magic satchel, but the treasure is hard to find and even harder to keep. The satchel can only be found at night but must be retrieved by sunrise. During this time the trolls cause such terror that one might lose the treasure by letting go of the satchel or speaking a single word.
full_text: Hunger and need have fallen upon Niklas's village. How many will not survive the winter? Desperate, Niklas ventures into the forest where he accidentally witnesses a troll take six grains from a leather satchel and in minutes grow an entire field of wheat. Niklas resolves to obtain the magic satchel for his starving village. But the treasure is hard to find and even harder to keep. The satchel can only be found at night but must be retrieved by sunrise. During the hunt, the trolls cause such terror — shaking the ground, sending ghostly apparitions, howling like wolves — that one might lose the treasure if one lets go of the satchel or speaks a single word. Niklas must endure the longest night of his life, clinging to the satchel while every fiber of his being screams to run or cry out. When dawn finally breaks, the trolls retreat and the satchel is his. He returns to his village a hero, the magic grains saving them all from starvation.
moral: Endurance through the darkest night brings the brightest dawn.

title: The Fisherman Sikur and the Troll Net
summary: Sikur the fisherman acquires a magical fishing net. When a troll steals the net, Sikur's three sons — one the fastest in the world, one the strongest, and one the wisest — team up to trick the troll and retrieve the net. Each son uses his unique gift to outwit the trollish foe in a classic trio-of-heroes structure.
full_text: Sikur the fisherman lives by a lake rich with fish, but his catches are meager until he acquires a magical fishing net that fills itself with fish each time it is cast. Word of the wonderful net reaches the ears of a troll living beneath the mountain, who steals the net while Sikur sleeps. Sikur's three sons — each possessing a unique gift — set out to retrieve it. The eldest is the fastest runner in the world, able to outpace the wind. The second is the strongest, capable of lifting boulders. The youngest is the wisest, able to see through any deception. Together, they track the troll to his mountain lair. The fastest son scouts ahead, the strongest moves obstacles from their path, and the wisest devises a plan to trick the troll. Using each gift at precisely the right moment, they retrieve the net and return it to their father, who once again fills his nets and provides for the village. John Bauer's illustration for this tale captured the moment the troll reaches for the net.
moral: Working together, each using their unique strengths, even the most formidable foe can be overcome.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1908 (Cyrus Granér). Illustrated by John Bauer.

title: Sir Birre and the Trolls
summary: Sir Birre lives on a farm below the mountain where two trolls, Jock and Turing, dwell. When his sweetheart Tinka is kidnapped by the troll Domp-Domp-Dubbeldomp, Birre must ask Jock and Turing for help. In exchange, he teaches them to build a house and smelt iron. The trolls rescue Tinka, who traveled in Turing's furry ear, and they all become friends.
full_text: Sir Birre lives on a beautiful farm below the mountain where two trolls, Jock and Turing, dwell. He wants to show them his new love Tinka-Lillfot-Gullhår, but the large, dim-witted trolls cannot see the tiny humans. One day Tinka is kidnapped by the troll Domp-Domp-Dubbeldomp. Birre must ask Jock and Turing for help. In exchange he offers to teach them useful skills: building a house and smelting iron ore so they no longer have to eat it raw. After two weeks the trolls return with Tinka — she traveled in Turing's furry ear and found it soft and warm. Birre teaches the trolls their new skills while Tinka entertains the troll wives, who are enchanted by her tiny, doll-like beauty. They all become good friends; sometimes the trolls make the mountain rumble just to amuse Tinka.
moral: Even the most unlikely beings can become friends when kindness bridges the divide.

title: The Tomtarna
summary: The boy Andreas has left his childhood farm to seek fortune in the city. His aging parents miss him terribly. The farm tomte takes pity on them and sets out to find the boy. Andreas works as a guard in the troll prison until a troll bites off his fingers. In despair, he runs into the forest. The tomte finds him, produces the mother's apron, and tucks it into the boy's hand. Andreas dreams his mother is leading him home. When he wakes, the tomte has enchanted his hand with 'troll fingers' that work better than ever.
full_text: The boy Andreas has left his childhood farm to seek fortune in the city, like his five older siblings before him. His aging parents miss him terribly. The farm tomte (house gnome) takes pity on them and sets out to find the boy and watch over him secretly. Andreas works as a guard in the troll prison — a dangerous but well-paid job, hoping to earn enough to return home and restore the farm. One day a troll bites off the fingers on Andreas's right hand. In despair, he runs into the forest, ashamed to face his parents as a cripple. The tomte finds him sleeping, produces the mother's apron that he smuggled from the farm, and tucks a corner into the boy's hand. In his dream Andreas believes his mother is leading him home and follows willingly. When he wakes, the tomte has enchanted his injured hand, saying: 'Just think that you want to, for what one wants, one can.' Andreas now has 'troll fingers' and can work better than ever. He returns home joyfully and rebuilds the farm with the tomte's help.
moral: Home is not a place but the love that guides you back.

title: The Boy and the Tomte Cap
summary: A burlesque story about a boy who borrows the tomte's invisibility cap and embarks on hair-raising adventures. With the cap's magic, he rescues an enchanted girl from a troll's power and wins both her hand and great riches. The tale follows the classic trickster-hero pattern common in Swedish folklore where a clever human outwits supernatural beings.
full_text: A clever and mischievous boy living on a farm discovers that the old tomte who guards the barn possesses a magical red cap that grants invisibility to anyone who wears it. One night, the boy sneaks into the barn and borrows the cap while the tomte sleeps. With the cap's magic, he embarks on a series of hair-raising adventures. He wanders into the deep forest invisible, spies on the secret meetings of the trolls, and learns of a beautiful girl held captive by a wicked troll king. Using his invisibility, the boy infiltrates the troll mountain, outwits the troll guards, and rescues the enchanted girl from the troll's power. In classic trickster-hero fashion, he uses cleverness rather than strength to overcome each obstacle. He returns home a hero, wins the girl's hand in marriage, and receives great riches from her grateful father. The story is told with humor and warmth, celebrating the cleverness of the common folk.
moral: Cleverness and courage can overcome even the most powerful enemies.

title: Beauty and the Beast (Swedish)
summary: Helena Nyblom's Swedish adaptation of the classic French fairy tale. A merchant's daughter sacrifices herself to live with a hideous Beast to save her father's life. Over time she learns to see past appearance and grows to love the Beast, whose love breaks an enchantment and reveals a handsome prince. Nyblom's version appeared in Bland tomtar och troll (1911) with illustrations by John Bauer.
full_text: Helena Nyblom's Swedish adaptation of the classic French fairy tale by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve and Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont. A wealthy merchant loses his fortune, and his family falls into poverty. While traveling, he loses his way and seeks shelter in a magnificent castle where he picks a rose for his youngest daughter. The castle's master, a terrifying Beast, appears and demands the merchant's life in payment for the stolen rose. The merchant's brave daughter offers herself in her father's place and goes to live in the Beast's castle. Over time, she learns to see past the Beast's frightening appearance and discovers his gentle, kind nature. Through her growing love and acceptance, the Beast's enchantment is broken, revealing a handsome prince. Nyblom's version was published in Bland tomtar och troll (1911) with distinctive illustrations by John Bauer that capture both the terrifying and tender aspects of the story. Her retelling emphasizes the power of inner beauty and the transformative nature of true love.
moral: True beauty lies within, and love has the power to transform even the most beastly appearance.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1911 (Helena Nyblom). Nyblom's collection Djur och människor (1914).

title: The Brooms' Broom, King of All Brooms
summary: Jon, a poor broom-maker living in the forest, decides to bind a magical broom: Kvastarnas kvast, alla kvastars kung. He visits three forest hags for help — Vingmor (flight), Tassmor (bouncing), and Labbmor (striking power). At the Great Mountain, he confronts the troll king, commands his broom to steal his scepter, and rescues lost husbands from the trolls. The broom is sold for enough to build a dream home.
full_text: Jon is a poor broom-maker living in the forest — as poor as a stick but as happy as a green twig. One day he decides to bind a special broom with magical powers: Kvastarnas kvast, alla kvastars kung (The Brooms' Broom, King of All Brooms). He visits three forest hags for help: Vingmor, who fills the broom with 'flight'; Tassmor, who fills it with 'hoppsan' (bouncing/jumping power); and Labbmor, dressed in bearskin with gingerbread eyes and a steaming red mouth, a belt of gnawed skulls and bones, who gives it 'striking power.' At the Great Mountain, Jon confronts the troll king who sits with his scepter. Jon commands his broom to steal the scepter and makes it the broom's handle. He rescues the lost husbands of Vingmor, Tassmor, and Labbmor from the trolls, returns them home, and finally sells the magical broom to a wealthy man for enough money to build a dream home for his family. The broom fills Jon with strength, joy, and good humor for life.
moral: Skill, courage, and a touch of magic can transform a humble life into one of joy.

title: The Fiddler Who Got the Madonna's Golden Shoe
summary: A wandering fiddler (spelman) in medieval Sweden finds himself blessed by a miraculous encounter with the Madonna, who gives him one of her golden shoes as a reward for his devoted music. The tale draws on Catholic and folk motifs, blending Christian iconography with Scandinavian folk music tradition.
full_text: In medieval Sweden, a poor wandering fiddler travels from village to village, playing his music at weddings, feasts, and gatherings. Despite his poverty, his music is filled with such joy and devotion that it seems to come from heaven itself. One evening, as he plays beneath the stars, a radiant vision appears before him — the Madonna herself, accompanied by a heavenly glow. Moved by the purity of his music and his faithful heart, she removes one of her golden shoes and gives it to him as a miraculous gift. The fiddler, overcome with gratitude, continues his journey, now carrying a tangible sign of divine favor. From that day forward, his music takes on an even more enchanting quality, and he is known throughout the land as the fiddler blessed by the Madonna. The tale blends Catholic Marian devotion with the deep Scandinavian tradition of the wandering spelman (folk fiddler), suggesting that art created with pure devotion can attract the attention of the divine.
moral: Art created with pure devotion is a prayer that heaven answers.
source: Bland tomtar och troll, 1915 (Emil Linders). Illustrated by John Bauer.

title: The Treasure Seekers
summary: In Swedish folk belief, those who seek buried treasures must maintain absolute silence or the search will be in vain. Four men form a party to unearth treasures in Josäterdal. As they dig, supernatural illusions appear — an officer, soldiers firing at them, a red calf, a gallows. One digger wears a red shirt that catches the spirits' attention. When a voice cries out 'Shall we begin with him wearing the red shirt?' the man flees in panic.
full_text: It is an established rule in Swedish folk belief that those who seek buried treasures must maintain absolute silence, or the search will be in vain and harm will befall them, body and soul. Four men, aware of this, formed a party to unearth treasures said to be buried in Josäterdal. One midsummer night, as they crossed Lake Sälen, they saw a strange man approaching, dragging a large fir tree behind his boat. Shortly after, another man appeared asking if they had seen any float-wood. Recognizing these rowers as fairies (vättar), the treasure seekers pretended not to hear and continued to Josäterdal without incident. As they began digging into the hillside, a grand officer approached and addressed them, but no one answered. Soon soldiers marched up and fired at them, but they remained undeterred. The illusions escalated — a red calf appeared, the soldiers pressed closer until the diggers stood enveloped in powder-smoke so thick they could not see each other. Finally, a tall gallows was raised on the hillside. One digger wore a red shirt that caught the spirits' attention. A voice cried out, 'Shall we begin with him wearing the red shirt?' At this, the man lost his nerve and fled, the others following in panic. The tale illustrates the folk belief that treasure is guarded by supernatural beings who test the courage and resolve of those who seek it.
moral: Buried treasure is guarded not by locks but by the courage of those who seek it.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1890), Dalarne section.

title: Food for Kings: Oatcake and Herring
summary: When King Charles XI confiscated most noble properties in the Great Reduction of 1680, his aunt Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp was affected. When the king came to visit, she greeted him with a box on the ear, then served him nothing but a herring's tail and an oatcake. When the king offered to return her property, she cut him off and gave him a second box on the ear. The king hastily commanded that she keep her property.
full_text: When King Charles XI of Sweden confiscated most noble properties for the crown in the Great Reduction of 1680, his aunt on his father's side, Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp (daughter of John Casimir, sister of Charles X Gustav), was among those affected. Shortly after the confiscations, the king came to Höjentorp on one of his journeys. As he approached the entrance, the countess met him on the stairs and greeted him with a resounding box on the ear. Astounded, the king exclaimed, 'It is fortunate that it is me that you have struck! But why are you in such a combative mood, my aunt?' 'Why?' replied the countess. 'Because you have taken all my possessions from me.' She led the king to the dining hall and placed before him nothing but a herring's tail and an oatcake. 'Have you no better fare than this?' asked the king. 'No,' answered the lady, 'as you have spread the cloth, so you must dine.' When the king offered to return her gold and silver in exchange for a promise of generous provision until her death, she cut him off: 'Shame on you! Will you not allow me to keep so little as my gold and silver either?' Advancing on him, she gave him a second box on the ear. The king retreated hastily and commanded that the countess was to keep her property until the end of her days.
moral: A bold heart and quick wit can sometimes prevail against even the most powerful authorities.
source: Swedish folk tradition, collected by Herman Hofberg. Associated with Countess Maria Eufrosyna of Höjentorp (17th century).

title: The Plague
summary: In Swedish folk tradition, the devastating plagues were personified as a boy with a rake and a girl with a broom, wandering from village to village. Where the boy raked, some were spared; where the girl swept, death left an empty house. On Soller Island, the plague spirits strewed gold along the roads, but the treasure was so infected that anyone who touched it died before the next sunset.
full_text: Memories of the devastating epidemics that ravaged Sweden still live in folk tradition, transformed into myth over time. During the plague, a boy and a girl were seen wandering from village to village — the boy carrying a rake, the girl a broom. Where the boy used his rake, some were spared from death; but where the girl swept with her broom, death left an empty house. Places neither approached escaped entirely. On Soller Island in Lake Siljan, the plague spirits strewed gold and precious stones along the roads and paths, but the treasure was so infected that anyone who so much as moved a stone with their hand became a corpse before the next sunset. Only two wise old men, Bengh and Harold, survived on the island by resisting the lure of the gold. Many islanders fled northward through the 'Twelve-Mile Roads' toward Vermland. Among the refugees was a young maiden named Malin, who spotted a glittering jewel depicting Christ on the cross. Ignoring her companions' warnings, she picked it up. When they reached their first campsite at Rossberg, Malin fell to her knees in prayer. As the evening sun set behind the mountain, she sank lifeless upon a stone. That stone is still called 'Malin's Church,' and every midsummer the local herdsmen decorate it with fresh leaves and flowers in her memory.
moral: Those who resist the lure of easy riches may survive when others perish.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1890), Dalarne section.

title: Lars, My Lad!
summary: A spendthrift duke, reduced to poverty, discovers a scrap of paper in a hidden chest that summons a magical servant named Lars who grants every wish. He builds a palace, marries a princess, but foolishly gives the paper back to Lars and loses everything, ending up naked in a ruined hut and nearly hanged before tricking Lars into returning the paper.
full_text: A duke who squandered his fortune found shelter in a forest hut, where he discovered a box-within-box containing a paper reading 'Lars, my lad!' Speaking these words summoned an invisible servant who provided food, bed, a palace, and servants. The king noticed the new palace and sent soldiers, but Lars provided the duke with a larger army. The king's soldiers told the duke of the princess, and Lars brought her sleeping to the duke so he could see her beauty. The duke married her, but when Lars asked for the paper as payment, the duke agreed. Lars vanished with everything, leaving the duke naked in the old hut. The angry king ordered the duke hanged, but the princess bribed the executioner to spare him. While hanging, Lars arrived with seven cartloads of worn-out shoes, mocking the duke while holding up the paper. The duke snatched it back, commanded Lars to restore everything, and reclaimed his palace and wife. When the king came to investigate, the duke denied everything. Later, Lars asked for leave, and they buried the paper seven ells underground under a stone. The duke ruled wisely and lived happily ever after.
moral: Wisdom comes from experience; one should not give away what is valuable, nor take good fortune for granted.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Sausage
summary: A mysterious lady borrows a woman's brewing pan and grants her three wishes in return. Eager to please her hungry husband, the woman wishes for a sausage—then her angry husband wishes it stuck to her nose. Their last wish must be used to remove it.
full_text: An old woman alone in her cottage lent her brewing pan to a fine lady, who returned it two days later and granted her three wishes. The woman wanted to wait for her husband to decide what to wish for, but seeing only sour milk and hard bread for his supper, she sighed 'I wish I had that sausage'—and a sausage appeared. When her husband came home and heard she had wasted a wish on a sausage, he shouted 'I wish the sausage were sticking to your nose!' It stuck fast. No pulling could remove it. With one wish left, they realized they could not enjoy riches with a sausage on the woman's nose. The husband finally wished his wife rid of the sausage, and it vanished. They danced with joy.
moral: Haste and thoughtless wishing can lead to trouble; sometimes the simplest solution is the best.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Old Woman and the Tramp
summary: A clever tramp talks a stingy old woman into giving him shelter, then tricks her into contributing ingredients one by one to make 'nail broth'—starting with a nail and adding oatmeal, beef, potatoes, barley, milk, and brandy until they feast together.
full_text: A tramp traveling through a forest met an old woman who grudgingly let him stay the night. She claimed to have no food, but the tramp offered to make 'nail broth' with a four-inch nail. He put the nail in a pot of water and stirred. He hinted it needed oatmeal—she fetched some. Then he hinted at salted beef and potatoes—she brought them. Then barley and milk—she supplied those too. Finally he said the king and queen always took a dram and sandwich with this soup—she brought out brandy, butter, cheese, and smoked meats. They feasted together, and she gave him a silver dollar the next morning, grateful to learn how to make broth from a nail. The tramp replied, 'It isn't very difficult, if one only has something good to add to it.'
moral: Generosity rewarded through cleverness; those who give nothing receive nothing, while those who contribute share the feast.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: What Shall Baby's Name Be?
summary: A well-to-do couple's daughter Peggy finally gets a suitor, but when she goes to the cellar for beer she becomes so preoccupied with what to name her future first baby that she, her mother, and her father all become lost in thought, causing the impatient suitor to leave forever.
full_text: A couple with one daughter, Peggy, despaired that no suitor came despite her large dowry. Finally Peter South-farm arrived to propose. While Peggy was sent to fetch beer from the cellar, she began pondering what to name her first baby. She stood outside the cellar lost in thought. Her mother went to find her and also became absorbed in the naming question. Her father went next and joined them in pondering. Meanwhile the suitor sat alone, grew angry thinking they were mocking him, took his hat and left. As he passed, the father called out they were deciding the name of Peggy's first baby. The suitor replied 'The baby will not be called after me!' and departed, never to return.
moral: Do not let future concerns distract from present opportunities; strike while the iron is hot.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: St. Peter and the Two Women
summary: St. Peter, disguised as a poor traveler, is turned away by a rich farmer's wife but welcomed by a poor widow. He blesses the widow so that whatever she starts doing she continues all day—she measures cloth that grows endlessly and becomes wealthy. The greedy rich woman demands the same blessing but begins pumping water and drowns.
full_text: St. Peter traveling on earth asked shelter at a rich farm but was refused. He went to a poor widow's cottage and was welcomed. Before leaving, he said 'The first thing you do today you shall be doing all the day.' She began measuring a piece of cloth, which grew endlessly—she measured all day and sold three cartloads, becoming rich. The rich woman heard this and when St. Peter later came to her, she demanded the same blessing. After he left, she began pumping water but could not stop—she pumped all day as water rose around her. The water reached her chin and she drowned, her yard measure and cloth floating on the water.
moral: Kindness to strangers is rewarded; greed and inhospitality bring ruin.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Old Woman and the Fish
summary: An old woman catches a talking pike that grants three wishes: that her pails fetch water by themselves, that whatever she strikes breaks right off, and that whatever she pulls becomes long. Each wish backfires—she slaps her knees in delight and breaks her legs off, then blows her nose and it grows terribly long.
full_text: An old woman living alone by a town pulled a talking pike from her well. The fish offered three wishes. She wished: 1) her pails would fetch water by themselves, 2) whatever she struck would break right off, 3) whatever she pulled would become long. All came true. The pails went up the hill by themselves. Delighted, she slapped her knees—'Crack!'—both legs fell off. She began to cry and blew her nose in her apron, pulling so hard that her nose grew terribly long. She still sits on the well lid to this day.
themes: be careful what you wish for
moral: Think carefully before making wishes, for each may have unintended consequences.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Valiant Chanticleer
summary: A childless woman is given an egg to hatch in her bosom, but out comes a cockerel. He grows enormous, ploughs the fields with a single ox, then sets off to win the king's daughter. He gathers a fox, wolf, bear, and lake as companions, defeats the king's geese, cattle, and horses, survives being roasted, and finally the princess wrings his neck—releasing a handsome prince.
full_text: A childless woman was given an egg by an old woman. She kept it in her bosom and hatched a cockerel. He grew huge, ploughed the field with one ox, and decided to marry the king's daughter. He took a soup-ladle as a conveyance and gathered traveling companions: a fox, wolf, bear, and a lake. At the palace, the king refused and set geese on him—he called the fox who killed all geese. He set cattle on him—he called the wolf who killed all cattle. He set horses on him—he called the bear who killed all horses. The king put him on a spit to roast—he called the lake who put out the fire. Half-roasted, he was served to the king who swallowed him whole, but the cock revived in his stomach and crowed until the king vomited him. The cock then perched on the crown and crowed until the king surrendered the princess. She was forced to marry him but cried bitterly. The cock asked her to wring his neck—she did, and he transformed into a handsome prince. A great wedding followed.
moral: Do not judge by appearances; true worth may be hidden beneath an ugly exterior.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Twigmuntus, Cowbelliantus, Perchnosius
summary: A clever farmer's lad determines to win the king's daughter by outwitting the king and his seven learned professors. On his journey, a twig hits him (Twigmuntus), a cow bellows (Cowbelliantus), and a perch bites his nose (Perchnosius). When the professors finish their learned gibberish, he stumps them with these three nonsense words.
full_text: A very learned king issued an edict: any man who could silence the king and his seven professors would win the princess and half the kingdom; those who failed would lose their heads. Many tried and died. A farmer's son studied with the parson but soon tired of books, saying luck matters more than learning. On his way to the palace, a twig struck his mouth—he called it 'Twigmuntus.' A cow bellowed—he called it 'Cowbelliantus.' A perch bit his nose as he swam a river—he called it 'Perchnosius.' At the palace, the professors delivered long learned harangues. The lad nodded to all of it. When asked to reply, he said he could prattle like them as a baby, then asked: 'Twigmuntus, Cowbelliantus, Perchnosius? Can you give me an answer to that?' The professors searched books, stared at the ceiling, meditated—but could not answer. Nor could the king. The lad won the princess and half the kingdom and ruled in his own way.
moral: Native wit can triumph over bookish learning; sometimes the simplest answer confounds the most learned.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Lad and the Fox
summary: A lad on his way to church sees a fox sleeping on a stone and begins daydreaming about killing it, selling the skin, buying rye, and shouting at passersby to stay off his field—but he screams so loudly in his fantasy that he wakes the fox, which escapes.
full_text: A lad on his way to church saw a fox sleeping on a big stone. He picked up a heavy stone and planned: 'If I kill the fox and sell the skin, I'll buy rye and sow it in father's field. When churchgoers pass, they'll admire my rye. I'll tell them to keep away. They won't listen. I'll shout louder. Then they'll listen.' But he screamed so loudly that the fox woke up and fled into the forest, and the lad got nothing.
moral: Do not count your chickens before they are hatched; of undone deeds you should never screech.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Old Nick and the Girl
summary: A poor girl mad for dancing makes a deal with Old Nick (the Devil) for leather dancing shoes. After a year, she tricks him into a race around Lake Fryken by using her identical twin sister at both ends of the lake, wearing out Old Nick's shoes while proving her bark shoes superior.
full_text: A poor girl loved dancing so much she nearly went mad at the sound of a fiddle. At Amberg Heath fair, Old Nick offered her fine German leather dancing shoes. She could have them for a year free if she danced in his interest, after which she would belong to him. A year later, Old Nick came to claim her. She claimed the shoes had no spring and challenged him to a race around Lake Fryken—she in bark shoes, he in the leather ones. She secretly had an identical twin sister. One sister stood at Frykstad (south end), the other at Fryksend (north end). Old Nick raced frantically but found 'the girl' at both ends each time. They raced three times; each time Old Nick arrived exhausted to find her already there. His leather shoes were destroyed and his feet were bleeding. Defeated, he freed her. The girl never danced again.
moral: Cleverness and quick thinking can defeat even the Devil himself.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Stone Statue
summary: A man who blasphemed in a churchyard was turned to stone. After many parsons failed to save him, a new parson brings the statue into his study and prays daily. The statue laughs during evening prayers and explains that the parson's quarrels with his wife invite imps into the house. The parson reforms, the imps leave, and the statue finds peace.
full_text: Two men crossed a churchyard. One said 'God's peace to all who rest here.' The other replied 'They lie as they have made their bed, and they get what they deserve'—and was instantly turned to stone. Many parsons tried to release him but failed. A new, learned but quick-tempered parson had the statue brought to his study. During evening prayers, the statue tittered. On the third night, the parson asked why. The statue explained that when the parson and his wife quarreled, little imps swarmed the house; during prayers they fled, and a limping little imp hobbled out so comically that the statue couldn't help laughing. The parson became kinder to his wife; the imps vanished. The statue then found peace, said it had done a good deed, and asked for one last prayer. The parson prayed, the statue became flesh and blood, drew a last breath, and was buried respectfully.
moral: A harmonious home leaves no room for evil; harsh words invite mischief.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Artful Lad
summary: A clever servant lad helps a poor farmer by exposing his unfaithful wife who feeds the neighbor instead of her husband. The lad tricks the neighbor into giving him grain, repeatedly exhumes a spying old woman (the neighbor's mother-in-law) who dies in a chest, and manages three funerals before finally burying her for good.
full_text: A poor farmer had a lazy wife who fed their neighbor while giving her husband only water-gruel. The farmer hired a clever lad. On the first day, the lad hid in a settle-bed and saw the wife feed the neighbor. He marked the path so the wife brought dinner to her husband instead. The lad then tricked the neighbor into giving two barrels of rye, half a barrel of peas, and a quartern of wheat as hush money. The neighbor's wife then hid her mother in a chest to spy. The lad forced it open, found the old woman dead (with pancake in her mouth), and closed it again. The neighbor's wife had three funerals for her mother because the lad kept digging her up and moving her—to the cellar, to the pea-bin, and finally riding a foal past the parson—until the neighbor paid the lad 100 dollars to bury her permanently. The lad refused further payment and went to work for the parson.
moral: A clever servant can right wrongs that the master cannot, but even the best trickster eventually moves on.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: All I Possess!
summary: A miserly farmer marries a woman who supposedly eats almost nothing. Suspicious, he spies on her three times—down the chimney, inside a feather bed, and in a beer barrel. Each time she outwits him (with smoke, beating, and scalding lye). On his deathbed, his cries of 'All I possess!' are interpreted as his final wish that she inherit everything.
full_text: A miserly farmer's first wife died from starvation. He married a woman who supposedly ate only half a pea per meal. Suspicious, he climbed the roof to look down the chimney—his wife built a fire that nearly suffocated him. He hid inside a feather bed—she beat it with hazel sticks. He hid in a beer barrel in the cellar—she poured boiling juniper lye into it, scalding him horribly. On his deathbed, seeing the wife prepare food for the parson, he screamed 'All I possess!' in agony. The wife told the parson her husband meant she should inherit all. The parson and clerk witnessed it as his last will. The wife inherited everything and later married the lad on the farm.
moral: Stinginess and suspicion lead to suffering; a clever spouse will always outwit a miser.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Katie Grey
summary: The Devil himself cannot sow discord between a loving couple, so he enlists the witch Katie Grey. She tricks the wife into trying a 'charm' (cutting hairs from her sleeping husband's beard) and convinces the husband that his wife will cut his throat. The misunderstanding leads to both spouses killing each other. Even the Devil is terrified of Katie Grey.
full_text: A loving couple had lived 36 years without a harsh word. Old Nick (the Devil) tried everything to cause discord but failed. He hired Katie Grey, a wicked witch. She told the wife to cut three hairs from her sleeping husband's beard while drawing a razor along a strop to keep his love. She told the husband that his wife would cut his throat that night and gave him an axe. That night, the wife approached with the razor; the husband seized the axe and struck her dead. Realizing what he'd done, he cut his own throat. Katie Grey laughed outside the window. Old Nick came with her promised payment (jacket and petticoat) on a long pole, but when she asked him to come closer, he cried 'No, keep away from me!' and fled, throwing the clothes at her. Two white pigeons (the souls of the couple) flew up to heaven.
moral: Even the Devil fears a truly wicked woman; malicious gossip can destroy what even Satan cannot.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Cock and the Crested Hen
summary: A vain little crested hen thinks herself too good for the other farmyard hens and goes off alone against the cock's warnings. A hawk swoops down and nearly catches her; she escapes but loses her finest feathers, learning humility.
full_text: A cock had a farmyard of hens, among them a tiny crested hen who thought herself too grand for the others. She wanted to strut alone. The cock cried 'Go not there!' and the old hens cackled warnings, but she left anyway. A hawk circled and swooped down. The cock saw him and cried for help. People came running and frightened the hawk away, but he had already plucked her tuft and finest feathers. The humbled hen ran home crying 'See how I look!' The cock said 'Didn't I tell you?' From then on she stayed with the others on the dust-heap.
moral: Pride goes before a fall; heed the wisdom of those who have seen more of the world.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Old Nick and the Pedlar
summary: A struggling pedlar makes a bargain with Old Nick: he will sell all he buys, but if his bags ever become empty he belongs to the Devil. Business booms until a buyer (Old Nick in disguise) purchases the entire lot. The pedlar escapes by claiming Katie Grey is in one of the bags, and Old Nick—terrified of her—flees.
full_text: A pedlar whose business was failing met Old Nick and made a bargain: he would sell all he bought, but if his bags ever became empty he would belong to the Devil. The pedlar bought goods on credit and business boomed, though he always kept something in his bags. At Hinnersmess fair, business was so brisk he had to leave his stall. A man asked his mate to buy the entire lot—horse, cart, bags and all—for 600 crowns. The man (Old Nick) said he would collect at night. The pedlar despaired until a woman (Katie Grey) offered to help. When Old Nick came, the pedlar pointed to a big bag and said it was part of the lot. He opened it—Katie Grey peeped out. Old Nick screamed, released the pedlar from his bond, and flew up through the chimney, carrying off the roof.
moral: Even the Devil has limits—and those limits are called Katie Grey.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: Why the Executioner is Called Assessor
summary: An executioner on his way to an execution discovers a robbers' den in the forest. After completing his duty, he tells the king, who disguises himself as a tramp and together they defeat twelve robbers. In gratitude, the king decrees that executioners shall be called 'Assessor' and treated with respect.
full_text: An executioner walking to an execution discovered a robbers' den when a woman's head appeared through a trap door. He marked the trees and continued. After performing the execution, he told the king. The king, bold and strong, insisted they two should capture the robbers alone. They disguised themselves as ragged tramps, hid a sword and an axe, and met the twelve robbers in the forest. Claiming to be starving beggars, they were taken to the cave and fed. When the robbers had eaten, the executioner and king pushed the table against the robbers, pinned them to the wall, threw brandy in their eyes, and killed all twelve. The king offered the treasure, but the executioner asked that his profession be made respectable. The king issued an edict that executioners be called 'Assessor' and wear a three-cornered hat, and no one should spit after them.
moral: Every honest profession deserves respect, no matter how unpleasant its duties.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Parson and the Clerk
summary: A miserly parson who refuses charity disguises himself as a tramp to turn away beggars. The clever clerk, also in disguise, torments the parson all night by pretending his corn has been spilled and his cattle let loose, while impersonating the parson to his wife. The terrified parson reforms and becomes generous.
full_text: A miserly parson dressed as a tramp and sat in his kitchen to turn away beggars. The clerk, also disguised as a tramp, came seeking shelter. The wife sent him to the clerk's house, but the clerk claimed it was full. He insisted on staying and was put to sleep in the brew-house with the other 'tramp' (the parson). The clerk took the bed, making the parson sleep on the bench. He repeatedly snuck out, then woke the parson claiming he had: 1) made a hole in the wood-shed loft so corn spilled among the firewood, 2) let out all the cattle. Each time the parson rushed out to fix it. Meanwhile the clerk went to the house, imitated the parson's voice, and rested by the fire with the wife. Finally the clerk claimed he had put ditchwater in the beer barrel. The parson, exhausted and reformed, ordered the barrel sent to the clerk. The parson became a generous man who never refused the poor.
moral: Stinginess brings more trouble than generosity; a night of misery can cure a lifetime of greed.
source: Baron G. Djurklou, Fairy Tales from the Swedish (1901), translated by H.L. Brækstad.

title: The Giant Maiden in Boråseröd Mountain
summary: A young peasant captures a beautiful giant's daughter by throwing his knife between her and the mountain. At the wedding feast the giant eats until half full, ensuring only half the couple's descendants become great. After years of abuse the wife reveals her supernatural strength, teaching her husband true humility.
full_text:
In the mountain of Boråseröd, which is located in the parish of Svarteborg, there lived, in ancient days, a giant.
As with all the giant people, he has disappeared since the coming in of Christianity.
Some say that he died, but others believe that he moved to Dovre, in Norway, where
giants betook themselves when disturbed by the church bells.

However, there is even to-day a hollow in the mountain which is called “the giant’s
door,” and within the mountain, it is believed there are vaults filled with the giant’s
gold. No one has, however, dared venture to search for this treasure, and luckily,
for with property of giants, blessings do not go.

This giant had a daughter, so beautiful that he who once saw her could never drive
thoughts of her from his mind. Among the few whose fortune it was to see her was a
young peasant from the estate of Rom, adjacent to the mountain. When he was one day
out searching for the horses, which had gone astray, he suddenly came upon the wonderfully
beautiful maiden, sitting upon the side of the mountain, in the sunshine, playing
