full_text: Once there was a boy whose appetite was legendary; he could eat more than three grown men. One afternoon, he encountered a troll who boasted that no human could ever out-eat him. The troll challenged the boy to a match: whoever stopped eating first would become the servant of the other. The troll brought out mountains of meat, cakes, and porridge. The boy, knowing he couldn't eat as much as a troll, had secretly sewn a large pocket into his coat and filled it with a magic sponge that absorbed food. As the troll gorged himself, the boy pushed much of his meal into the pocket, appearing to swallow it all. Hours passed, and the troll began to feel sluggish and full. The boy, still appearing hungry, asked for another platter. The troll, overwhelmed and defeated, admitted his loss. He gave the boy a bag of gold and a magical sword, terrified by the boy's seemingly bottomless stomach.
moral: Intelligence and strategy can overcome raw power and size.

title: The Three Princesses of Whiteland
summary: A brave youth journeys to the distant kingdom of Whiteland to win the hand of one of three princesses. He must pass three impossible tests of courage and wisdom, aided by the guidance of a mysterious forest spirit.
full_text: In the frozen north lay Whiteland, where the three princesses were said to be as cold as the ice they lived upon. To win their favor, a suitor had to bring a flower that bloomed in winter, a stone that floated on water, and a song that could wake the dead. Many princes failed and were cast into the dungeons. A poor shepherd boy, guided by a talking fox, learned the secrets of the land. The fox told him to find the Sun-Flower in the deepest cavern, to carve the Floating Stone from the Heart-Mountain, and to learn the Song of Life from the wind. The boy succeeded in all three tasks, not through strength, but by listening to the nature of the world. The princesses, touched by his humility and persistence, chose him as their champion. He married the youngest princess, and together they brought a permanent spring to the land of Whiteland.
moral: Humility and a willingness to listen to the world are the keys to overcoming impossible odds.

title: Not a Pin to Choose Between Them
summary: A humorous story about two brothers who are so identical in appearance and temperament that no one can tell them apart. This symmetry leads to a series of comical misunderstandings, eventually forcing them to find their own unique identities.
full_text: There were once two brothers, Ole and Per, who were so alike that their own mother could barely tell them apart. They dressed the same, spoke with the same cadence, and even sneezed in unison. The village said there was 'not a pin to choose between them.' One day, a wealthy merchant offered a reward to anyone who could distinguish the two. Various tests were tried: one was asked to paint, the other to sing, but they performed both tasks with identical mediocrity. The confusion reached a peak when both brothers fell in love with the same woman, and she spent weeks talking to one while believing it was the other. Eventually, a great storm struck the village, and during the chaos, Ole saved a child from a river while Per organized the rescue party. In the heat of the crisis, their true natures emerged—one was a man of action, the other a man of leadership. They realized that while they looked the same, their souls were different, and they lived the rest of their lives as distinct individuals.
moral: External similarity does not define a person; true identity is revealed through action.

title: One's Own Children Are Always Prettiest
summary: A satirical look at parental bias through the eyes of several families who insist their children are the most beautiful in the world. A neutral judge is brought in to settle the dispute, leading to a lesson about the nature of love and perception.
full_text: In a valley of competing farmers, a great argument broke out over whose child was the most beautiful. Each parent claimed their child was a masterpiece of nature, while the other children were merely 'adequate.' The dispute became so heated that the village council appointed a blind sage to be the judge, believing that only someone who could not see could be truly impartial. The sage did not look at the children; instead, he asked them to speak and act. He listened to their kindness, their honesty, and their willingness to help others. In the end, the sage declared that every child was the most beautiful in the eyes of the person who loved them most. He explained that beauty is not a measurement of features, but a reflection of the love the parent holds for the child. The parents, humbled by the wisdom, ceased their fighting and celebrated the diversity of their children.
moral: Love creates its own definition of beauty, and that is the only definition that truly matters.

title: How One Went Out to Woo
summary: A clumsy but well-meaning man attempts to find a wife, encountering a series of absurd obstacles and eccentric characters. Through sheer luck and a refusal to be discouraged, he eventually finds a partner who appreciates his oddities.
full_text: Once there was a man who decided he was tired of loneliness and went out to woo a bride. He was not a handsome man, nor was he wealthy, but he had a heart full of hope and a tendency to trip over his own feet. He visited the first village and proposed to the daughter of the mayor, but he accidentally knocked over the wedding cake and was chased out with a broom. He visited the second village and attempted to impress a lady with a poem, but he forgot the words and started singing a lullaby to a goat instead. Just as he was about to give up, he met a woman who was just as clumsy as he was; she had accidentally set fire to her own kitchen while trying to bake bread. They looked at each other, laughed at their shared misfortunes, and realized they were perfect for one another. They married in a small ceremony where the cake was slightly lopsided, but the love was perfectly straight.
moral: You don't need to be perfect to find someone who loves you; you just need to find someone whose imperfections match your own.

title: The Three Aunts
summary: A story about a young man who inherits a strange set of rules from his three aunts. To claim his inheritance, he must spend a year following their eccentric whims, leading to a series of surreal events and personal growth.
full_text: A young man named Erik inherited a vast estate, but only on the condition that he live with his three aunts for one full year. The aunts were peculiar: one only spoke in riddles, one only walked backward, and the third only ate foods that were blue. Erik found the conditions maddening, but the promise of wealth kept him there. Over the year, the aunts' oddities began to teach him lessons. The riddle-speaker taught him to look for hidden meanings; the backward-walker taught him to consider the past before moving forward; and the blue-eater taught him to find joy in the unusual. By the time the year ended, Erik no longer cared about the money. He had developed a deep affection for his aunts and a philosophical outlook on life. He used the inheritance to build a sanctuary for eccentric people, ensuring that no one would ever be judged for being different.
moral: Embracing the strange and unconventional in others can lead to the most profound personal growth.

title: The Widow's Son
summary: The youngest son of a widow is underestimated by his brothers and father, but possesses a kindness and curiosity that leads him to magical discoveries. He eventually saves his family from ruin through his generosity and bravery.
full_text: In a small hamlet lived a widow and her three sons. The two eldest were strong and arrogant, while the youngest was quiet and kind. The elders mocked him, calling him 'the dreamer.' One day, the family's livestock fell ill, and the farm faced bankruptcy. The eldest sons tried to solve the problem with force and medicine, but failed. The youngest son went into the forest to seek the help of the Old Sage. Along the way, he shared his last piece of bread with a starving crow and helped a trapped squirrel. In gratitude, the animals led him to the Sage, who gave him a magical salve that cured the livestock and a seed that grew into a tree of gold. The youngest son used the wealth to save the farm and forgive his brothers, proving that the quietest heart often holds the greatest strength.
moral: Kindness to all creatures is the surest path to success and happiness.

title: The Two Step-Sisters
summary: A kind-hearted girl is mistreated by her cruel stepmother and step-sisters, who force her to do all the hard work. Through her perseverance and the help of magical elements, she manages to overcome their malice, proving her inner beauty and virtue, and eventually finds her rightful place of happiness and honor.
full_text:
THE TWO STEP-SISTERS

Once on a time there was a poor man who had a daughter, and she was the loveliest child in the whole world. But when the girl was still a little child, her mother died, and her father took a wife; snip and this wife had two daughters of her own, and they were as ugly as two toads, and as cross as two sticks. Now the step-mother and her daughters had a great hate for the poor girl, and they treated her so badly that she was forced to do all the hard work in the house, while they sat and did nothing. 

One day the step-mother said to the girl, 'Go out and pick a bushel of lentils, but mind you don't leave a single one behind.' The girl went out and began to pick, but the step-sisters stood by and laughed at her, and they threw the lentils all over the ground, so that she had to start again and again. But the girl did not complain; snip she just kept picking until the bushel was full. 

Then the step-mother said, 'Now go and clean the whole house, and not a speck of dust must be left.' The girl worked and worked, but the step-sisters kept making a mess, throwing ashes and mud on the floor. But the girl did not give up; snip she cleaned and cleaned until the house was sparkling. 

At last the step-mother decided that the girl must go and seek the magic flower that grows on the highest peak of the mountain; snip for she knew that the flower could grant any wish, but it was a dangerous journey, and the step-mother hoped the girl would never return. The girl set off, and on her way she helped an old woman who was trapped under a fallen tree. In gratitude, the old woman gave her a magic ring and told her that whenever she was in trouble, she should just turn the ring and call for help. 

Eventually, the girl reached the peak and plucked the magic flower. When she returned home, the step-mother and step-sisters were furious that she had succeeded. They tried to steal the flower from her, but the magic ring protected her. The flower revealed the girl's true virtue and the step-sisters' cruelty to the whole village. The king, hearing of the girl's kindness and bravery, invited her to the palace and made her his queen, while the step-mother and her daughters were left to their own misery, forever haunted by the kindness they had tried to destroy.
moral: Inner beauty and kindness always triumph over outer ugliness and cruelty.

title: The Cock and Hen a-Nutting
summary: A cock and a hen venture out to gather nuts in the forest, where they encounter a dangerous troll. Using their wit and coordination, they manage to trick the troll and escape his clutches, illustrating that intelligence and teamwork can overcome brute strength and predatory threats.
full_text:
THE COCK AND HEN A-NUTTING

Once on a time a cock and a hen went out a-nutting. They walked through the forest, gathering the finest hazel nuts into their baskets. But as they were returning home, they came upon a great, ugly troll who lived in a cave. The troll had a huge appetite and decided that the cock and hen would make a wonderful meal. 

'Stop right there!' roared the troll. 'I shall gobble you both up!' 

The cock, being the braver of the two, stepped forward and said, 'Wait a moment, Mr. Troll! We can offer you something far more delicious than ourselves. If you let us go, we will return tomorrow with a feast that will make your eyes pop!' 

Intrigued and greedy, the troll agreed, but warned them that if they did not return, he would hunt them down and eat them whole. The cock and hen hurried home and spent the night planning their escape. They gathered a large amount of sticky pine resin and a pile of heavy stones. 

The next morning, they returned to the troll's cave, carrying a large pot covered with a cloth. 'Here is your feast!' the cock shouted. 'But it is so precious that we must seal it tight.' 

As the troll leaned over the pot, the cock pulled the cloth away, and a cloud of sticky resin flew into the troll's face, gluing his eyes and nose shut. While the troll struggled to see, the hen began throwing the heavy stones at him, and the cock pecked at his toes. Confused and blinded, the troll stumbled back into his own cave and tripped over a large boulder, knocking himself unconscious. 

With a triumphant crow, the cock led the hen safely back to their farm, and they lived the rest of their days in peace, always remembering that a quick wit is more powerful than a giant's strength.
moral: Intelligence and quick thinking can defeat even the strongest enemy.

title: Why the Bear Is Stumpy-Tailed
summary: In this origin story, a cunning fox tricks a gullible bear into believing he can catch fish by leaving his tail in a frozen pond overnight. The bear's tail freezes solid, and when he tries to pull it out, it snaps off, explaining why bears have short, stumpy tails today.
full_text:
WHY THE BEAR IS STUMPY-TAILED

Long ago, when the animals could speak, the bear had a long, beautiful tail that he was very proud of. He would wave it back and forth, boasting to all the other creatures about its magnificence. The fox, who was as cunning as they come, decided to play a trick on the bear to humble his pride. 

One cold winter's day, the fox approached the bear and said, 'Bruin, have you ever tried the new way of fishing? It is the easiest method in the world. All you have to do is go to the frozen pond, make a small hole in the ice, and sit very still with your tail lowered into the water. The fish are so attracted to a long, furry tail that they will bite onto it in droves. By morning, you will have a tail full of fish!' 

 The bear, eager for an easy meal and blinded by his own greed, followed the fox's advice. He went to the pond, cut a hole in the ice, and lowered his magnificent tail into the freezing water. He sat there for hours, shivering in the cold, but he remained patient, imagining the feast that awaited him. 

As the night grew colder, the water around his tail began to freeze. The ice gripped the fur in a tight, frozen hold. When the sun rose the next morning, the bear decided he had waited long enough. He gave a mighty heave and pulled with all his strength. But instead of a catch of fish, there was a loud snap! The ice had frozen so solidly that the bear's tail snapped off at the root. 

Bruin roared in pain and anger, but the fox only laughed and laughed. From that day on, the bear has had only a short, stumpy tail, as a lasting reminder to never trust a fox's fishing tips.
moral: Do not let pride and greed blind you to the deception of others.

title: Bruin and Reynard
summary: A series of encounters between Bruin the Bear and Reynard the Fox, where the fox's superior intelligence and deceptive nature allow him to repeatedly outsmart the stronger but slower-witted bear, often leading the bear into ridiculous and embarrassing situations.
full_text:
BRUIN AND REYNARD

The Bear and the Fox had once bought a firkin of butter together; snip they were to have it at Yule and hid it till then under a thick spruce bush. After that they went a little way off and lay down on a sunny bank to sleep. So when they had lain a while the Fox got up, shook himself, and bawled out 'yes'. 

Then he ran off straight to the firkin and ate a good third part of it. But when he came back, and the Bear asked him where he had been, since he was so fat about the paunch, he said: 'Don’t you believe then that I was bidden to barsel, to a christening feast.' 

'So, so', said the Bear, 'and pray what was the bairn’s name.' 

'Just-begun', said the Fox. 

So they lay down to sleep again. In a little while up jumped the Fox again, bawled out 'yes', and ran off to the firkin. This time too he ate a good lump. When he came back, and the Bear asked him again where he had been, he said: 'Oh, wasn’t I bidden to barsel again, don’t you think.' 

'And pray what was the bairn’s name this time', asked the Bear. 

'Half-eaten', said the Fox. 

The Bear thought that a very queer name, but he hadn’t wondered long over it before he began to yawn and gape and fell asleep. Well, he hadn’t lain long before the Fox jumped up as he had done twice before, bawled out 'yes' and ran off to the firkin, which this time he cleared right out. When he got back he had been bidden to barsel again, and when the Bear wanted to know the bairn’s name, he answered: 'Licked-to-the-bottom.' 

After that they lay down again, and slept a long time; snip but then they were to go to the firkin to look at the butter, and when they found it eaten up, the Bear threw the blame on the Fox, and the Fox on the Bear; snip and each said the one had been at the firkin while the other slept. 

'Well, well', said Reynard, 'we’ll soon find this out, which of us has eaten the butter. We’ll just lay down in the sunshine, and he whose cheeks and chaps are greasiest when we wake, he is the thief.' 

Yes, that trial Bruin was ready to stand; snip and as he knew in his heart he had never so much as tasted the butter, he lay down without a care to sleep in the sun. Then Reynard stole off to the firkin for a morsel of butter, which stuck there in a crack, and then he crept back to the Bear, and greased his chaps and cheeks with it; snip and then he, too, lay down to sleep as if nothing had happened. 

So when they both woke, the sun had melted the butter, and the Bear’s whiskers were all greasy; snip and so it was Bruin after all, and no one else, who had eaten the butter.
moral: Do not trust those who use flattery and trickery to hide their own faults.

title: Gudbrand on the Hill-side
summary: Gudbrand, a poor and humble youth, embarks on a journey and helps various animals and spirits along the way. His kindness and selflessness are rewarded when he is guided to a hidden treasure and the heart of a beautiful princess, proving that a good heart is the greatest asset.
full_text:
GUDBRAND ON THE HILL-SIDE

In a small village nestled in the valleys of Norway lived a youth named Gudbrand. He was poor in possessions but rich in spirit, known throughout the land for his kindness and his willingness to help anyone in need. One day, feeling a call to explore the wider world, Gudbrand set off on a journey, carrying nothing but a small bag of bread and a heart full of hope. 

As he climbed the high hills, he encountered a small bird with a broken wing. Instead of continuing his journey, Gudbrand knelt in the grass and carefully tended to the bird's wing, sharing his precious bread to keep the bird strong. Once healed, the bird spoke to him in a human voice, thanking him for his mercy and telling him that kindness is never wasted. 

Further along his path, Gudbrand found an old man struggling to carry a heavy load of firewood through a storm. Despite the biting cold and the wind, Gudbrand spent the entire day helping the old man reach his cottage, refusing any payment for his toil. The old man, who was actually a powerful mountain spirit in disguise, blessed Gudbrand, granting him the ability to understand the language of all living things. 

Guided by the animals he had helped, Gudbrand eventually discovered a hidden valley where a beautiful princess was held captive by an ancient curse. To break the curse, one had to perform three acts of selfless sacrifice. Gudbrand did not hesitate; snip he gave away his only cloak to warm a shivering lamb, shared his last drop of water with a dying flower, and offered his own sleep to watch over the princess's safety. 

As the third sacrifice was made, the curse shattered, and the valley bloomed into a paradise. The princess, touched by Gudbrand's purity and selflessness, fell in love with him. Together they returned to the village, where Gudbrand used the hidden treasures of the valley to help all the poor and needy, living the rest of his days in peace and prosperity.
moral: Selflessness and kindness to all living beings lead to the greatest rewards.

title: Katie Woodencloak
summary: Katie, a girl born with a wooden cloak that makes her an object of ridicule, endures hardship and loneliness. However, her resilience and the hidden magical properties of her cloak eventually lead her to a destiny of love and royalty, showing that true value lies beneath the surface.
full_text:
KATIE WOODENCLOAK

Once there was a girl named Katie who was born with a strange and heavy cloak made of dark, polished wood. The cloak was fused to her shoulders, and no matter how hard her parents tried, they could not remove it. Because of this, the other children in the village called her 'Katie Woodencloak' and mocked her for her appearance, claiming she was more like a tree than a human. 

Katie spent most of her days in the forest, where she felt more at home among the towering pines and ancient oaks. She discovered that her wooden cloak had a mysterious property: it could blend perfectly with the trees, making her invisible to those who sought to do her harm. Despite the loneliness and the cruel words of the villagers, Katie remained gentle and compassionate, often helping lost travelers find their way back to the path. 

One winter, a great famine struck the land, and the king announced that he would marry whoever could find the legendary Golden Seed, which could grow a crop of grain in a single night. Many brave knights and cunning merchants set out, but none returned. Katie, wanting to help her starving village, decided to seek the seed herself. 

Using her cloak to hide from the predators of the wild, Katie navigated the deepest parts of the enchanted forest. She encountered a series of trials—riddles posed by a talking raven and a bridge guarded by a stone giant—but her patience and honest heart allowed her to pass. Finally, she reached the Heart of the World and retrieved the Golden Seed. 

When she returned to the palace and presented the seed to the king, the wooden cloak suddenly shattered into a thousand pieces, revealing a girl of breathtaking beauty. The king was amazed not only by her appearance but by her courage and purity of spirit. Katie and the prince fell deeply in love, and their wedding was the grandest the kingdom had ever seen, proving to all that the most precious gems are often hidden behind the roughest shells.
moral: True worth is found in character and heart, not in outward appearance.

title: The Fox as Herdsman
summary: A farmer hires a fox to be a herdsman for his sheep, believing the fox's promises of efficiency. However, the fox uses his cunning to deceive the farmer, stealing the sheep one by one while making the farmer believe the sheep are disappearing due to supernatural causes.
full_text:
THE FOX AS HERDSMAN

In a lush valley lived a farmer who possessed a large flock of fine, woolly sheep. However, he was plagued by the fact that his sheep were constantly wandering off or getting lost in the thickets. One day, a fox approached him with a polished manner and a silver tongue. 

'My good sir,' the fox said, 'I see you struggle with your flock. I am a professional herdsman of the highest order. I can promise you that not a single sheep will go astray under my watch. In exchange, I only ask for a modest payment of a few chickens a week.' 

The farmer, desperate for a solution, agreed to the arrangement. For the first few weeks, everything seemed perfect. The sheep were well-tended and remained close together. But slowly, the farmer began to notice that his flock was shrinking. One sheep would disappear on Monday, another on Wednesday. 

When the farmer questioned the fox, Reynard looked deeply distressed. 'Oh, it is a tragedy!' the fox wailed. 'The hills are haunted by invisible spirits who snatch the sheep away in the blink of an eye. I fight them bravely, but they are too spectral for any fox to defeat.' 

Believing the fox, the farmer even increased his payment, thinking the fox needed more strength to fight the spirits. Meanwhile, Reynard was simply leading the sheep to a hidden cave nearby, where he enjoyed a feast of mutton every single evening. 

Eventually, the farmer’s suspicions grew. He decided to follow the fox in secret. He watched as Reynard led the flock toward the hills, but instead of fighting spirits, he saw the fox lead the sheep straight into his cave. The farmer rushed forward with his dogs, but the fox was too quick. He led the farmer on a wild chase through a maze of briars, laughing all the while. By the time the farmer escaped the thorns, the fox and the remaining sheep had vanished. The farmer learned a bitter lesson: when you hire a predator to guard your prey, you are merely paying for the speed of your own loss.
moral: Do not trust those whose nature is fundamentally opposed to your own interests.

title: Shortshanks
summary: A small boy named Shortshanks is underestimated by everyone due to his size. He embarks on a journey where he uses his exceptional cunning to outwit several ogres and trolls, eventually winning the hand of a princess and proving that size is no measure of capability.
full_text:
SHORTSHANKS

Once upon a time, there was a boy who was so small that his own family called him 'Shortshanks'. He was often the butt of every joke in the village, and his brothers mocked him for his inability to lift heavy loads or run as fast as they could. But Shortshanks possessed a weapon they lacked: a mind that worked faster than a mountain stream. 

Determined to prove his worth, Shortshanks set out on a quest to find the Lost City of Gold. Along his journey, he encountered a towering ogre who guarded the only bridge across a deep canyon. 'I shall eat you for a snack!' the ogre roared. 

Shortshanks did not tremble. Instead, he pulled a small piece of cheese from his pocket and squeezed it until a drop of water came out. 'Look at this!' he cried. 'I can squeeze water from a stone!' 

The ogre, amazed by this 'magic', decided that Shortshanks must be a powerful wizard. He allowed the boy to pass and even gave him a bag of gold, fearing that the boy might otherwise cast a spell on him. 

Later, Shortshanks was captured by a mountain troll who intended to keep him as a pet. The troll challenged him to a contest of strength: whoever could throw a stone the farthest would win. Shortshanks took a small, light pebble and threw it high into the air, where it disappeared into the clouds. The troll, attempting to match him, threw a massive boulder with such force that he strained his back and collapsed in a heap of exhaustion. 

Eventually, Shortshanks reached the Lost City and found the Princess, who had been imprisoned by a wicked sorcerer. Through a series of clever tricks, Shortshanks managed to steal the sorcerer's magic key while the man slept. He freed the Princess and led the city's people to freedom. When he returned home, the villagers who had once laughed at him now bowed in respect, and the Princess chose Shortshanks as her husband, for she valued his wit far more than any man's height.
moral: Capability and wisdom are not determined by physical size.

title: Doll i' the Grass
summary: A tiny, fairy-like girl discovered in the grass is initially seen as a curiosity. Through her bravery, intelligence, and helpfulness, she becomes indispensable to the royal family, demonstrating that even the smallest among us can possess the greatest strength of character.
full_text:
DOLL I’ THE GRASS

In a sun-drenched meadow, a young shepherd boy once found a tiny girl, no larger than a thumb, sleeping inside a curled leaf. She was so delicate and beautiful that she looked like a living doll made of porcelain and silk. He named her 'Doll i' the Grass' and carried her in his pocket, sharing his crumbs and drops of dew with her. 

Doll i' the Grass was not merely a curiosity; snip she possessed a wisdom and a courage that far exceeded her diminutive stature. One day, the shepherd boy took her to the palace to show her to the King. The King was amused by the tiny girl, but he was also deeply troubled by a mystery: the royal treasury had been robbed by a thief who could vanish into thin air, leaving no trace behind. 

'I can find the thief,' Doll i' the Grass announced in a voice that sounded like a silver bell. 

The court laughed, but the King, desperate for answers, granted her a trial. Doll i' the Grass spent the night hiding in the crevices of the palace walls, where no human could ever fit. She watched as the thief—a shapeshifting spirit of the wind—entered the treasury through a keyhole. 

Using a tiny thread of spider-silk, she managed to tie the spirit's ankle to a heavy gold coin. When the spirit tried to vanish, he was jerked back, crashing into a suit of armor with a deafening clang. The guards rushed in and captured the thief, recovering all the stolen treasure. 

The King was overwhelmed with gratitude and offered Doll i' the Grass anything she desired. She asked only for a small garden of her own and the promise that the shepherd boy would always be her friend. The King granted her request and named her the Royal Advisor, for he realized that the smallest eyes often see the things that the largest eyes miss.
moral: One's value is not measured by their size, but by their contribution and courage.

title: Tom Totherhouse
summary: A clever youth enters into a wager with a woman who is having an affair with a neighbor while her husband is deaf. Through a series of ingenious tricks and psychological manipulations, he forces the woman to confess her infidelity, winning the wager and exposing the truth.
full_text:
TOM TOTHERHOUSE

Once on a time there was a Goody who had a deaf husband. A good, easy man he was, but that was just why she thought more of the lad next door, whom they called 'Tom Totherhouse'. Now the lad that served the deaf man saw very well that the two had something between them, and one day he said to the Goody: 'Dare you wager ten dollars, mother, that I don’t make you lay bare your own shame?' 

'Yes I dare', said she; snip and so they wagered ten dollars. So one day, while the lad and the deaf man stood thrashing in the barn, the lad saw that Tom Totherhouse came to see the Goody. He said nothing, but a good while before dinnertime he turned toward the barn-door, and bawled out 'Halloa!' 

'What! are we to go home already?' said the man, who hadn’t given any heed to what the lad did. 'Yes, we must, since mother calls', said the lad. So when they got into the passage, the lad began to hem and cough, that the Goody might get Tom Totherhouse out of the way. But when they came into the room, there stood a whole bowl of custards on the table. 'Nay, nay, mother', cried out the man; snip 'shall we have custards to-day?' 

'Yes, that you shall, dear', said the Goody; snip but she was as sour as verjuice, and as cross as two sticks. So when they had eaten and drank all the good cheer up, off they went again to their work, and the Goody said to Tom: 'Deil take that lad’s sharp nose, this was all his fault; snip but now you must be off as fast as you can, and I’ll come down to you in the mead with a snack between meals.' 

This the lad stood outside in the passage and listened to. 'Do you know, father', he said, 'I think we’d best go down into the hollow and put our fence to rights, which is blown down, before the neighbours’ swine get in and root up our meadow.' 'Aye, aye, let’s go and do it', said the man; snip for he did all he was told, good, easy man. 

So when the afternoon was half spent, down came the Goody sneaking along into the mead, with something under her apron. 'Nay, nay, mother', said the man, 'it can’t be you any longer; snip are we to have a snack between meals too?' 'Yes, yes, that you shall', she said; snip but she was sourer and wilder than ever. 

So they made merry, and crammed themselves with bannocks and butter, and had a drop of brandy into the bargain. 'I’ll go off to Tom Totherhouse with a snack—shan’t I, mother?' said the lad. 'He’s had nothing between meals, I’ll be bound.' 'Ah! do; snip there’s a good fellow', said the Goody, who all at once got as mild as milk. 

As he went along the lad broke a bannock to bits, and dropped the crumbs here and there as he walked. But when he got to Tom Totherhouse he said: 'Now, just you take care, for our old cock has found out that you come too often to see our Goody. He won’t stand it any longer, and has sworn to drive his axe into you as soon as ever he can set eyes on you.' 

As for Tom, he was so frightened he scarce knew which way to turn, and the lad went back again to his master. 'There’s something wrong', he said, 'with Tom’s plough, and he begs you to be so good as to take your axe, and go and see if you can’t set it right.' Yes, the man set off with his axe, but Tom Totherhouse had scarce caught sight of him before he took to his heels as fast as he could. The man turned and twisted the plough round and round, and looked at it on every side, and when he couldn’t see anything wrong with it he went off home again; snip but on the way he picked up the bits of broken bannock which the lad had let fall. His old dame stood in the meadow and looked at him as he did this for a while, and wondered and wondered what it could be her husband was gathering up. 

'Oh, I know', said the lad, 'master’s picking up stones, I’ll be bound; snip for he has marked how often this Tom Totherhouse runs over here; snip and the old fellow won’t stand it any longer; snip and now he has sworn to stone mother to death.' 

Off went the Goody as fast as her legs could carry her. 'Ah! ah! don’t stone me to death; snip don’t stone me to death! and I’ll give you my word never to let Tom Totherhouse come near me again.' 

'Now the ten dollars are mine', bawled out the lad; snip and so they were.
moral: Dishonesty and betrayal will eventually be uncovered by a keen mind.

title: Soria Moria Castle
summary: Halvor, a boy known for his laziness, leaves home to see the world. He travels to the legendary Soria Moria Castle, where he defeats three trolls and rescues three princesses, returning home with immense wealth and the love of the youngest princess.
full_text:
SORIA MORIA CASTLE

Once on a time there was a poor couple who had a son whose name was Halvor. Ever since he was a little boy he would turn his hand to nothing, but just sat there and groped about in the ashes. His father and mother often put him out to learn this trade or that, but Halvor could stay nowhere; snip for, when he had been there a day or two, he ran away from his master, and never stopped till he was sitting again in the ingle, poking about in the cinders. 

Well, one day a skipper came, and asked Halvor if he hadn’t a mind to be with him, and go to sea, and see strange lands. Yes, Halvor would like that very much; snip so he wasn’t long in getting himself ready. How long they sailed I’m sure I can’t tell; snip but the end of it was, they fell into a great storm, and when it was blown over, and it got still again, they couldn’t tell where they were; snip for they had been driven away to a strange coast, which none of them knew anything about. 

Well, as there was just no wind at all, they stayed lying wind-bound there, and Halvor asked the skipper’s leave to go on shore and look about him; snip he would sooner go, he said, than lie there and sleep. 'Do you think now you’re fit to show yourself before folk', said the skipper, 'why, you’ve no clothes but those rags you stand in?' But Halvor stuck to his own, and so at last he got leave, but he was to be sure and come back as soon as ever it began to blow. 

So off he went and found a lovely land; snip wherever he came there were fine large flat corn-fields and rich meads, but he couldn’t catch a glimpse of a living soul. Well, it began to blow, but Halvor thought he hadn’t seen enough yet, and he wanted to walk a little farther just to see if he couldn’t meet any folk. So after a while he came to a broad high road, so smooth and even, you might easily roll an egg along it. Halvor followed this, and when evening drew on he saw a great castle ever so far off, from which the sunbeams shone. 

In the castle kitchen a great fire was blazing, and Halvor went into it, but such a kitchen he had never seen in all his born days. It was so grand and fine; snip there were vessels of silver and vessels of gold, but still never a living soul. So when Halvor had stood there a while and no one came out, he went and opened a door, and there inside sat a Princess who span upon a spinning-wheel. 

'Nay, nay, now!' she called out, 'dare Christian folk come hither? But now you’d best be off about your business, if you don’t want the Troll to gobble you up; snip for here lives a Troll with three heads.' 

'All one to me', said the lad, 'I’d be just as glad to hear he had four heads beside; snip I’d like to see what kind of fellow he is. As for going, I won’t go at all. I’ve done no harm; snip but meat you must get me, for I’m almost starved to death.' 

When Halvor had eaten his fill, the Princess told him to try if he could brandish the sword that hung against the wall; snip no, he couldn’t brandish it, he couldn’t even lift it up. 'Oh!' said the Princess, 'now you must go and take a pull of that flask that hangs by its side; snip that’s what the Troll does every time he goes out to use the sword.' So Halvor took a pull, and in the twinkling of an eye he could brandish the sword like nothing; snip and now he thought it high time the Troll came; snip and lo! just then up came the Troll puffing and blowing. Halvor jumped behind the door. 

'HUTETU', said the Troll, as he put his head in at the door, 'what a smell of Christian man’s blood!' 

'Aye', said Halvor, 'you’ll soon know that to your cost', and with that he hewed off all his heads. 

The Princess was so glad that she was free, she both danced and sang, but then all at once she called her sisters to mind, and so she said: 'Would my sisters were free too'. 

Halvor decided to help. He traveled to the second and third castles, which were even grander than the first. At each castle, he encountered a Troll with more heads—six at the second and nine at the third. Using the same method of drinking from a magic flask and wielding a heavy sword, he defeated the trolls and rescued the other two princesses. 

The three princesses led Halvor to the great Soria Moria Castle, where they lived in luxury. Halvor eventually felt a deep longing for his parents and requested to return home. The princesses gave him a magic ring that allowed him to teleport anywhere he wished. 

Returning to his village, Halvor discovered that his parents had grown old and poor. He revealed his identity and his new wealth, bringing joy to his family and the whole village. He married the youngest princess, and they lived in a palace of their own, proving that even the laziest of boys could achieve greatness if they had the courage to explore the unknown.
moral: Fortune favors the bold and those who venture beyond their comfort zone.

title: Lord Peter
summary: A story of a young man who overcomes the challenges set by a vengeful father-in-law to prove his worth. Through his courage and a series of magical quests, including defeating a dragon, he secures his marriage and attains great wealth and honor.
full_text:
LORD PETER

Once on a time there was a young man who had fallen in love with a beautiful girl. However, her father, Peter the Pedlar, was a man of great greed and a sharp tongue, and he refused to let the young man marry his daughter unless he could prove his worth. Peter set a series of impossible tasks for the youth, hoping that he would fail and be gone from their sight forever. 

The first task was to retrieve three feathers from the tail of the Dragon of Deepferry. The Dragon was a creature of immense power and terror, and many had tried to steal his feathers, but none had returned. The young man set off with a determined heart. Along his way, he encountered several kings and queens who were plagued by their own mysteries—a well that would not run clear, a lost daughter, and missing gold keys. 

In each case, the young man offered his help. He learned that the Dragon held the answers to all their problems. When he finally reached the Dragon's castle, he was aided by a kind princess who lived there. She gave him a magic flask that granted him the strength of ten men, allowing him to wield a sword that no other human could lift. 

Under the cover of night, while the Dragon slept, the young man crept forward and, with one swift stroke, hewed off the Dragon's head and plucked the three feathers. He also learned the answers to the kings' and queens' riddles: the well was blocked by a rotten stump, the lost daughter was the princess he had just rescued, and the gold keys were hidden in a bush. 

When he returned to Peter the Pedlar with the feathers and the answers, the old man was stunned. He tried to set one more task, but the young man's success and the wealth he had gathered from the Dragon's hoard were too great to ignore. Peter finally gave his blessing, and the young man married the beautiful girl. They lived in a grand palace, and the young man's kindness and courage were praised throughout the land, while Peter the Pedlar's greed became a cautionary tale for all who sought to hinder true love with impossible demands.
moral: Courage and a kind heart can overcome any obstacle, no matter how impossible it seems.

title: Little Annie the Goose-Girl
summary: A young woman is betrayed by her companions and forced to work as a goose-girl, hiding her royal identity. Through patience and the help of her animal friends, she eventually regains her status and finds true love.
full_text: Once on a time there was a princess who was sent to a distant kingdom to marry a prince. On the way, she was betrayed by her maid, who forced her to swap clothes and take over the duties of a goose-girl. The princess was forced to tend to the geese and hide her noble birth, while the treacherous maid pretended to be the princess. However, the princess's kindness to the animals and her unwavering spirit eventually led the prince to discover the truth of her identity. In the end, the betrayal was revealed, the impostor was punished, and the true princess was restored to her rightful place and wed to the prince.
moral: True nobility and kindness will always shine through, regardless of outward appearance.

title: The Lad and the Devil
summary: A clever lad tricks the Devil into shrinking himself and entering a nut, which the lad then seals with a pin. He later uses a blacksmith's hammer to destroy the nut, effectively defeating the Devil with wit.
full_text: Once on a time there was a lad who was walking along a road cracking nuts, so he found one that was worm-eaten, and just at that very moment he met the Deil. 'Is it true, now,' said the lad, 'what they say, that the Deil can make himself as small as he chooses, and thrust himself in through a pinhole?' 'Yes, it is,' said the Deil. 'Oh! it is, is it? then let me see you do it, and just creep into this nut,' said the lad. So the Deil did it. Now, when he had crept well into it through the worm's hole, the lad stopped it up with a pin. 'Now, I've got you safe,' he said, and put the nut into his pocket. So when he had walked on a bit, he came to a smithy, and he turned in and asked the smith if he'd be good enough to crack that nut for him. 'Ay, that'll be an easy job,' said the smith, and took his smallest hammer, laid the nut on the anvil, and gave it a blow, but it wouldn't break. So he took another hammer a little bigger, but that wasn't heavy enough either. Then he took one bigger still, but it was still the same story; and so the smith got wroth, and grasped his great sledge-hammer. 'Now, I'll crack you to bits,' he said, and let drive at the nut with all his might and main. And so the nut flew to pieces with a bang that blew off half the roof of the smithy, and the whole house creaked and groaned as though it were ready to fall. 'Why! if I don't think the Deil must have been in that nut,' said the smith. 'So he was; you're quite right,' said the lad, as he went away laughing.
moral: Cunning and quick thinking can overcome even the most powerful adversaries.

title: The Seven Foals
summary: Three brothers attempt to win a princess by watching seven magical foals for a day. While the elder brothers are lazy and fail, the youngest, Boots, discovers the foals' true identity as princes and helps them break a curse.
full_text: Once on a time there was a poor couple who lived in a wretched hut, far far away in the wood. They had three sons, and the youngest of them was Boots. One day the eldest lad set off to earn his bread and came to a king's palace. The King offered him the Princess and half the kingdom if he could watch seven foals for one day and tell the King what they ate and drank. However, the lad was lazy and slept through the day, failing the task. The second son fared the same. Then Boots set out. He discovered that the foals were actually princes under a curse from a Troll. Boots helped them by wielding a magical sword and cutting off their foal heads, which restored them to human form. In return, the foals told him what they ate and drank—wine and wafers from a church—allowing him to win the Princess and half the kingdom.
moral: Hard work and attentiveness lead to reward, while laziness leads to failure.

title: Goosey Grizzel
summary: A housekeeper named Grizzel is made to look like a monster by her employer. She joins a band of thieves and uses her frightening appearance to scare others, eventually encountering a priest who tries to 'bind' her.
full_text: Once on a time there was a Widower, who had a house keeper named Grizzel. The man grew weary of her advances and decided to rid himself of her. One day, while she slept, he cut her skirts short and rubbed her face with tallow and soot until she looked like a monster. Grizzel, believing herself to be the Devil, joined two thieves and helped them steal sheep and geese by screaming and scaring the farmers. The farmers, thinking the Devil was loose, began to read and pray. Eventually, a farmer tried to get a priest to bind Grizzel, but the priest ended up falling into a mossy bog. Grizzel's frightening appearance and loud voice were her tools of chaos, but she remained a misunderstood figure of the countryside.
moral: Those who judge solely by appearance often find themselves in ridiculous situations.

title: The Twelve Wild Ducks
summary: A queen exchanges her twelve sons for a daughter, who grows up to seek her brothers. She discovers they are transformed into wild ducks and spends years weaving magical clothes to break their curse.
full_text: Once on a time there was a Queen who, desiring a daughter, traded her twelve sons to a Troll in exchange for a daughter, Snow-white and Rosy-red. As the princess grew, she learned of her lost brothers and set off to find them. She discovered they lived in a wooden hut and were transformed into wild ducks every morning. To break the spell, she had to pick thistle-down and weave twelve coats, shirts, and neckerchiefs without talking, laughing, or weeping. Despite the interference of a king who fell in love with her and a jealous stepmother who tried to frame her for eating her own children, the princess completed the task. Her brothers were restored to human form, and she lived happily with the king.
moral: Unwavering love and determination can overcome the most difficult of trials.

title: The Master Thief
summary: A young man becomes a 'Master Thief' through his extraordinary cunning. He outwits a farmer, a priest, and a squire, eventually winning the hand of the squire's daughter by proving his skills.
full_text: A youth sets out into the world and becomes a student of a band of robbers, eventually becoming their leader, the Master Thief. He proves his skill by stealing three oxen from a farmer through a series of clever tricks involving shoes and hanging himself from trees. He further outwits a priest by tricking him into a sack and dragging him through a 'narrow path to heaven' (a goose-house). Finally, he wins the hand of a squire's daughter by stealing a roast from a kitchen while distracting the household with hares, and by stealing horses from under their grooms using a sleepy drink. Through wit and audacity, he secures wealth and a royal marriage.
moral: Intelligence and resourcefulness can open doors that strength and wealth cannot.

title: The Old Dame and her Hen
summary: An old woman's hen is stolen by a Man of the Hill. Her three daughters search for it; the first two are killed, but the youngest outwits the ogre and rescues her sisters.
full_text: An old widow's prized hen is stolen by a Man of the Hill. Her three daughters go to find it, and the first two are killed. The youngest daughter, however, is observant. She discovers her dead sisters in the cellar and manages to avoid the ogre's wrath by pretending to be his sweetheart. She tricks the ogre into carrying sacks of gold and her revived sisters (using a magical ointment) back to her mother. Finally, she lures the ogre to her home where a sharpshooter kills him, and she recovers her family and wealth.
moral: Caution and observation are more valuable than blind courage.

title: The Best Wish
summary: Three brothers each receive one wish. The elders wish for money, but the youngest wishes that every woman he sees falls in love with him, leading to a life of luxury and affection.
full_text: Three brothers are each granted a single wish. The two older brothers wish that they can pull money out of their pockets whenever they like. The youngest, Boots, wishes that every woman he encounters falls instantly in love with him. While the older brothers are wealthy, they are often arrogant. Boots, despite having no money initially, is treated with immense kindness and given magical gifts—scissors that cut silk from air, a tablecloth that produces feasts, and a tap that pours the finest drinks—by the wives of innkeepers. Eventually, he wins the heart of a princess and half a kingdom, proving that affection is more valuable than gold.
moral: True wealth lies in the love and affection of others rather than in material riches.

title: Boots Who Made the Princess Say 'That's a Story'
summary: A king offers his daughter's hand to anyone who can make her admit a story is a lie. Boots wins by telling an increasingly absurd tale of exaggeration that eventually forces the princess to concede.
full_text: A king's daughter is a prolific liar, and the king promises her hand to anyone who can get her to say 'That's a story' in response to a lie. Many fail, but Boots succeeds by engaging her in a competition of exaggerations. He describes a farmyard so large that shepherds at opposite ends cannot hear each other's horns, an ox so big that men on its horns cannot touch with a rule, and a cow whose milk fills vats. He culminates the tale by describing a spruce tree that grew from a mare's backbone, leading him to heaven where the Virgin Mary spun foam into ropes, and finally falling into a fox's hole where the princess's father was being boxed on the ear. The princess, unable to resist, exclaims, 'That's a story!', and Boots wins the prize.
moral: Creativity and a sense of humor can overcome the most stubborn of personalities.

title: Well Done and Ill Paid
summary: A man tricks a bear into being bound to a sledge, but the fox who helped him is betrayed by the man's own wife, who sends hounds to hunt the fox.
full_text: A man is forced to promise his horse to a bear. A cunning fox helps the man trick the bear by pretending to be a marksman in the woods, leading the bear to voluntarily be bound to the man's sledge for 'transport.' Once bound, the man kills the bear. However, when the fox asks for his payment—a fat wether—the man's wife convinces him to send hounds instead. The fox narrowly escapes the hounds, reflecting on how 'well done is often ill paid' and how betrayal can come from one's own home.
moral: Do not trust those who are quick to betray others, as they may eventually betray you.

title: True and Untrue
summary: Two brothers, True and Untrue, leave home. Untrue blinds his brother and leaves him, but True recovers his sight and uses secrets learned from animals to win the king's favor and a princess.
full_text: Two brothers, True and Untrue, set out to find their fortune. Untrue betrays True by stealing his food and eventually plucking out his eyes. While blind, True climbs a tree and overhears animals discussing cures for the King of England's ailments: dew for his blindness, a toad for his daughter's muteness, and a spring under a stone for his water. True uses these secrets to cure the King and the Princess, gaining wealth and a royal marriage. Untrue eventually returns, pretending to be a beggar, but is outwitted by True and left with nothing, as his nature remains fundamentally untrue.
moral: Honesty and goodness are eventually rewarded, while deceit leads to ruin.

title: Boots and His Brothers
summary: Three brothers try to win a princess by felling a magical oak and digging a well. The older brothers fail and are exiled, but the youngest, Boots, uses magical tools to succeed.
full_text: A King offers his daughter and half the kingdom to anyone who can fell a magical oak that regrows and dig a well that holds water year-round. The two elder brothers fail and are exiled to a barren island. The youngest, Boots, finds a magical axe, a magical spade, and a magical walnut. Using these tools, he effortlessly fells the tree and fills the well. He wins the Princess and the kingdom, while his brothers remain on the island, forever wondering why they underestimated the curious nature of Boots.
moral: Curiosity and a willingness to explore often lead to solutions that conventional methods cannot find.

title: Why the Sea is Salt
summary: A man possesses a magical mill capable of producing any item on command. A curious sailor steals the mill and, not knowing the command to stop it, asks for salt. The mill produces salt relentlessly, filling the ocean and explaining why the sea remains salty to this day.
full_text: Once on a time there was a man who had a mill which could produce anything that was asked of it. He had it from his father, and he lived quite happily with it for many years. One day a sailor came to him and asked to borrow the mill for a short time. The man, being kind, agreed. But the sailor was greedy; he didn't want to give the mill back. He sailed away with it to his own country. When he got there, he wanted to see what the mill could do, so he asked it to produce salt. The mill began to produce salt, and it didn't stop. It produced salt and salt, and salt and salt, until the whole ship was full of salt, and then the salt began to flow into the sea. The sailor didn't know the word to stop the mill, and so it continued to produce salt until the entire ocean became salty, as it is to this very day.
moral: Greed and a lack of knowledge can lead to an uncontrollable and disastrous outcome.

title: The Princess on the Glass Hill
summary: A determined young man seeks to win the hand of a princess who resides atop an impenetrable glass hill. Through his bravery, wit, and the help of magical entities, he overcomes the obstacles and ascends the hill to claim his bride.
full_text: Once on a time there was a princess who lived in a castle atop a hill made of glass. The hill was so smooth and slippery that no one could climb it; all who tried simply slid back down. The king declared that whoever could ascend the glass hill and reach the princess would be allowed to marry her. Many brave knights and strong men tried, but all failed. Then came a young man of humble origin but great heart. He encountered a series of magical creatures who, impressed by his kindness, gave him a magical ointment to rub on his shoes, allowing him to grip the glass. With great effort and determination, he climbed the steep, transparent slope. Upon reaching the summit, he found the princess, and having proved his worth and courage, they were wed and lived in happiness.
moral: Courage, determination, and kindness can overcome the most impossible of obstacles.

title: Buttercup
summary: A plump and fond-of-good-things boy named Buttercup is captured by a wicked witch. Despite her terrifying nature, Buttercup uses his quick wit and appetite to outmaneuver her, eventually escaping her clutches to find wealth and freedom.
full_text: Once on a time there was an old wife who sat and baked. She had a little son who was so plump and fat, and so fond of good things, that they called him Buttercup. One day, while his mother was baking, a dog named Goldtooth began to bark, and the mother sent Buttercup out to see what was happening. There he met a witch who lured him away. The witch intended to eat him, but Buttercup was a clever lad. He managed to trick the witch into performing tasks for him and used his wit to avoid being cooked. He discovered the witch's secrets and, through a series of clever ruses, managed to escape her hut with a hoard of gold and jewels, returning home to live a life of luxury with his mother.
moral: Intelligence and quick thinking are more powerful than brute force or dark magic.

title: Big Peter and Little Peter
summary: Two brothers of vastly different sizes—one a giant and one tiny—set out to seek their fortune. Though they are opposites in physical stature, they find that their differing abilities complement each other perfectly, allowing them to succeed where others failed.
full_text: Once on a time there were two brothers, Big Peter and Little Peter. Big Peter was as large as a mountain, and Little Peter was as small as a thumb. They decided to leave home to find their fortune. Throughout their journey, they encountered obstacles that only one of them could solve. When a heavy boulder blocked their path, Big Peter lifted it with ease. When they needed to retrieve a key from a narrow crevice, Little Peter slipped inside and brought it back. They realized that neither could have succeeded alone, and by combining their strengths, they outsmarted various trolls and giants, eventually winning great riches and a peaceful life together.
moral: Everyone possesses unique strengths, and true success is achieved through cooperation and mutual support.

title: Tatterhood
summary: A girl dressed in rags and underestimated by society possesses profound wisdom and courage. Through a series of trials, she solves complex riddles and saves a kingdom, proving that intellect and heart are more valuable than fine clothing.
full_text: There was once a girl who was so poorly dressed that everyone called her Tatterhood. She was mocked for her rags, but she possessed a mind sharper than any sword. One day, a prince was placed under a curse that only the most intelligent person in the land could break. Many lords and scholars tried and failed. Tatterhood, though dismissed by the court, stepped forward and solved the riddles that had baffled the wise. Through her insight and bravery, she broke the curse and restored the kingdom's peace. The prince, captivated by her wisdom and spirit, married her, and she became a beloved queen, teaching all that appearance is no measure of a person's worth.
moral: True value resides in character and intellect, not in outward appearance or social status.

title: Bushy Bride
summary: A man marries a woman who appears bushy and strange in form, much to the surprise of his family. However, her outward appearance hides a magical nature and a virtuous heart, which eventually bring prosperity and joy to the household.
full_text: Once upon a time, a man decided to marry a woman known as the Bushy Bride. She was covered in strange, bushy foliage and looked more like a shrub than a human, causing the townspeople to laugh and his parents to recoil in horror. Despite the social stigma, the man saw a kindness in her eyes and remained devoted to her. As time passed, the Bushy Bride used her magical abilities to heal the sick and make the crops flourish. Eventually, her bushy exterior fell away to reveal a woman of stunning beauty. Her virtues had transformed her form, and the man's unwavering acceptance had unlocked her true self, leading to a lifetime of happiness.
moral: Do not judge others by their appearance; true beauty and virtue are often hidden beneath an unlikely surface.

title: The Cat on the Dovrefjell
summary: A youth journeys to the Dovrefjell mountains where he encounters a magical cat. The cat, acting as a guide, leads the youth through perilous landscapes to a hidden treasure and helps him win the heart of a princess.
full_text: A young man, seeking his destiny, traveled to the high peaks of the Dovrefjell mountains. There, he met a peculiar cat with eyes like gold and a voice like a human. The cat offered to guide him in exchange for a portion of whatever he found. Together they navigated treacherous cliffs and outsmarted mountain trolls. The cat led him to a hidden cavern filled with gold and ancient artifacts. With this wealth and the courage he gained through the journey, the youth returned to the kingdom and successfully courted the princess. He remained lifelong friends with the cat, recognizing that the animal's guidance was the true source of his fortune.
moral: Kindness toward animals and a brave spirit can lead to unexpected guidance and great rewards.

title: Farmer Weathersky
summary: Farmer Weathersky possesses a unique ability to interact with the weather. When opportunistic neighbors try to exploit his gift or cheat him out of his land, he uses his cleverness to turn the tide in his favor, ensuring justice and prosperity.
full_text: Farmer Weathersky was a man who seemed to have the wind and rain in his pocket. While other farmers struggled with droughts or floods, Weathersky's fields were always perfectly watered and sunny. His success drew the jealousy of the local landlord and a few cunning neighbors, who attempted to trick him into a contract that would strip him of his land. Weathersky, however, was as sharp as a tack. He played along with their schemes, leading them to believe they had won, only to reveal a clause in the agreement that left the cheats bankrupt and himself with more land than ever. He lived out his days in peace, respected by all for his wit.
moral: Honesty and intelligence are the most reliable tools for protecting one's livelihood and defeating injustice.

title: The Blue Belt
summary: A protagonist obtains a magical blue belt that grants superhuman strength and the ability to overcome impossible trials. Using the belt, they defeat monsters and solve the puzzles of a distant king to win a great reward.
full_text: Once on a time, a poor youth was gifted a shimmering blue belt by a mysterious wanderer. The belt granted the wearer immense strength and the courage of a lion. With the blue belt fastened around his waist, the youth set out to find the legendary Golden City. Along the way, he fought off giant serpents and moved mountains that blocked his path. Upon arriving at the city, the king challenged him to a series of impossible feats. Thanks to the belt, he succeeded in every task, eventually proving himself to be the most capable man in the land. He was rewarded with a kingdom of his own, but he never forgot the kindness of the wanderer who gave him the belt.
moral: When combined with a brave heart, a tool of power can be used to change one's destiny for the better.

title: The Honest Penny
summary: A poor boy finds a money box but throws it away, believing it contains stolen wealth, keeping only one silver penny that floats on water, which he deems 'honest.' This act of integrity leads him to a series of adventures and eventual immense wealth.
full_text: Once on a time there was a poor woman who lived in a tumble-down hut. She sent her little boy into the wood to gather fuel. He found a white crooked stone and, thinking it was frozen, covered it with his jacket. Beneath the stone, he found a money-box full of silver. Believing it was stolen, he threw it into a tarn, but one silver penny floated on the top. 'This is honest,' the lad said, and kept it. Later, he worked as a scullion for a merchant. The merchant bought a cat for the lad's penny. This cat proved invaluable, helping the merchant clear mice from foreign lands and earning him huge sums of money. Ultimately, the merchant gave all the wealth back to the lad, who married the merchant's daughter and lived in luxury.
moral: True honesty is rewarded in the end, even when it seems foolish or impractical to others.

title: Father Bruin in the Corner
summary: A humorous tale about Father Bruin, a bear who believes himself to be superior but is repeatedly tricked by the cunning Reynard the Fox. The story highlights the contrast between Bruin's strength and Reynard's intelligence.
full_text: Father Bruin was a bear of great size and even greater pride. He believed he was the king of the forest and that all other animals should defer to him. However, Reynard the Fox was far cleverer. Reynard often convinced Bruin to engage in 'tests of strength' or 'games of wit' that always ended with Bruin in a ridiculous position—often stuck in a corner or trapped in a hole. In one instance, Reynard convinced Bruin that the ice of a frozen lake was a fine place to fish, leading the bear to get his tail frozen fast. Through these mishaps, Bruin slowly learned that strength without wisdom is a fragile thing.
moral: Overconfidence and gullibility make one an easy target for those with superior wit.

title: The Sheep and the Pig Who Set up House
summary: A sheep and a pig decide to partner up and create a household. The story follows their comedic attempts at domestic management, showcasing the friction and eventual cooperation required to maintain a home.
full_text: A sheep and a pig, tired of their respective lives, decided to set up a household together. They agreed to share all expenses and chores. However, their natures were vastly different; the sheep was meticulous and cautious, while the pig was impulsive and messy. This led to endless arguments over the placement of furniture and the cooking of meals. After many comical disasters, including a failed attempt to build a fence, they realized that their differences were actually complementary. By learning to compromise and appreciate each other's strengths, they turned their chaotic house into a warm and prosperous home.
moral: A successful partnership requires mutual understanding, compromise, and the acceptance of others' differences.

title: Friends in Life and Death
summary: Two inseparable friends swear to remain loyal even after death. When one passes away, the other visits the grave and journeys into the afterlife. There, he observes the symbolic representations of human greed and contentment, learning a profound lesson about the soul.
full_text: Two young men swore they would never part, either in life or death. When one died, the survivor knocked on the grave, and the dead friend emerged. The survivor decided to accompany his friend into the afterlife. They passed through dark wastes and reached a land of green hills. They saw two herds of cattle: one herd was thin and wretched despite eating constantly, representing the greedy who are never satisfied. The other herd was fat and sleek despite having little to eat, representing the content. The survivor eventually returned to the living world, only to find that centuries had passed. Realizing the fleeting nature of time and the importance of peace, he accepted his fate with a quiet heart.
moral: True friendship transcends the boundary of death, and contentment is the only true wealth of the soul.

title: Three Years without Wages
summary: A youth serves a miserly master for three years without pay, receiving only a puppy, a kitten, and a lizard as his wages. He discovers the animals are transformed royalty and, with their help, attains a magical ring and a kingdom.
full_text: A lazy youth was hired as a ploughboy by a rich man on the condition that he serve three years without wages. In return, the master promised to buy him three things—the first items he encountered in town on three separate mornings. The youth received a puppy, a kitten, and a lizard. He soon discovered the lizard was actually a prince under a witch's spell. After saving the prince, the youth was gifted a magical ring that granted any wish. Using the ring, he outsmarted the miserly master and the king, eventually marrying a princess and ruling a prosperous land, proving that patience and kindness are rewarded.
moral: Persistence and an open heart toward the unlikely can lead to rewards far greater than material wages.

title: Birth of Wainamoinen
summary: In primeval times, the Daughter of the Ether descends to the ocean. A duck nests on her knee, laying six golden eggs and one iron egg. These eggs break, forming the earth, sky, sun, moon, and stars. Wainamoinen is born and spends years in the womb before emerging as an old, wise singer and enchanter who shapes the early world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. In primeval times, the Daughter of the Ether descends to the ocean. A duck nests on her knee, laying six golden eggs and one iron egg. These eggs break, forming the earth, sky, sun, moon, and stars. Wainamoinen is born and spends years in the womb before emerging as an old, wise singer and enchanter who shapes the early world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen's Sowing
summary: Wainamoinen attempts to sow the earth, but the land remains barren. He is challenged by powerful winds and storms that seek to prevent the growth of life. Using his profound magical songs, he calms the elements and prepares the land for agriculture, establishing the first signs of order and sustenance in the world.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen attempts to sow the earth, but the land remains barren. He is challenged by powerful winds and storms that seek to prevent the growth of life. Using his profound magical songs, he calms the elements and prepares the land for agriculture, establishing the first signs of order and sustenance in the world. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

title: Wainamoinen and Youkahainen
summary: Youkahainen, a young and arrogant singer, challenges the elderly Wainamoinen to a singing match to prove his superiority. Wainamoinen uses his deep knowledge of the origins of things and his superior magical songs to sing Youkahainen into a swamp, demonstrating that ancient wisdom far outweighs youthful arrogance.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Youkahainen, a young and arrogant singer, challenges the elderly Wainamoinen to a singing match to prove his superiority. Wainamoinen uses his deep knowledge of the origins of things and his superior magical songs to sing Youkahainen into a swamp, demonstrating that ancient wisdom far outweighs youthful arrogance. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: Ancient wisdom outweighs youthful arrogance.

title: The Fate of Aino
summary: Wainamoinen falls in love with the young Aino, but she is horrified by his advanced age and lacks any affection for him. Despite her desperate pleas, her parents force her to marry the old enchanter. In a final act of desperation to escape her fate, Aino leaps into the sea, where she is transformed into a mermaid-like creature.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Wainamoinen falls in love with the young Aino, but she is horrified by his advanced age and lacks any affection for him. Despite her desperate pleas, her parents force her to marry the old enchanter. In a final act of desperation to escape her fate, Aino leaps into the sea, where she is transformed into a mermaid-like creature. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: True love transcends all boundaries.

title: Wainamoinen's Lamentation
summary: Overcome with grief after Aino's death, Wainamoinen wanders the world, mourning the loss of the woman he loved. Through his songs, he reflects on the nature of love, the pain of loss, and the inevitability of fate, while continuing to refine his magical abilities and search for meaning in his solitude.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. Overcome with grief after Aino's death, Wainamoinen wanders the world, mourning the loss of the woman he loved. Through his songs, he reflects on the nature of love, the pain of loss, and the inevitability of fate, while continuing to refine his magical abilities and search for meaning in his solitude. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: True love transcends all boundaries.

title: Wainamoinen's Hapless Journey
summary: Seeking a new bride to end his loneliness, Wainamoinen sails to distant and strange lands. During his journey, he encounters numerous obstacles and faces various trials that test both his physical endurance and his magical prowess, often finding himself in precarious situations that require his ingenuity to survive.

title: Grettis Saga - The Fight with Glámr
summary: Grettir Ásmundarson, a man of extraordinary strength, is hired to protect a farm haunted by a wight. He eventually faces the undead shepherd Glámr in a brutal battle. title: Eyrbyggja Saga - The Seal of Frodis-water

title: The Tale of the Silver Salmon
summary: A fisherman catches a shimmering silver salmon that can speak. The fish promises to grant him one wish in exchange for its freedom. The man asks for the ability to understand the language of all animals, which allows him to uncover a plot to overthrow the local chieftain.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Tale of the Silver Salmon has been told for generations. A fisherman catches a shimmering silver salmon that can speak. The fish promises to grant him one wish in exchange for its freedom. The man asks for the ability to understand the language of all animals, which allows him to uncover a plot to overthrow the local chieftain. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Guardian of the Hidden Valley
summary: Deep in the highlands lies a valley that remains green and warm even in the dead of winter. It is guarded by a massive white wolf who allows only those with a selfless heart to enter. Those who enter find a paradise where time stands still and all wounds are healed.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Guardian of the Hidden Valley has been told for generations. Deep in the highlands lies a valley that remains green and warm even in the dead of winter. It is guarded by a massive white wolf who allows only those with a selfless heart to enter. Those who enter find a paradise where time stands still and all wounds are healed. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Curse of the Raven's Eye
summary: A man steals a gemstone from the eye of a carved raven statue in an ancient temple. The gem gives him the ability to see the future, but it also attracts the attention of the raven's spirit, which slowly consumes his sanity until he returns the stone to its rightful place.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Curse of the Raven's Eye has been told for generations. A man steals a gemstone from the eye of a carved raven statue in an ancient temple. The gem gives him the ability to see the future, but it also attracts the attention of the raven's spirit, which slowly consumes his sanity until he returns the stone to its rightful place. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Midsummer Night's Dance
summary: On the night of the summer solstice, the veil between worlds is thinnest. Humans are invited to dance with the Huldufólk in the mountain meadows. Those who dance until dawn are forever changed, possessing a lingering longing for the hidden world and a strange affinity for the nature of the land.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Midsummer Night's Dance has been told for generations. On the night of the summer solstice, the veil between worlds is thinnest. Humans are invited to dance with the Huldufólk in the mountain meadows. Those who dance until dawn are forever changed, possessing a lingering longing for the hidden world and a strange affinity for the nature of the land. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: Respect the unseen forces of the world.

title: The Legend of the Sleeping Warrior
summary: In a deep cave beneath a mountain, a great warrior of old sleeps in a magical trance. Legend says that he will awaken only when Iceland is in its greatest hour of need, rising with his sword to defend the land from an overwhelming foe.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Legend of the Sleeping Warrior has been told for generations.
