The tradition was instrumental in preserving the Faroese language during centuries of Danish rule and continues as a living cultural practice at festivals and gatherings.
full_text: The Faroese chain dance (Føroya Dansur) accompanied by kvæði (ballads) is the national dance and a living tradition that preserves folklore. Dancers form a circle holding hands, moving rhythmically while a kvæðamaður (ballad singer) recites verses recounting tales of legendary heroes, folklore figures, and historical events. Many kvæði date to the Middle Ages and preserve stories otherwise lost, including the Sjúrðar Kvæði (cyclic ballads about Sigurd the Dragon-Slayer from Norse mythology) and ballads about Finnur hin Fróði and other Faroese figures. The tradition was instrumental in preserving the Faroese language during centuries of Danish rule and continues as a living cultural practice at festivals and gatherings.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: Wikipedia - Nordic Folklore; Trap.fo - Tradition and Tales on the Faroe Islands

title: Tippafossur and the Killing of Jógvan
summary: A grim legend within the Ormur cycle. Jógvan, a farmer from Oyri, caught Ormur of Skála stealing his sheep and attacked him. Miraculously, Jógvan overpowered the huge Ormur and held him down, calling for his son to bring a knife. But the son, terrified of Ormur, fled and hid at the Tippafossur gorge. Ormur, pinned and fearing death, promised the devil a piece of his little finger in exchange for strength. Suddenly gaining supernatural power, Ormur broke free and killed Jógvan. He then found the boy at Tippafossur and killed him too. Later, in his sleep, Ormur muttered: 'The clothes lie under the corn and the bodies under Tippafossur.' His farmhands heard, found the evidence, and informed the authorities.
full_text: A grim legend within the Ormur cycle. Jógvan, a farmer from Oyri, caught Ormur of Skála stealing his sheep and attacked him. Miraculously, Jógvan overpowered the huge Ormur and held him down, calling for his son to bring a knife. But the son, terrified of Ormur, fled and hid at the Tippafossur gorge. Ormur, pinned and fearing death, promised the devil a piece of his little finger in exchange for strength. Suddenly gaining supernatural power, Ormur broke free and killed Jógvan. He then found the boy at Tippafossur and killed him too. Later, in his sleep, Ormur muttered: 'The clothes lie under the corn and the bodies under Tippafossur.' His farmhands heard, found the evidence, and informed the authorities.
moral: Evil deeds eventually come to light.

title: The Huldufólk Nursery Rhyme: Hulda, Mulda, Tulda
summary: A traditional Faroese skjaldur (short nursery rhyme or tale) that invokes the Huldufólk. The rhyme 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' is a charm asking the Hidden People not to hide lost objects. It reflects the common belief that Huldufólk, being mischievous tricksters, enjoy hiding household items from humans. The rhyme tradition is one of the few surviving forms of Huldufólk interaction in the modern era—residents of Suðuroy claim the hidden people left with the introduction of electricity in the 1950s. These skjaldur, studied by linguist Stephen Pax Leonard, represent an endangered oral tradition unique to the Faroe Islands, blending pre-Christian and Christian elements.
full_text:
A traditional Faroese skjaldur (short nursery rhyme or tale) that invokes the Huldufólk. The rhyme 'Hulda, mulda, tulda' is a charm asking the Hidden People not to hide lost objects. It reflects the common belief that Huldufólk, being mischievous tricksters, enjoy hiding household items from humans. The rhyme tradition is one of the few surviving forms of Huldufólk interaction in the modern era—residents of Suðuroy claim the hidden people left with the introduction of electricity in the 1950s. These skjaldur, studied by linguist Stephen Pax Leonard, represent an endangered oral tradition unique to the Faroe Islands, blending pre-Christian and Christian elements.

--- 
This story is preserved from an oral tradition. We acknowledge the communities and storytellers who have kept this legend alive through generations.
moral: The hidden world exists alongside our own and must be respected.
source: Stephen Pax Leonard - Faroese skjaldur (2010); The Adventure Seekers

title: Finnur hin Fróði and Faroese Wizard Legends
summary: Faroese folklore includes tales of powerful wizards and cunning folk (wise men and women) who could control weather, heal sickness, and ward off trolls. One of the most famous is Finnur hin Fróði (Finn the Wise), a legendary figure associated with magical knowledge and runic wisdom. These wizard legends blend Norse pagan traditions with later Christian elements—wizards were said to possess books of black magic (the 'Black Book' tradition) that could only be passed on to a willing successor. The island of Svínoy and the village of Sumba are particularly associated with wizard lore. These tales reflect the historical tension between official Christianity and older folk beliefs that persisted for centuries.
full_text:
Faroese folklore includes tales of powerful wizards and cunning folk (wise men and women) who could control weather, heal sickness, and ward off trolls. One of the most famous is Finnur hin Fróði (Finn the Wise), a legendary figure associated with magical knowledge and runic wisdom. These wizard legends blend Norse pagan traditions with later Christian elements—wizards were said to possess books of black magic (the 'Black Book' tradition) that could only be passed on to a willing successor. The island of Svínoy and the village of Sumba are particularly associated with wizard lore. These tales reflect the historical tension between official Christianity and older folk beliefs that persisted for centuries.

--- 
This story is preserved from an oral tradition. We acknowledge the communities and storytellers who have kept this legend alive through generations.
moral: Folklore preserves the wisdom of a people.
source: Superstition Sam - Witches and Wizards of the Faroe Islands; Faroese oral tradition

title: The Sjörå – Lady of the Baltic Sea
summary: The sjörå (sea spirit) is a guardian entity of the Baltic waters surrounding the Åland archipelago. She appears as a beautiful woman, sometimes described as hollow-backed like the skogsrå, who can calm storms or raise tempests depending on how she is treated. Fishermen and sailors traditionally greeted her with respect before setting out. Those who showed her reverence were rewarded with calm seas and good catches, while those who disrespected her faced endless wind and perilous voyages. The sjörå embodies the deep bond between the Åland islanders and the sea that has sustained them for millennia. Stone cairns along the shores of Eckerö, Finström, and Geta mark ancient graves of seafarers — each a monument to lives carried across these treacherous waters. Even today, some islanders speak of her presence when the tide falls silent and the horizon blurs.

title: The Storm-Witches of Kastelholm
summary: Åland folklore tells of storm-witches who possessed the secret art of 'tying the wind' into knots of rope. These women could sell sailors a favorable breeze or calm at sea by selling them a knotted rope: untying one knot released a light wind, untying two filled the sails, but untying all three would awaken a devastating tempest. The practice is associated with the maritime culture of Kastelholm Castle, the 14th-century fortress at the heart of the main island. Even today, some islanders refuse to throw rope knots into the sea without cutting them first, for fear of stirring what should sleep. This legend reflects the deep folk belief in weather magic, a tradition found throughout the Nordic coastal regions but with a distinctly Åland character rooted in the archipelago's dependence on safe sea passage.

title: The Kastelholm Witch Trials (1665–1668)
summary: The Kastelholm witch trials were the largest witch trials in Finnish history, held at Kastelholm Castle in Åland between 1665 and 1668. Governor Nils Psilander, educated at the University of Dorpat (Tartu) where witchcraft demonology was a major subject, conducted the trials. Unlike typical Finnish witch trials which involved single male defendants accused of sorcery, the Kastelholm trials were a mass trial modeled on continental European patterns. Several women were accused of attending a Witches' Sabbath and making a pact with the Devil. Six women were ultimately executed. The trials were unique in Finland for their scale and their focus on diabolical witchcraft rather than traditional folk magic. The first woman executed was Karin Persdotter, and the events have since inspired the 2016 Finnish film 'Devil's Bride' (Tulen morsian).

title: The Devil's Dance at Bomarsund (Djävulsdansen)
summary: One of Åland's most famous legends, the Devil's Dance tells of a masked ball at the Russian military settlement of Skarpans near Bomarsund in the 1830s–1840s. A mysterious, elegantly dressed stranger arrived at the dance and asked which woman was the finest dancer. A priest's wife stepped forward. The stranger danced with her so wildly that blood came through her shoes. When the musicians stopped playing, the fiddles continued on their own. Finally, a clergyman named Prosten Sadelin was summoned. He confronted the stranger, who revealed a horse's hoof instead of a foot — the Devil himself. Sadelin drove the Devil out through a small hole in a window casement. The legend has inspired three theatrical plays and an opera performed at Alandica in Mariehamn in 2022.

title: The Skogsrå – Forest Spirit of Åland's Woodlands
summary: The skogsrå (forest spirit) is a female nature spirit found throughout Swedish-speaking regions including Åland. She appears as a beautiful woman from the front, but seen from behind she has a hollow, rotten back and often a cow's tail or one horse foot. She lures men deep into the forests, leading them astray into swamps or wilderness. Men who follow her may lose their health or sanity after repeated visits. However, she is known to be kind to charcoal burners and woodcutters, and can reward hunters with good fortune. In the Christian reinterpretation common to Åland and the Nordic region, the skogsrå and similar rå spirits were said to be angels who fell from heaven with Lucifer — those who landed in forests became skogsrå, those in water became sjörå. The Åland archipelago, with its blend of pine forests and rocky shores, is considered a natural home for these borderland spirits.

title: The Näcken – Water Fiddler of Åland's Lakes and Streams
summary: The Näcken (Näkk in Finnish Swedish) is a male water spirit who sits naked among the reeds or under waterfalls, playing hauntingly beautiful violin music. His playing is irresistibly seductive, luring listeners — especially children and young women — toward the water, where they drown. However, the Näcken can be outwitted by those who know the proper countermeasures: if someone learns to play a tune the Näcken cannot match, or if a priest thwarts him with prayer, the spirit can be driven away. In some versions, the Näcken teaches his art to those who bring him a stolen offering. In the Åland context, with its thousands of lakes, inlets, and streams, the Näcken was a powerful cautionary figure used to keep children away from dangerous waters. The figure is closely related to the Norwegian Fossegrim and the Finnish Näkki.

title: The Sea Draugr – Ghost of Drowned Sailors
summary: In the Nordic maritime tradition that extends to Åland, the draugr or sea draug is the restless spirit of a drowned sailor. It appears as a figure in oilskins with a head of seaweed, sailing in half a boat. The sea draugr serves as an omen of impending drowning: if a fisherman sees the draug or hears its cries, his own vessel is likely to be lost. The draugr could be placated with offerings — in the old Christmas tradition, beer was left out as 'draug-beer' to keep these spirits from becoming malicious. For Åland, where seafaring has been the backbone of life for thousands of years and where entire communities have lost men to the sea, the draugr legend gave voice to collective grief and the ever-present danger of the Baltic. The tradition of leaving offerings at sea cairns on islands like Eckerö and Geta connects to this belief in appeasing the drowned.

title: The Tomtegubbe – Åland's Household Guardian
summary: The tomte (or gårdstomte) is a household spirit who lives on farms and in barns, watching over the family and livestock. On Åland, as in the rest of the Swedish-speaking world, the tomte was both protector and disciplinarian: he rewarded hard work and good management with prosperity, but punished laziness, drunkenness, and night work with mischief or outright harm. The tomte expected to be fed — typically a bowl of porridge with butter on Christmas Eve. If neglected, he would cause accidents, make the cows go dry, or leave the farm entirely. The tradition is preserved in the annual children's anthology 'Bland tomtar och troll' (Among Gnomes and Trolls), first published in 1907 and still going today, which features stories collected from across the Swedish language area including Åland. The tomte represents the moral code of the traditional Åland farming community, where hard work and respect for the unseen were paramount.

title: The Bäckahästen – The Brook Horse of Åland's Waters
summary: The bäckahästen (brook horse) is a shapeshifting water spirit that appears as a beautiful, seemingly tame horse near streams, lakes, or the sea. When someone climbs onto its back, the horse gallops into the water and drowns its rider. The legend was particularly used to warn children away from dangerous waters. In some versions, the bäckahästen can be tricked by throwing a piece of steel or iron over its head, forcing it to revert to its true form. This creature overlaps with the Näcken tradition and is closely related to the Celtic kelpie. The countless inlets, streams, and shallow coastal waters of the Åland archipelago made this a particularly relevant cautionary tale for island children. The creature appears numerous times in the Sagenkartan entries from the Swedish-speaking regions of Finland.

title: The Jomala Runestone – Odin's Mark on Åland
summary: The Jomala runestone, located at Jomala Church — the oldest stone church in Finland — is one of the most significant Viking-age artifacts on Åland. The runestone bears inscriptions invoking Odin's guidance and names voyagers who once crossed the Baltic. Its serpent forms curl around the old Younger Futhark alphabet in the classic runestone style of 11th-century Scandinavia. The stone represents Åland's role as a crossroads between Sweden and Finland during the Viking Age, when the islands served as vital waystations for sea travelers. Local folklore holds that the stone possesses protective powers and that disturbing it would bring ill fortune. Jomala Church itself, built atop earlier pagan sacred ground, represents the layering of Christian and pre-Christian belief that characterizes so much of Åland's folk tradition.

title: The Saltvik Runestone – Serpent of the Norse Sea
summary: The Saltvik runestone is a second significant Viking-age monument on Åland, located in the Saltvik parish north of Mariehamn. Like the Jomala stone, it is carved with runic inscriptions in the Younger Futhark, its serpentine bands curling like waves around the ancient alphabet. The stone marks the connection between Åland's Viking-era settlers and the wider Norse world, commemorating voyages, kinship, and courage. In local folk belief, runestones were not mere memorials but living objects with spiritual power. Some Åland traditions held that runestones could speak at midnight, or that they protected the land from evil spirits. The Saltvik stone, in particular, is associated with the legend of the Devil's Dance — the parish church at Saltvik being the home of the priest's wife who allegedly danced with the Devil at Bomarsund.

title: The Midsummer Bonfires – Ancient Solar Rites on Åland
summary: Every June, when the sun barely sets at this northern latitude, the people of Åland gather for Midsummer's Eve (Midsommar) — one of the most important celebrations of the year. Across the archipelago, bonfires blaze on hilltops and beaches, echoing solar rites far older than Christianity. These fires honor the eternal return of the sun, mark the height of fertility and growth, and ward off malevolent spirits. Couples dance around the midsummer pole (midsommarstång) decorated with birch leaves and wildflowers, while musicians play nyckelharpa, accordion, and fiddle in rhythms passed down through generations. In folk belief, Midsummer night was a time when the boundaries between worlds grew thin: the skogsrå and sjörå were particularly active, and magical plants gathered at midnight held special powers. Young women would pick seven different flowers and place them under their pillow to dream of their future husbands. The tradition remains one of the strongest living folk practices on Åland.

title: The Vittra – The Hidden People of Åland
summary: The vittra (singular: vitter) are an underground-dwelling people who live in a parallel civilization beneath the hills and meadows of Åland. Invisible to most humans, they have their own farms, livestock, and churches. They are known to do favors for humans who treat them with dignity and respect, but can become vengeful if their homes are disturbed — for example, by building a structure on their invisible property. The vittra are closely related to the Swedish 'de underjordiska' (the underground ones) and the Scandinavian 'huldrefolk' (hidden folk). Offerings of food, particularly porridge, were traditionally left for them. In the Sagenkartan records from Åland, many entries describe encounters with vittra who borrowed tools from farmers or requested that specific areas be left undisturbed. The tradition reflects a deep-seated belief that the land is shared with unseen inhabitants who must be respected.

title: The Älvor – Meadow Elves of the Åland Archipelago
summary: The älvor (elves) are beautiful, otherworldly female beings who dance in the meadows and misty fields of Åland, particularly at dawn and dusk. Their dancing leaves behind 'älvringar' (fairy rings) — circles of flattened grass or mushrooms where the ground is forever enchanted. Humans who interrupt their dances may be lured into their realm, losing all sense of time and place. Those who step into an älvring may become invisible to other humans or fall into a sleep from which they cannot wake. In Swedish-speaking Åland tradition, the älvor are skilled in magic and illusions. They are sometimes described as half-transparent spirits, closely linked to the coastal mists that roll in from the Baltic. The figure of the älva is related to the pre-Christian female deities called dís (plural dísir), powerful spirits associated with fate and protection. Even today, the word 'dis' remains a synonym for mist or very light rain in Swedish.

title: King Erik XIV's Imprisonment at Kastelholm
summary: In the autumn of 1571, the deposed Swedish King Erik XIV was imprisoned at Kastelholm Castle by his brother Johan III. Erik had been dethroned in 1568 and was moved between various castles before being held on Åland. Local legend holds that Erik's ghost still wanders the halls of Kastelholm, his footsteps echoing through the stone corridors at night. The imprisonment at Kastelholm is part of a larger body of historical folklore surrounding the castle, which first appears in written records in 1388. The castle has witnessed sieges, royal visits, witch trials, and fires — and each layer of its history has contributed to its reputation as one of the most haunted places in Åland. During the restoration of the castle in the 20th century, workers reported unexplained sounds and cold drafts, which some attribute to the restless spirits of its many historical occupants.

title: The Russian Fortress of Bomarsund – A Cursed Ruin
summary: The unfinished fortress of Bomarsund, built by Russia between 1832 and 1854, is one of Åland's most evocative historical sites. Local folklore holds that the fortress was cursed from the start — built on ground that was never meant to be fortified. Thousands of laborers, prisoners, and soldiers worked on the project, which was to be the largest construction in Åland's history. But in August 1854, during the Crimean War, an Anglo-French fleet destroyed the fortress in just three and a half days. The defeat led to the demilitarization of Åland (which remains in effect today). Folk tradition speaks of strange lights seen among the ruins, ghostly Russian soldiers still marching their posts, and the phantom sounds of construction echoing from the hills. The nearby hilltop Djävulsberget ('Devil's Mountain') takes its name from the folklore surrounding the site. The Bomarsund legend exemplifies how a historical event became woven into the supernatural fabric of Åland's folk tradition.

title: Diné Bahaneʼ: The Four Worlds
summary: The Navajo creation epic describes four worlds through which the Diyin Dineʼé (Holy People) journeyed. The First World (Niʼ Hodiłhił, Black World) was small with a floating island in four seas, inhabited by Air-Spirit People, insect people, and the four rulers of the seas. First Woman (Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́) and First Man (Áłtsé Hastiin) were formed from clouds and wind. The Second World (Niʼ Hodootłʼizh, Blue World) held blue-gray mammals and swallow people. The Third World (Niʼ Hałtsooí, Yellow World) had two crossing rivers, six sacred mountains, and the Holy People including Talking God, Water Sprinkler, House God, and Black God. A great flood forced emergence into the Fourth World (Niʼ Hodisxǫs, White World) where the current world was shaped and monsters (anaye) roamed until the Hero Twins defeated them.
source: Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Diné Bahaneʼ Wikipedia entry

title: The Birth of Changing Woman
summary: In the Navajo tradition, Changing Woman (Asdzą́ą́ Nádleehé) was the first human born in the Fourth World. She was originally born as Yoołgaii Asdzą́ą́ (White Shell Girl) and matured into Changing Woman, a central deity who represents the cycles of life, the seasons, and the earth itself. She later gives birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer (Naayééʼ Neizghání) and Child of the Water (Tóbájíshchíní), who defeat the monsters plaguing the Navajo people. Changing Woman eventually departs to live in the west, where she resides in a floating house made of turquoise and abalone, creating the seasons as she ages and rejuvenates each year.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: First Man and First Woman
summary: In the First World, Black Cloud and White Cloud came together in the East, and from their breath First Man (Áłtsé Hastiin) was formed, carrying white corn and crystal. Blue Cloud and Yellow Cloud came together in the West, forming First Woman (Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́) with yellow corn, white shell, turquoise, and yucca. First Woman made a fire with turquoise and First Man with crystal. Seeing each other's light, they joined together, establishing the pattern of life. Great Coyote (Mąʼiitoʼí Áłchíní) appeared, claiming to know all under water and sky. Then First Angry (Áłtsé Hashké) appeared, claiming seniority and bringing witchcraft into the world. Conflict among the Air-Spirit People led to their expulsion from the First World.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956); Wikipedia

title: Spider Woman and the Origin of Weaving
summary: In the Third World of Navajo tradition, Spider Woman and Spider Man, who lived near the Crossing of the Waters, taught the people the art of weaving. Spider Man showed them how to shape a spindle wheel and spin cotton thread from planted cotton seeds. First Woman declared that spinning must be done toward the body to attract beautiful goods, not away. Spider Man named the spindle 'turning around with beautiful goods' while Spider Woman called it 'turning around with mixed chips.' They constructed a rectangular loom frame, established the warp and weft, and created all parts of the loom. After teaching the people, Spider Man instructed that newborn girls should have spider web rubbed on their hands and arms so they would weave skillfully without tiring.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Separation of Women and Men
summary: In Navajo mythology, First Woman boasted that women could live without men, claiming she thanked her vagina for the deer First Man brought. Enraged, First Man led all men and the two hermaphrodite twins across the river to the north bank. For four years, the sexes lived apart. Women tried to satisfy themselves with bones, feathers, and long stones, while men used animal livers. The people suffered and longed for each other. Owl warned that the separation was wrong and that monsters would be born from it — and indeed, from the women's unnatural acts, monsters were conceived that later terrorized the people. Finally, First Man called across the river, they reconciled, and the people reunited on rafts, resuming their lives together with restored harmony.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: Big Water Creature and the Great Flood
summary: In the Third World, after the reunion of men and women, a mother and her two daughters missed the raft crossing and swam the river. Big Water Creature (Tééhoołtsódii) seized the daughters and dragged them underwater. Talking God and Water Sprinkler created bowls of white and blue shell that spun an opening to an underwater house. First Man and First Woman rescued the girls while Coyote (First Angry) stole Big Water Creature's own two children. In retaliation, Big Water Creature sent a great flood from all directions. The people fled to the sacred mountain Sisnaajiní, where First Man planted the Male Reed. Woodpecker hollowed the reed, and the people climbed into the Fourth World through its passage, just ahead of the rising waters.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: Creation of the Sun and Moon
summary: In the Fourth World of Navajo tradition, First Man, First Woman, and Great Coyote planned the creation of the sun and moon. They summoned Black God (Fire God) from beneath the earth along with Water Sprinkler, House God, and Talking God. Turquoise Boy agreed to become the Sun and White Shell Girl the Moon, but demanded payment: the lives of all beings on earth. First Man heated turquoise on a buckskin with Black God's fire and Turquoise Boy entered it, becoming the Sun (Jóhonaaʼéí, 'The One Who Rules the Day'). White Shell Girl entered white shell and became the Moon (Tłʼéhonaaʼéí, 'The One Who Rules the Night'). They established twelve months with White Shell Girl's whistle of twelve holes. First Angry Coyote, excluded from planning, cursed the arrangement so the moon's months would not match the sun's, bringing irregular seasons and occasional frost and drought.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Coming of Death
summary: In Navajo mythology, death came into being when one of the hermaphrodite twins (Nádleeh) stopped breathing after the first day of the Sun's journey. The people abandoned the body in fear, and the next day it was gone — a man looked down through the reed to the Third World and saw the twin sitting by the river. Coyote First Angry proposed a test: he threw a black rock into Black Water Lake, declaring that if it floated, spirits would return and there would be no death; if it sank, death would be permanent. The rock sank. The people learned that looking at the dead is dangerous when the two witnesses also died four days later. Coyote justified his decision by explaining that without death, the world would overpopulate, elders would remain trapped in infirm bodies, and there would be no room for new children.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Mescalito et al. in Wikipedia

title: The Stars and Constellations
summary: First Man, First Woman, Great Coyote, and the Holy People gathered pieces of rock-star mica to create the night sky in Navajo tradition. First Man drew a plan in the dirt to arrange stars like a woven rug. Black God placed the North Star (Sǫʼtsoh). First Man placed the Big Dipper (Náhookǫs bikąʼí) and First Woman the Little Dipper (Náhookǫs baʼáádí). First Man placed the Pleiades (Dilyéhé), which Black God claimed represented parts of his body. They placed Orion's belt (Coyote's Feather), the Rabbit Tracks, and many others. First Angry Coyote, excluded from the careful arrangement, grew impatient and snapped the blanket, scattering the remaining mica chaotically across the sky — explaining why stars appear scattered rather than neatly ordered.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Re-Creation of the Sacred Mountains
summary: After emerging into the Fourth World, First Man, First Woman, Water Sprinkler, and Black God re-created the six sacred mountains from dirt First Man had brought from the Third World. They placed Sisnaajiní (Blanca Peak) in the East, decorated with white shells, white lightning, white corn, and dark clouds for male rain. They placed Tsoodził (Mount Taylor) in the South, adorned with turquoise, dark mist, animals, and light clouds for female rain. Dookʼoʼoosłííd (San Francisco Peaks) in the West received abalone shell and yellow corn. Dibé Nitsaa (Hesperus Mountain) in the North was covered with black beads and heavy rain. At each mountain they created resident deities — Rock Crystal Boy and Girl at Sisnaajiní, Turquoise Boy and Corn Girl at Tsoodził, and others at each sacred peak.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Hero Twins: Monster Slayer and Child of the Water
summary: In Navajo mythology, Changing Woman gave birth to the Hero Twins, Monster Slayer (Naayééʼ Neizghání) and Child of the Water (Tóbájíshchíní), fathered by the Sun. The twins were born to rid the world of monsters (anaye) that terrorized the people. They journeyed to their father the Sun, passing tests of strength and courage. The Sun tried to kill them with a sweat lodge of stone knives, a heated stone bed, and poisoned smoke, but Spider Woman protected them. They received lightning arrows and armor before returning to earth. Monster Slayer killed the giant Yéʼiitsoh by striking his leg armor and then his head with arrows. He and his brother slew Déélgééd the Horned Monster by shooting arrows into his body and causing him to fall into the ocean. They disposed of other monsters including the giant birds, the monster who kicked people off cliffs, and those who caused hunger, poverty, cold, and old age. Some monsters were allowed to remain in diminished form as a balance for humanity.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Slaying of Yéʼiitsoh
summary: Monster Slayer's greatest feat in Navajo tradition was killing Yéʼiitsoh, the Big Giant, who wore armor of flint knives on his legs. The Hero Twins approached him after their father the Sun had weakened the giant by striking him four times. Monster Slayer shot an arrow into the giant's leg armor but it shattered; a second arrow broke through. The giant collapsed face-down, and Monster Slayer shot a third arrow through his head. Child of the Water also shot the giant's head. When Yéʼiitsoh began to rise again, Spider Woman warned the twins. Monster Slayer shot a fourth arrow which pinned the giant's hair to the ground. As Yéʼiitsoh died, rivers of blood flowed from his wounds. Monster Slayer used the giant's own stone knife to cut off his head and returned home carrying it as a trophy.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Slaying of Déélgééd the Horned Monster
summary: After defeating Yéʼiitsoh, the Hero Twins faced Déélgééd, the Horned Monster, a creatures described as having one horn and living in the water. In some versions the monster was a giant water creature with a single horn. Monster Slayer and Child of the Water tracked it to its lair. Monster Slayer shot the creature multiple times; its blood gushed out and it plunged into the ocean. As it floated on the surface, both twins shot arrows into it until it was dead. The head of Déélgééd floated to the shore where people later used its horn for ceremonies. This victory was one of several that cleared the world of the monsters that had been devouring the people since the time of the separation of men and women.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Story of Noqoilpi the Great Gambler
summary: In Navajo tradition, Noqoilpi was a powerful gambler who challenged and defeated all who came to him, taking their possessions and even their freedom. He was said to live at the base of Chʼóolʼį́ʼí (Great Spruce Mountain or El Capitan). Noqoilpi gambled with entire nations, winning their wealth and enslaving their people. The Holy People decided he had grown too powerful and needed to be stopped. They sent a challenger who gambled with Noqoilpi in a series of contests. The challenger, aided by the Holy People, finally beat Noqoilpi at the moccasin game and other gambling games. Defeated, Noqoilpi was forced to leave the Navajo world. He departed eastward, taking his gambling implements with him, and was never seen again, though his influence lingered in the form of gambling as a human vice.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: The Story of the Moccasin Game
summary: The Moccasin Game is a traditional Navajo story explaining the origin of a sacred winter game. In the mythological account, the animals gathered to play the moccasin game, which involves hiding a stone or yucca ball under one of four moccasins while the opposing side guesses its location. The game was played between the day animals and the night animals, with the stakes being control over light and darkness. Through trickery and strategy — particularly from Coyote who cheated by using a yucca ring instead of a stone — the night animals won, establishing that darkness would have its place in the world. This story is traditionally told only during winter months and is considered sacred, teaching lessons about balance, trickery, and the boundaries between day and night, summer and winter.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956); Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Maiden Who Became a Bear
summary: A Navajo story about a young woman who transformed into a bear after being mistreated by her family or community. The maiden wandered into the wilderness in despair, and as she walked, her hands and feet slowly transformed into bear claws, her body grew fur, and she became a powerful bear. She later encountered hunters from her village, who recognized her by the remnants of her human appearance. The story serves as a cautionary tale about the treatment of others and carries ceremonial significance related to the Mountain Top Chant ceremony. Bear holds special power in Navajo tradition, and this story explains the deep spiritual connection between bears and specific healing ceremonies.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956); Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: The Mountain Top Chant
summary: The Mountain Top Chant (Dzil Naʼoodilii) is a major Navajo ceremony whose origin story tells of a maiden who encountered a bear during her puberty ceremony. A young Navajo woman, while gathering materials for her ceremony, was approached by a bear who taught her the sacred songs, prayers, and sandpaintings of the Mountain Top Chant. The bear spirit took her into the mountains where she learned the entire ceremonial cycle over several days. When she returned to her people, she taught them the ceremony, which is used to invoke the protection and power of the mountain spirits and to cure illnesses caused by animals. The nine-day ceremony features elaborate sandpaintings, songs, and dances that reenact the maiden's journey and transformation.
source: Washington Matthews, The Mountain Chant: A Navajo Ceremony (1887)

title: The White Bead Maiden's Marriage to the Sun
summary: In this Navajo story, White Bead Maiden (Yoołgaii Asdzą́ą́) was sought in marriage by the Sun. The Sun, disguised as a handsome warrior, courted the maiden. After many trials, she agreed to marry him and traveled to his house in the eastern horizon. The Sun's house was a square room of turquoise and white shell with the four directions represented. White Bead Maiden bore the Sun's children, who became the Hero Twins. However, the Sun already had another wife, and tensions arose in their celestial household. White Bead Maiden eventually returned to earth, where she was transformed into Changing Woman, the central female deity of Navajo religion who governs the cycles of life, seasons, and time itself. This story establishes the sacred lineage from the Sun to the Navajo people and the deep connection between celestial and earthly realms.
source: Aileen O'Bryan, The Diné: Origin Myths of the Navajo Indians (1956)

title: Old Age, Cold, Poverty, and Hunger
summary: After the Hero Twins killed the major monsters, four lesser monsters remained: Old Age (Są́), Cold (Dǫǫh), Poverty (Téʼéʼį́), and Hunger (Báhí). When the twins confronted these beings, they pleaded for their lives. Old Age argued that without him, the world would become overcrowded and people would not appreciate youth. Cold said he was necessary to remind people of their mortality. Poverty claimed his existence motivated people to work and share. Hunger argued that without him, people would not appreciate food. The Hero Twins, acknowledging the necessity of balance in the world, allowed these four to remain in reduced form as part of the natural order of human existence, explaining why humans continue to experience aging, cold, poverty, and hunger.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: Departure of Changing Woman
summary: In Navajo tradition, after the monsters were defeated and the world was made safe, Changing Woman decided to leave the earth. She gathered the Holy People and announced her departure to the West. She created a beautiful floating house made of turquoise, abalone shell, white shell, and jet, located on an island in the western ocean. Before leaving, she blessed the Navajo people and gave them the corn, sheep, and knowledge they would need to prosper. She taught them the ceremonies that would protect and heal them. Changing Woman decreed that she would live in the west forever, aging through the winter and rejuvenating each spring — thus establishing the cycle of seasons. She promised to watch over the Navajo people from her western home and to send her blessings whenever the proper ceremonies were performed.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

title: Tawa Creates the Worlds
summary: In the Hopi creation story, in the beginning only Tawa, the Sun Spirit, existed in endless space (Tokpella). Tawa formed the First World but its insect-like creatures lived unhappily in caves. To improve things, Tawa created Sotuknang, his nephew, who in turn created Spider Woman (Spider Grandmother) as a messenger and helper. Spider Woman was sent to lead the first creatures — then resembling insects — to the Second World, where they took on animal forms of wolves and bears. Still dissatisfied, Tawa created a Third World and Spider Woman guided the creatures there, where they finally became human. Spider Woman taught them weaving and pottery, and Hummingbird brought them the fire drill. This cycle of creation, destruction, and renewal through four worlds is central to Hopi cosmology, with humanity now living in the Fourth World.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Barry Pritzker, The Hopi (2011); Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis

title: The Sipapu Emergence
summary: In one version of the Hopi emergence story, the people of the Third World grew wicked and corrupt. With the help of Spider Grandmother or bird spirits, a hollow bamboo reed grew at the opening between the Third World and the Fourth World. This opening, called the sipapu, is traditionally located at the Grand Canyon. Only the people with good hearts — those who showed kindness — were able to make the journey through the sipapu to the Fourth World, the world of the present. In the Fourth World, they encountered Masauwu, the Spirit of Death and Earth God, who wore a hideous but also beautiful mask. Masauwu gave them their land and four sacred tablets outlining their way of life and their future wanderings. He taught them to worship properly and ensured the dead would return safely to the underworld.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis

title: The Great Flood and the Voyage to the Fourth World
summary: In the alternative Hopi emergence story, mainly told at Oraibi, Tawa destroyed the Third World in a great flood because of the people's wickedness. Before the destruction, Spider Grandmother sealed the more righteous people inside hollow reeds, which floated as boats. After the waters receded, the survivors found themselves on a small piece of dry land with nothing but water in every direction. They planted a giant bamboo shoot and climbed it to survey the land, but still saw only water. Spider Woman then instructed them to make boats from more reeds. Using island stepping-stones, the people sailed east until they reached the mountainous coasts of the Fourth World. The Hopi Water Clan's name (Patkinyamu) means 'dwelling-on-water,' preserving the memory of this ocean voyage. Different stories (sipapu vs. flood journey) coexist among different Hopi villages.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis

title: The Hopi Clan Migrations
summary: Upon arriving in the Fourth World, the Hopi divided and began great migrations across the land. Led by various signs and helped by Spider Woman, clans traveled to every direction, sometimes stopping to build towns, then abandoning them to continue their migrations. They left symbols on rocks to show that Hopi had been there. Clans were named after events or signs received during their journeys. Periods of harmonious living alternated with wickedness, contention, and separation — a pattern that began in the First World. The clans traveled to the farthest extremity of the land in every direction; far to the north was the 'Back Door' (possibly the Bering land bridge), but this was closed to the Hopi, though other peoples came through it. Eventually, guided by Masauwu, the Hopi were led to their permanent home in northeastern Arizona, where Masauwu gave them the land in exchange for their stewardship and service.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis; Ekkehart Malotki, Hopi Ruin Legends

title: The Kachinas Bring Agriculture
summary: In Hopi tradition, during a great drought when the people were starving, they heard singing and dancing coming from the San Francisco Peaks (Nuvatukya'ovi). Investigating, they met the Kachinas — ancestral spirits and supernatural beings who inhabit the peaks. The Kachinas returned with the Hopi to their villages and taught them agriculture, including how to plant and care for corn, beans, and squash. They also taught the Hopi their ceremonial dances and songs, which are vital for bringing rain to the arid land. For six months each year (from winter solstice to July), the Kachina spirits leave the San Francisco Peaks and live in the Hopi villages. Men who put on the masks become the Kachinas — the mask is not a costume but a face that transforms the wearer. The Niman (Homegoing) ceremony in late July marks the Kachinas' return to the mountains.
source: Wikipedia: Hopi mythology; Pecina and Pecina, Hopi Kachinas (2013)

title: The Kana-a Kachinas of Sunset Crater
summary: At old Mishongnovi lived a young maiden who refused all suitors. A Kachina youth from the San Francisco Peaks courted her through magical means — traveling by rainbow — and brought her across the desert to Palotsmo (Sunset Crater), home of the Kana-a Kachinas. There she underwent tests in the kiva, surviving four nights in an icy room with the North Wind, and passed. She married the Kachina youth. But a wicked man impersonated the Kachina husband and deceived the maiden. The Kana-a Kachinas at Sunset Crater took pity on the people suffering from the consequences of this deception. They brought sweet corn and food to the Hopi villages, dancing through the streets, asking each family to leave an ear of corn in their empty storage room. When the people awoke the next morning, their storage rooms were miraculously full of corn. The Kana-a Kachinas then entered a small butte near Mishongnovi, which remains their home.
source: Edmund Nequatewa, Truth of a Hopi; Sacred-texts.com

title: The Ahö'li and the Kachinas of Walpi
summary: In Walpi lived an old Ahö'li Kachina with his sister, the Kachin-mana, leading her because of his age and frailty. They planted in their field south of Walpi, depositing prayer offerings at a báho shrine. Meanwhile, the Ââ'ototo, Big-Horn Kachina, and others gathered and descended the mesa, marking shrines between villages. They encountered the Cóoyoko, a dangerous being with protruding eyes, a large knife, and rattles around his body, who killed and devoured children. The Kachinas negotiated safe passage, promising Cóoyoko could destroy only the bad. The Kachinas descended to a beautiful house in the valley where they sang all night, and the next morning rain poured. By noon, the fields were filled with corn, melons, and squashes. Big-Skeleton (Wokómásauwuu), who owns the earth, met them and told them to live on top of the mesa, where Walpi eventually moved.
source: H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905)

title: The Circle Kachina and His Wife Become Stars
summary: In Hopi tradition, a maiden grinding corn was approached by the Póngo (Circle) Kachina, who danced at various kivas around the village. She agreed to marry him and went to the Kachina kiva where many different Kachinas gathered. She prepared their food while they prepared her bridal costume. They had two children, also Circle Kachinas. The Hotóto Kachina seduced the wife away to Shongópavi. The Circle Kachina, following her tracks, took his children to the Kachina house. After four days of flute playing at Kíba, the wife emerged from the water in her bridal costume. The Flute priests declared: 'You shall always follow each other this way.' The two ran westward and are still running — they became the stars that pursue each other across the sky, Nangö'sohu, one constantly overtaking and then falling behind the other.
source: H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905)

title: The Toad and the Snow Kachinas
summary: In Oraibi, the Snow Kachinas were practicing for a dance in the Chórzhovi kiva. North of the village at Katcínavala lived Toad Woman with her son. The boy often went to listen to the Kachinas sing. On the dance day, the Toad Boy painted his face and wore a wildcat robe. Children laughed at him and took him to an anthill because he liked ants. He ate the ants and returned to the plaza to enjoy the dance. When the children gave him red piki (paper bread) from the Kachinas, he brought it home to his mother. She was happy, and they ate it together. This story teaches about the inclusion of even the lowliest creatures in Kachina celebrations and demonstrates the Hopi value of kindness to those who are different.
source: H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905); told by Kwáyeshva of Oraíbi

title: Coyote Helps Decorate the Night
summary: In this Hopi story about the origins of the night sky, the animals were decorating the darkness. Various animals placed stars and patterns in the sky, but Coyote, growing impatient with the slow, careful work, grabbed a handful of stars and threw them carelessly across the sky. This explains why some stars are scattered randomly while others form orderly patterns. Another version tells that Coyote was given the task of placing the stars but became distracted or deliberately did a poor job, resulting in the Milky Way and the chaotic scattering of stars. The story warns against impatience and the consequences of rushing sacred work.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: The Boy Who Crossed the Great Water and Returned
summary: A Hopi tale of a boy who journeyed across the great waters and returned with knowledge and power. The boy's curiosity led him to leave his village and travel east toward the rising sun. He encountered various supernatural beings and overcame trials, including crossing a body of water that no Hopi had crossed before. Guided by Spider Grandmother, he learned sacred songs and ceremonies from the spirits on the other side. When he returned to his people, he brought new ceremonies, dance forms, and agricultural knowledge that benefited the Hopi. The story reflects the Hopi belief that spiritual power and knowledge can be gained through journeys to sacred places and that those who venture forth with pure hearts return to enrich the community.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: Two Friends: Coyote and Bull Snake Exchange Visits
summary: Coyote and Bull Snake were friends who decided to visit each other's homes. Coyote visited Bull Snake first and found his burrow beautiful and comfortable. Bull Snake then visited Coyote's den. During the visit, Coyote played a trick on Bull Snake, or vice versa, depending on the version. The story humorously explores the nature of friendship across differences, the expectations of hospitality, and the consequences of deception between friends. It also incorporates elements of Hopi social values, including proper guest behavior and the responsibilities of hosts. The story ends with the two friends parting ways, each having learned something about trust and respect for different ways of living.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: Why the Salt is Far Away
summary: A Hopi legend explaining why the Hopi must travel long distances to obtain salt. Long ago, salt was close to the Hopi villages. But a woman or a group of people violated a taboo involving salt — either by being wasteful, disrespectful, or by using salt improperly during a ceremony. The Salt Woman (or salt spirits) became angry and moved the salt deposits far away, to the Grand Canyon and other distant locations. Ever since, the Hopi have had to undertake long, dangerous journeys to gather salt, a sacred substance used in ceremonies and daily life. The story teaches respect for natural resources and the consequences of taking blessings for granted.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993); various Hopi oral traditions

title: The Journey to the Land of the Dead
summary: In Hopi tradition, the journey to the land of the dead begins at the sipapu in the Grand Canyon. The spirit travels westward, following the path of the setting sun, to the land of the ancestors. Along the way, the spirit encounters various challenges and must have the proper knowledge, gained through ceremonial instruction during life, to pass safely. The spirit is judged by Masauwu and the Kachinas who determine its fate. Good souls pass into a land of plenty, while those who lived badly face difficulties. The dead eventually become cloud people who can bring rain to their living descendants. The spirits of the dead are associated with the Kachinas and reside at the San Francisco Peaks. This cycle of life, death, and return as rain-giving spirits reinforces the Hopi connection between proper living, ceremonial participation, and the well-being of the community.
source: Harold Courlander, The Fourth World of the Hopis; H.R. Voth, The Traditions of the Hopi (1905)

title: Mockingbird Gives Out the Calls
summary: In this Hopi story, Mockingbird was responsible for distributing the languages and calls among all the peoples of the world. During the migrations after the emergence into the Fourth World, the people were all speaking the same language. Mockingbird called the people together and assigned different languages and dialects to different groups, effectively creating the diversity of tribes and nations. He gave various bird species their distinctive calls as well. According to some versions, Mockingbird received this task from Spider Grandmother or the Kachinas, and his imitative ability was used to create the range of human languages. The story explains why Mockingbird can mimic other birds and why there are so many different languages among the peoples of the earth.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: Sikakokuh and the Hunting Dog
summary: A Hopi story about a boy named Sikakokuh who had a remarkable hunting dog. The dog was no ordinary animal but possessed spiritual power, able to track game without fail and communicate with its master. Sikakokuh's success as a hunter made others jealous. Through the dog's guidance, Sikakokuh learned the proper rituals for hunting, including prayers and offerings to the spirits of the animals he killed. When enemies or jealous rivals tried to harm the dog, supernatural forces protected both boy and animal. The story teaches the sacred relationship between humans and animals, the importance of gratitude, and the proper conduct of hunters who must respect the spirits of the animals they take.
source: Harold Courlander, People of the Short Blue Corn (1993)

title: The Iatiku and Nautsiti Creation (Acoma Pueblo)
summary: In the Acoma Pueblo creation myth, two sisters named Iatiku (Life Bringer) and Nautsiti (Full Basket) emerged from the underworld through a sacred place called Shipapu, guided by Tsichtinako (Thought Woman). They were given two baskets containing seeds, animal figurines, and sacred objects. Tsichtinako instructed them to pray to the Sun (Uchtsiti), their father, who had created the world from a clot of his own blood. The sisters created the mountains, directions, plants, and animals. Iatiku created the Corn Mother and the Kachinas. Nautsiti introduced death into the world when she disobeyed a taboo. The sisters became the mothers of the Acoma people, with Iatiku's children becoming the Pueblo clans. The story explains the origins of ceremonies, clans, and the relationship between the people, the land, and the spirits.
source: Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo (1928); Peter Nabokov edition (2015)

title: The Pueblo Emergence from the Underworld
summary: A central Pueblo creation theme across multiple Pueblo tribes (Acoma, Zuni, Hopi, Laguna, etc.) involves the emergence of the first people from the underworld through a sacred opening called the sipapu. In the beginning, the people lived in darkness beneath the earth. Guided by spirits, culture heroes, or twin war gods, they climbed up through four underground worlds, each representing a stage of development. They used a giant reed, spruce tree, or ladder to ascend. As they climbed, they transformed from insect-like or unfinished beings into fully formed humans. The last people to emerge were often an old man and woman who became trapped, becoming the rulers of the underworld — establishing that all must return there after death. The sipapu is often identified with the Grand Canyon or a specific location within each Pueblo's traditional territory.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo; Wikipedia: Pueblo mythology

title: The Pueblo Hero Twins
summary: In Pueblo mythology, the Twin War Gods (Masewi and Oyoyewi in some traditions) are the most important heroic figures. They were born to a virgin mother who conceived from the Sun, much like the Navajo Hero Twins. These twins are credited with creating the world and all living creatures, as well as slaying a giant monster that terrorized the Pueblo people — a beast so large it could eat entire villages in one bite. The twins tracked the monster and killed it with their magical weapons, which included lightning arrows and shields. They established the directions, brought order to the world, and taught the people how to live. After completing their work, they returned to the underworld but continue to watch over the Pueblo people. They are often celebrated in ritual performances and dances, reinforcing their cultural significance as protectors and culture heroes.

title: The Ants That Pushed on the Pine Tree
summary: A Pueblo story from Isleta about Kahp-too-óo-yoo (Corn-stalk Young Man), a famous hunter, warrior, and wizard who controlled the clouds and brought rain. After being bewitched by a false friend, he was imprisoned in the top of a giant pine tree that reached the sky. His family and village suffered from drought without his rain powers. The Little Black Ant discovered Kahp-too-óo-yoo in the tree and summoned all the armies of the Little Black Ants and Big Red Ants. They climbed the tree, placed a cup of cornmeal and honey at the top, and then — with the Black Ants pushing from above and the Red Ants pulling from below — drove the giant pine tree deep into the earth, freeing Kahp-too-óo-yoo. He returned home, playing his flute, and the rains returned. The story reflects Pueblo values of community, cooperation, and the power of even the smallest creatures to help.
source: Pueblo Indian Folk-Stories (Sacred Texts Archive); sacred-texts.com

title: Tsichtinako Teaches the Sisters
summary: In the Acoma Pueblo creation story, Tsichtinako (Thought Woman) appeared as a voice guiding the sisters Iatiku and Nautsiti from within the earth. She couldn't be seen because she was a spirit without a human body. Tsichtinako instructed the sisters in all aspects of life: how to plant seeds from their baskets, how to make prayer sticks (pahos), how to conduct ceremonies, and how to organize their society into clans. She explained that Uchtsiti (the Sun, their father) had created the world but it was not yet complete, and the sisters were to complete it. Tsichtinako also taught them language, naming each plant, animal, and feature of the landscape. She established the four sacred directions and the relationship between the people and all beings. Tsichtinako continues to guide the Acoma people invisibly, representing the power of thought and the spiritual foundation of existence.
source: Edward Proctor Hunt, The Origin Myth of Acoma Pueblo (1928)

title: The Kachina Spirits of the Pueblo
summary: Across Pueblo cultures, the Kachinas (Katsinam) are supernatural beings who are the spirits of ancestors, natural forces, and all things in the universe — rocks, stars, animals, and plants. They reside in the San Francisco Peaks (north of Flagstaff, Arizona) and come to the pueblos for six months each year during the winter season. The Kachinas bring rain, fertility, and other blessings through ceremonial dances. Each Kachina has a specific identity, mask, songs, and role in the ceremonial calendar: Crow Mother brings rain, the warrior Kachinas police behavior, and the clown Kachinas expose human pretension through comedy. When a man wears the Kachina mask, he becomes the spirit, not merely a representation. The tihu (Kachina dolls) given to children are teaching objects to help them learn to recognize each Kachina. The Kachinas emerged from the underworld with the people and taught them how to live properly.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Wikipedia: Kachina; Susanne and Jake Page publications

title: The Origin of Corn (Pueblo)
summary: In Pueblo mythology, corn was a gift from the Holy People. The origin story varies between communities but commonly involves a culture hero or deity who obtained corn from the underworld or from supernatural beings. In one version, the Corn Maidens (Blue Corn Maiden, Yellow Corn Maiden, etc.) brought corn to the people. In another, a boy discovered corn growing in a hidden place and brought it to his village. The sacred nature of corn is reflected in its inclusion in nearly every Pueblo ceremony. The different colors of corn (white, yellow, blue, red, black, and speckled) each have spiritual significance and are associated with the six directions. The successful growth of corn depends not only on farming skill but on the proper performance of ceremonies and the people's moral conduct. Corn is understood as a sentient being with which humans have a reciprocal relationship — humans care for corn, and corn nourishes humans.
source: Various Pueblo oral traditions; Frank Cushing, Zuni Folk Tales

title: The Jicarilla Apache Creation
summary: In the beginning there was nothing — no earth, no living beings, only darkness, water, and Cyclone the wind. Only the Hactcin (supernatural beings) existed. They made the earth as a woman facing upward and the sky as a man facing downward. Black Hactcin, the most powerful, created the first four-legged animal from clay, then made many animals from its body. He made birds from mud mixed with raindrops. When the animals and birds asked for a companion, Black Hactcin created the first man from turquoise, coral, opal, jet, abalone, and other precious materials brought by the animals. Four times Black Hactcin commanded the man to speak, laugh, and shout, and each time he did. Man dreamed of a woman when lice were placed on his head, and he awoke to find Ancestral Woman beside him. They had children and the people multiplied, living in darkness until the sun and moon were created.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); World History Encyclopedia

title: The Emergence (Jicarilla Apache)
summary: In the Jicarilla Apache emergence story, White God of the East made a mountain that grew toward the sky and caused reeds to stand in the center. Four girls twisted the reeds, stopping their growth. Four ladders were made — black, blue, yellow, and variegated — and placed at the mountain. Whirlwind went to the upper world and found much water. Beaver built dams, Badger followed him, and they reported the land was exposed. The people climbed the ladders, starting with black, then blue, then yellow, and finally variegated. After the last ladder wore out, an old woman and old man remained behind. 'You will come back here to me,' one called after the departing people, establishing that the dead return to the underworld. The people moved east along four parallel trails under four chiefs, and leadership alternated between fortunate and unfortunate. They finally settled near Taos. The two who remained became the rulers of the dead.
source: Pliny Earle Goddard, Jicarilla Apache Texts (1911)

title: Holy Boy Makes the Sun and Moon
summary: In Apache tradition, Holy Boy was unhappy with the darkness in the underworld and decided to create a sun. He tried many materials without success. Cyclone, spying on the Hactcin, told Holy Boy that White Hactcin had the sun. Holy Boy went to White Hactcin who gave him the sun, and to Black Hactcin who gave him the moon. Black Hactcin taught Holy Boy how to make a sacred sandpainting on a buckskin to hold the sun and moon. Holy Boy, Red Boy, Black Hactcin, and White Hactcin held a ceremony with songs, and White Hactcin released the sun while Black Hactcin released the moon. Light grew stronger as the sun moved from north to south, becoming daylight. The medicine people immediately claimed credit for making the sun, arguing with each other. The Hactcin warned them about eclipses. When an eclipse came, the medicine people could not restore the sun — only the Hactcin could, teaching that humans should not claim divine powers.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); nativeamerican-art.com

title: The Apache Origin of the Animals
summary: After the Apaches emerged from the underworld, they traveled south for four days with only seeds to eat. A Raven brought a quiver with meat to a teepee, and when children ate the meat they became instantly fat. The Raven's meat was stolen, and he flew to his eastern mountain home. An Apache boy was transformed into a puppy to spy on the Ravens. Hidden in a young raven's blanket, the puppy watched as an old raven removed a flat stone, revealing an opening to the underworld. Each evening, a buffalo emerged and was eaten. After four days, the boy resumed human shape, descended into the underworld with white and black feathers, and claimed the buffalo. All the animals followed him to the Apache camp. But an old grandmother struck a deer's nose with a fire stick, leaving a white mark and decreeing that animals would avoid humans. Thus the harmony between humans and animals ended, and deer now use their noses to detect humans from afar.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Jicarilla Apache Indians (1938); Margot Edmonds and Ella Clark, Voices of the Winds (2021)

title: The Gahe: Apache Mountain Spirit Dancers
summary: The Gahe (called Gaan by Western Apache) are the Mountain Spirits, powerful supernatural beings who live inside sacred mountains. The first Mountain Spirit ceremony originated when children of the Sun taught the Apache how to call and honor these spirits. The Mountain Spirits appear as masked dancers wearing distinctive tall, wooden-slat headdresses, with black-and-white body paint and black buckskin kilts. The Gahe ceremony is part of the Girls' Puberty Ceremony (Sunrise Ceremony), the longest and richest Apache ritual. Four Mountain Spirits typically dance together — one for each cardinal direction — along with a clown figure. The dancers move in a syncopated, swaying rhythm to singing and drumming, tracing sacred patterns in the dance ground with their feet. The Gahe are believed to possess healing powers and can cure illness, drive away evil influences, and bring blessings. Sierra Blanca (White Mountain) in New Mexico is considered the spiritual origin place of many Mountain Spirit dance groups.
source: Hoijer, Chiricahua Apache Texts (1938); openpublishing.psu.edu dissertation on Gahe/Gaan

title: White Painted Woman and Child of the Water (Chiricahua Apache)
summary: In Chiricahua and Mescalero Apache tradition, the creation begins not with an emergence from the underworld but with a great flood that destroyed a malevolent world. After the flood, White Painted Woman (Changing Woman) appeared. She conceived Child of the Water (also known as Killer-of-Enemies in Lipan tradition) from the rays of the Sun. Child of the Water became the dominant culture hero who rids the world of monsters. He is sometimes described as the Sun himself in some versions, and his younger brother is the Wise One. White Painted Woman is identified with the Moon and Thunder. The Girls' Puberty Ceremony reenacts White Painted Woman's first menstruation, when she received her power from the Sun. The ceremony lasts four days and includes the Mountain Spirit dance, blessing the young woman with health, fertility, and a long life, just as White Painted Woman was blessed.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Myths and Tales of the Chiricahua Apache Indians (1994); nativeamerican-art.com

title: The Lipan Apache Killer-of-Enemies
summary: In Lipan Apache tradition, Killer-of-Enemies is the primary creator and culture hero, identified with the Sun. Killer-of-Enemies seems synonymous with Child-of-the-Water, and he is considered the child of Changing Woman (who is identified as the Moon and Thunder). The Lipan emergence story includes Killer-of-Enemies creating the world and fighting monsters. He has a younger brother called Wise One, who assists him. Killer-of-Enemies journeyed through the world, establishing the proper order of things, teaching the Apache how to hunt, conduct ceremonies, and live properly. He defeated powerful enemies including giant animals and spirits who threatened the people. His battles established the sacred geography of the Lipan Apache world. After completing his work, Killer-of-Enemies departed, promising to return if the people ever truly needed him. The Lipan version shares similarities with the Navajo Hero Twins tradition but emphasizes the role of the single Sun-figure more strongly.
source: Morris Edward Opler, Lipan Apache narratives; nativeamerican-art.com

title: The White Mountain Apache Creation
summary: In the White Mountain Apache creation myth from Noze (recorded in 1910), a maiden went to the top of a high mountain where the rays of the rising Sun first struck. She raised her skirt and the breath of the Sun entered her. She went up the mountain four mornings, and four times the Sun's breath entered her. This unmarried girl became pregnant, and the people gossiped about her. She gave birth to Naiyenezgani (Monster Slayer) and Tobatc'istcini (Child of the Water). The twins grew rapidly and were given weapons by their father the Sun. They killed the monsters threatening the people, including Naiye' who devoured humans. After proving their power by cutting a tree in half with a thrown weapon, they assured the people that no more monsters would trouble them. They established that a messenger would circle the earth in one day to keep them informed. The twins then resumed their roles as protectors of the people and warriors against evil.
source: Pliny Earle Goddard, Myths and Tales from the White Mountain Apache (1911/Project Gutenberg)

title: The Awonawilona Creation (Zuni)
summary: In the Zuni creation myth recorded by Frank Cushing, only Áwonawílona, the All-Father, Maker and Container of All, existed in the beginning. He made himself into the Sun Father (Yatoka). The mist clouds formed into the Great Waters, from which Earth Mother (Áwitelin Tsíta) and Sky Father (Ápoyan Ta'chu) emerged. Together they conceived all men and creatures in the four-fold womb of the world. The Sun Father and Earth Mother brought forth the Twin Children of the Sun — the brothers Ko'wituma and Wats'usi (the Ahayuta twins) — who were given sacred knowledge, caps, bows, arrows, and shields to have dominion over all beings. The twins descended into the first underworld (Ánosin Téhuli) and formed a ladder for people to ascend to the second cave-world (Moss World, K'ólin Téhuli), then to the third (World of Mud, Áwisho Téhuli), and finally the fourth cave-world (Wing World, Tépahaian Téhuli), where the twins taught men the ways of life before they entered the Daylight World (Ték'ohaian Úlahnane).
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); The Mythic World of the Zuni (1988)

title: The Zuni People Emerge into Daylight
summary: In Ruth Benedict's version of the Zuni emergence, people dwelt in total darkness deep in the fourth world. Awonawilona, the Sun, took pity on them. His two sons, the bow priests, located the opening to the fourth world in the southwest and led the people upward. They planted seeds that grew into pine, spruce, silver spruce, and aspen. Each tree became a prayer stick stretched to the next world. The people climbed through four worlds, each time finding themselves still incomplete. They had no mouths, anuses, or fingers; their hands and feet were webbed, and they had horns and tails. In the first world (the one below daylight), they could finally see — they were covered in filth and slime. The bow priests cut mouths into their faces with a knife sharpened on a red whetstone, then cut anuses with a soot-blackened knife. They cut fingers and toes, and finally removed the people's horns and tails. When the people emerged into sunlight at last, they cried from the brightness, and sunflowers grew where their tears fell.

title: The Trial of Lovers: Maiden of Matsaki
summary: A Zuni folk tale about the beautiful Maiden of Matsaki, desired by many suitors. After the war god Áhaiyúta and his brother courted her, they discovered she was cruel — she had let the children of Field-mice die of cold while she gambled. The gods punished her but also showed compassion for the helpless mouse children, taking them to their grandmother who raised them. The story teaches that even gods can be cruel, but those who find helpless children must care for them. The Maiden of Matsaki's story illustrates Zuni values about compassion, the consequences of cruelty, and the duty to care for orphans and vulnerable children. It also demonstrates that even divine beings are subject to moral judgment and that mercy can follow justice.

title: The Youth and His Eagle
summary: A Zuni story about a young boy who raised an eagle and developed a deep spiritual bond with the bird. The eagle became his protector and provider, bringing him gifts from the sky world. However, jealous members of the village conspired against the boy and his eagle. The eagle, being a sacred creature connected to the Sun Father, possessed supernatural powers and could communicate with the spirit world. Through his relationship with the eagle, the boy learned important spiritual lessons about power, responsibility, and the consequences of greed. The eagle eventually returned to the sky world, but it left the boy with sacred knowledge that benefited his people. The story emphasizes the Zuni belief in the spiritual power of birds and the importance of maintaining proper relationships with animal spirits.

title: The Poor Turkey Girl
summary: A Zuni Cinderella-like story about a poor girl who was mistreated by her community and found companionship among the wild turkeys. The turkeys, taking pity on her, transformed her appearance with beautiful clothing and feathers so she could attend a sacred dance at the village. She danced beautifully and won the heart of a young man, but had to flee before dawn to return the turkeys' gifts. She lost one of her turkey-feather garments, which the young man used to find her. However, when the turkeys discovered she had not properly thanked them or kept her promises, they abandoned her and took back their gifts. The girl was left with nothing, learning that ingratitude and broken promises have consequences. The story teaches Zuni values of humility, gratitude, and keeping one's word.

title: How the Summer Birds Came
summary: A Zuni myth explaining the origin of small summer birds. In the days of the ancients, there were no little birds — only large birds like eagles, crows, and hawks. A Zuni man discovered a magical bundle of feathers left by the spirits. When he walked into a field of flowers, the bundle began to flutter, and small wings flew out from it, becoming the Sacred Birds of Summerland. The feathers were made living by the lives of the birds that had once borne them, and they came to life again in the environment of flowers and summer that they had loved. This is why, according to Zuni tradition, there are jaybirds, sparrows, finches, willow-birds, and all the beautiful little birds that herald summer and hover over flowers. The story concludes with the storyteller noting that Zunis have books too — not with marks but with words in their hearts from the ancients.

title: The Serpent of the Sea (Kolowisi)
summary: A Zuni legend about Kolowisi, the great horned water serpent that lives in the sacred lake. A maiden from Zuni went to the lake and was seen by Kolowisi, who took her as his bride beneath the waters. She lived with the serpent in his underwater home, where there was no light but she was treated well and given abundance. The people mourned her loss and held ceremonies to bring her back. Eventually, Kolowisi allowed her to return to the surface, but she was transformed and carried spiritual power from her time in the serpent's realm. She brought knowledge of healing ceremonies and water-songs back to the Zuni people. Kolowisi is generally a benevolent figure, associated with water, fertility, and the life-giving forces of the natural world. The serpent's horn symbolizes power, and offerings are made to Kolowisi during ceremonies asking for rain.
source: Frank Cushing, Zuñi Folk Tales (1901); various Zuni oral traditions

title: The Maiden of the Yellow Rocks
summary: A Zuni story about the beautiful daughter of the high priest of the Village of the Yellow Rocks. Despite her beauty, her pride and coldness drove away suitors. The twin war gods Áhaiyúta and Mátsailéma, living with their grandmother on Thunder Mountain, decided to court her. They appeared as ugly pygmies — boys who never grew to manhood. Meanwhile, the Field-mice, who were prolific and needed wool for their families, gambled with the gods. The mice lost everything, including the wool for their nests, and their babies died of cold. The war gods, despite their terrible nature, took the orphaned mouse babies to their grandmother, who raised them. The story teaches the Zuni value of caring for helpless children — if even the cruelest gods showed mercy, surely humans must care for abandoned infants and orphans.

title: The Foster-Child of the Deer
summary: A Zuni story about a child abandoned in the wilderness who was adopted and raised by deer. The deer taught the child to live in harmony with nature, to understand the language of animals, and to know the sacred plants of the forest. When the child grew up and encountered human hunters, a conflict arose between the human and deer worlds. The deer-mother bid farewell to her foster-child, who was torn between two worlds — the human world of their birth and the deer world of their raising. The story teaches respect for deer and the proper relationship between hunters and prey. It is connected to Zuni hunting rituals and the belief that deer willingly give themselves to respectful hunters who perform the correct ceremonies and prayers. The foster-child became a mediator between humans and deer, teaching people the rituals that honor the spirits of slain animals.

title: The Boy Hunter Who Never Sacrificed to the Deer
summary: A Zuni story about a boy who was an excellent hunter but never performed the proper sacrifices or prayers to the spirits of the deer he killed. As a result, the deer spirits became angry. The boy's disrespectful hunting caused supernatural consequences — his kills became tainted, and he began to have bad luck. The spirits of the deer he had killed without proper ceremony confronted him. To make amends, the boy had to learn the proper rituals of the hunt, including making prayer sticks, offering cornmeal, and speaking words of gratitude to the deer's spirit. This story explains the origin of the Society of Rattlesnakes, which has the power to heal snakebites and conduct ceremonies that maintain balance between humans and animals. The story reinforces the Zuni teaching that all life is sacred and must be taken with gratitude and proper ceremony.

title: Átahsaia the Cannibal Demon
summary: A Zuni legend about Átahsaia, a giant cannibal demon who terrorized the people. Átahsaia is described as a huge, monstrous being with burning eyes and an insatiable hunger for human flesh, especially children.
